topics.title: Introduction to NLP: NLP Tasks and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT601 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to NLP: NLP Tasks and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Natural Language Processing (NLP)

**Natural Language Processing (NLP)** is a sub-field of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Computational Linguistics concerned with the interaction between computers and human (natural) languages. It involves designing algorithms, models, and systems that enable machines to **read, understand, generate, and respond to** text and speech in a way that is both meaningful and contextually appropriate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *NLP is the discipline that combines computer science, artificial intelligence, and linguistics to develop systems capable of processing and analyzing large amounts of natural language data, performing tasks such as translation, summarization, sentiment detection, question answering, and conversational interaction.*

Mathematically, NLP can be framed as the task of learning a function:

$$
f : L \rightarrow R
$$

where $L$ is the space of natural language inputs (sentences, documents, audio) and $R$ is the desired structured representation (tags, classes, translations, summaries).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Polyglot Robot"

Imagine you are trying to teach a foreign tourist to understand Malayalam, Hindi, or English. You must teach them:
1. The **alphabet and words** (vocabulary),
2. The **grammar rules** (how to form sentences),
3. The **meaning** of words and sentences (semantics),
4. The **intent** behind what is said (pragmatics), and
5. The **context** of a conversation (discourse).

NLP is essentially the engineering of this "polyglot robot." The machine is the tourist, and natural language is the foreign country it must navigate. Just as a human tourist struggles with idioms, slang, and ambiguity, an NLP system struggles with the same phenomena — but at industrial scale and speed.

## 1.3 Why is NLP Hard? — The Ambiguity Problem

Human language is **inherently ambiguous**. A single sentence can have multiple valid interpretations depending on context, tone, and world knowledge.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A child learns language in 4–5 years with exposure to roughly 10 million words. An NLP system must derive similar competence from data, which is one of the central engineering challenges of the field.

### Types of Ambiguity in NLP

| Ambiguity Type | Description | Example Sentence |
|---|---|---|
| **Lexical** | A word has multiple meanings | "He went to the **bank**." (river bank vs. money bank) |
| **Syntactic** | A sentence has multiple parse trees | "I saw the man **with the telescope**." |
| **Semantic** | Meaning is unclear | "The chicken is ready to eat." |
| **Pragmatic** | Meaning depends on speaker intent | "Can you pass the salt?" (request, not a yes/no question) |
| **Discourse** | Meaning depends on prior context | "John told Mike **he** had won." (Who won?) |

## 1.4 The KTU 2024 Snapshot — Scope of the Module

According to the **PCCMT601 syllabus (2024 Scheme)**, Module 1 is foundational and covers:
- What NLP is, its origins, and modern relevance.
- The full spectrum of **NLP tasks** (low-level to high-level).
- The **applications** of NLP in industry, governance, healthcare, and education.
- Challenges that motivate the rest of the course.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Levels of Language Analysis (The NLP Pyramid)

NLP systems typically process language across a hierarchy of levels, from raw signals to high-level meaning:

1. **Phonology / Acoustic Level** — Deals with the sound system of speech (relevant for speech recognition).
2. **Morphological Level** — Analyzes the structure of words and smallest meaningful units (*morphemes*). Example: "unhappiness" → *un* + *happy* + *ness*.
3. **Lexical Level** — Identifies and classifies individual words (tokenization, lemmatization).
4. **Syntactic Level** — Analyzes sentence structure using grammar rules (parsing).
5. **Semantic Level** — Extracts literal meaning (word sense disambiguation, semantic role labeling).
6. **Discourse Level** — Connects sentences to form coherent meaning.
7. **Pragmatic Level** — Interprets intent and situational context.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Early NLP systems were *symbolic* (rule-based). Modern systems are largely *statistical* (probabilistic) and *neural* (deep learning). A solid answer to "evolution of NLP" should mention all three paradigms.

## 2.2 Classification of NLP Tasks

NLP tasks are broadly classified into two categories:

### A. Lower-Level Tasks (Foundational, Text → Structure)
- **Tokenization** — Splitting text into words/sub-words.
- **Sentence Segmentation** — Splitting paragraphs into sentences.
- **Stop-word Removal** — Filtering common words (*the, is, a*).
- **Stemming & Lemmatization** — Reducing words to root form (*running → run*).
- **Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging** — Labeling words as nouns, verbs, adjectives, etc.
- **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** — Identifying names of people, places, organizations.
- **Morphological Segmentation** — Splitting words into morphemes.
- **Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)** — Selecting the correct meaning of a polysemous word.

### B. Higher-Level Tasks (Structure → Meaning / Action)
- **Parsing (Syntactic & Dependency)** — Building parse trees.
- **Sentiment Analysis** — Classifying opinion as positive/negative/neutral.
- **Machine Translation (MT)** — Translating text from one language to another.
- **Text Summarization** — Producing a concise version of a document.
- **Question Answering (QA)** — Returning answers to natural language questions.
- **Text Generation** — Producing coherent natural language output.
- **Dialogue Systems / Chatbots** — Engaging in multi-turn conversation.
- **Coreference Resolution** — Linking pronouns to their referents (*"He" → "Rahul"*).
- **Topic Modeling** — Discovering abstract topics in a document collection.
- **Speech Recognition & Text-to-Speech (TTS)** — Converting between audio and text.

## 2.3 The Ambiguity Principle (Quantified)

The **Noisy Channel Model**, foundational to statistical NLP, captures ambiguity formally:

$$
P(\text{output} \mid \text{input}) = \frac{P(\text{input} \mid \text{output}) \cdot P(\text{output})}{P(\text{input})}
$$

The system must choose the most probable output:

$$
\hat{\text{output}} = \arg\max_{\text{output}} P(\text{output} \mid \text{input})
$$

This **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** decision rule is the mathematical backbone of translation, speech recognition, and POS tagging.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this table for last-minute revision before university exams. The vertical pipe symbol has been replaced with $\vert$ or $\mid$ to keep the markdown table intact.

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| Bayes' Rule (NLP form) | $P(H \mid D) = \dfrac{P(D \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(D)}$ | Probabilistic inference in disambiguation, tagging, MT |
| MAP Decision | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y} P(y \mid x)$ | Choose the most likely label, translation, or parse |
| Chain Rule (Joint Probability) | $P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1, \dots, w_{i-1})$ | Foundation of language models |
| N-gram Probability (Markov Assumption) | $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-(n-1)}, \dots, w_{i-1})$ | Simplifies language modeling |
| Perplexity (PP) | $PP(W) = P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_N)^{-1/N}$ | Measures how "surprised" a model is by a sentence |
| F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Standard metric for NER, POS, classification |
| BLEU Score | $\log \text{BLEU} = \min\!\left(1, \dfrac{r}{c}\right) + \sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n$ | Evaluates machine translation quality |
| ROUGE (Recall-Oriented) | $\text{ROUGE-N} = \dfrac{\sum \text{matching n-grams}}{\sum \text{reference n-grams}}$ | Evaluates summarization quality |
| Word Frequency (Zipf's Law) | $f(w) \propto \dfrac{1}{\text{rank}(w)}$ | A few words dominate text corpora |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "Explain the noisy channel model," draw the diagram (Source → Channel → Receiver) and write the Bayes' Rule equation. Examiners in KTU 2024 Scheme reward this exact combination for **7-mark questions**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | NLP Application | Engineering Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Healthcare** | Clinical NLP, ICD coding, EHR mining | Faster diagnosis, reduced paperwork |
| **Finance** | Sentiment analysis of news, fraud detection in claims | Algorithmic trading, risk modeling |
| **Legal** | Contract analysis, e-discovery, judgment summarization | Massive time savings for law firms |
| **Education** | Auto-grading, plagiarism detection, intelligent tutoring | Scalable personalized learning |
| **E-Governance (Kerala)** | Grievance chatbots (e.g., *Kerala Police Cyberdome bot*), multilingual citizen portals | Accessibility of public services |
| **Customer Service** | Virtual assistants, ticket classification | 24/7 automated support |
| **Search & IR** | Semantic search, query understanding | Better information retrieval |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Joint Probability to the N-Gram Model

This derivation shows how a seemingly intractable problem — modeling the probability of an entire sentence — is reduced to a tractable form using the **Chain Rule** and the **Markov Assumption**.

### Step 1: Define the Joint Probability of a Sentence

For a sentence $W = w_1, w_2, w_3, \dots, w_n$, the joint probability is:

$$
P(W) = P(w_1, w_2, w_3, \dots, w_n)
$$

This is the probability that the words occur in this exact order in natural language.

### Step 2: Apply the Chain Rule of Probability

Using the chain rule, we can decompose the joint probability sequentially:

$$
P(w_1, w_2, w_3, \dots, w_n) = P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1) \cdot P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2) \cdots P(w_n \mid w_1, \dots, w_{n-1})
$$

Generalized form:

$$
P(W) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{i-1})
$$

**Logical interpretation:** Each word's probability depends on **all** preceding words. This is linguistically accurate but statistically impractical.

### Step 3: Recognize the Computational Problem

To compute $P(w_n \mid w_1, \dots, w_{n-1})$, we would need to count every possible history of length $n-1$ in the training corpus. For a vocabulary of size $V = 50{,}000$ and $n = 10$, the number of possible histories is $V^{n-1}$, an astronomically large number. This violates the **sparse data problem** — most histories will never be observed in any finite corpus.

### Step 4: Apply the Markov Assumption

The Markov Assumption of order $k$ states that the probability of a word depends **only on the previous $k$ words**, not the entire history:

$$
P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-k}, \dots, w_{i-1})
$$

This dramatically reduces the number of parameters.

### Step 5: Specialize to the Bigram Model ($k=1$)

For $k = 1$, the assumption becomes:

$$
P(w_i \mid w_1, \dots, w_{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})
$$

The joint probability then simplifies to:

$$
P(W) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})
$$

### Step 6: Estimate Bigram Probabilities from Corpus

Bigram probabilities are estimated using **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)**:

$$
P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{\text{count}(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{\text{count}(w_{i-1})}
$$

The numerator is the number of times the bigram $(w_{i-1}, w_i)$ appears in the training corpus; the denominator is the number of times $w_{i-1}$ appears.

### Step 7: Generalize to N-Gram

The full generalization yields the **n-gram language model**:

$$
P(W) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{i-(n-1)}, \dots, w_{i-1})
$$

with the n-gram probability estimated as:

$$
P(w_i \mid w_{i-(n-1)}^{i-1}) = \frac{\text{count}(w_{i-(n-1)}, \dots, w_i)}{\text{count}(w_{i-(n-1)}, \dots, w_{i-1})}
$$

This completes the derivation.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Bigram Probability

**Corpus (toy):**
- S1: "<s> I love NLP </s>"
- S2: "<s> I love AI </s>"
- S3: "<s> I learn NLP </s>"

**Goal:** Compute $P(\text{NLP} \mid \text{love})$ using MLE.

### Step 1: Count occurrences of "love"
Occurrences of the word "love" in the corpus:
- S1: "I love NLP"
- S2: "I love AI"

Total count of "love" = 2.

### Step 2: Count occurrences of the bigram "love NLP"
The bigram "love NLP" appears only in S1, so count = 1.

### Step 3: Apply the MLE formula

$$
P(\text{NLP} \mid \text{love}) = \frac{\text{count}(\text{love}, \text{NLP})}{\text{count}(\text{love})} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5
$$

**Interpretation:** Given the previous word is "love", there is a **50%** probability the next word is "NLP" in this corpus.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: NLP Tasks vs. Technique vs. Application

This matrix is a KTU-favorite for **comparison / table-based 7-mark questions**.

| NLP Task | Input | Output | Classical Technique | Modern Technique | Real-World Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tokenization** | Raw text | List of tokens | Rule-based, regex | Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) | Pre-processing for all NLP systems |
| **POS Tagging** | Token sequence | Tags (NN, VB, JJ…) | HMM, CRF | BiLSTM, BERT | Grammar checkers, search engines |
| **NER** | Text | Tagged entities | Rule-based, MEMM | BERT, spaCy | News indexing, resume parsing |
| **Sentiment Analysis** | Review text | Polarity (+/–/neutral) | Naive Bayes, SVM | LSTM, Transformers | Brand monitoring, product reviews |
| **Machine Translation** | Source sentence | Target sentence | Statistical MT, IBM Models | Neural MT (Transformer) | Google Translate, Indic languages |
| **Summarization** | Document | Short summary | LexRank, TextRank | BART, T5, GPT | News apps, research tools |
| **Question Answering** | Question + context | Answer span | Information Retrieval | BERT, RAG, LLMs | Search engines, chatbots |
| **Speech Recognition** | Audio waveform | Text | HMM + GMM | Whisper, DeepSpeech | Voice assistants, dictation |
| **Chatbots** | User query | Reply | AIML, retrieval-based | GPT, Rasa, Dialogflow | Customer support, virtual agents |
| **Topic Modeling** | Document collection | Topic clusters | LDA, LSA | BERTopic | News categorization, research |
| **Coreference Resolution** | Document | Clustered mentions | Rule-based, mention-pair | Neural Coref (Lee et al.) | Summarization, QA |
| **WSD** | Word in context | Correct sense | Lesk, knowledge-based | BERT, XLNet | Search, translation, sentiment |

## 3.4 Perplexity — Quantifying Model Quality (Derivation)

Perplexity is the **standard evaluation metric** for language models. It is defined as the reciprocal of the geometric mean of the sentence probability.

**Starting Point:** Joint probability of a test sentence $W = w_1, w_2, \dots, w_N$:

$$
P(W) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_1, \dots, w_{i-1})
$$

**Step 1:** Take the $N$-th root of the joint probability:

$$
P(W)^{1/N} = \left( \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid \text{context}_i) \right)^{1/N}
$$

**Step 2:** Invert to define perplexity:

$$
PP(W) = P(W)^{-1/N} = \left( \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{P(w_i \mid \text{context}_i)} \right)^{1/N}
$$

**Step 3:** Equivalently, in logarithmic form (base 2 typically used):

$$
PP(W) = 2^{-\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2 P(w_i \mid \text{context}_i)}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation — Lower perplexity = better language model. If $PP(W) = k$, the model is as "confused" as if it had to choose uniformly among $k$ possibilities at each step.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always write the **interpretation** in addition to the formula. Examiners award 2 of the 7 marks for stating that lower perplexity = better model. A common student error is to write the formula but skip the interpretation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Classical NLP Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The diagram below maps the end-to-end flow of a typical NLP system — from raw input to actionable output. This is a **high-frequency diagram** in KTU 2024 scheme answer sheets, especially for Module 1 questions worth 7 or 14 marks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Text Input] --> B[Text Acquisition]
    B --> C[Text Preprocessing]
    C --> D[Tokenization]
    D --> E[Stopword Removal and Stemming]
    E --> F[POS Tagging and NER]
    F --> G[Parsing Syntactic and Dependency]
    G --> H[Semantic Analysis and WSD]
    H --> I[Discourse and Pragmatic Analysis]
    I --> J[Feature Representation Bag of Words TF IDF Embeddings]
    J --> K[Model Layer Classical ML Neural Transformer]
    K --> L[Application Output Translation Sentiment Summary QA Chatbot]
    L --> M[Post Processing and Evaluation F1 BLEU ROUGE]

    subgraph FOUNDATION_LAYER
        C
        D
        E
    end

    subgraph LINGUISTIC_LAYER
        F
        G
        H
        I
    end

    subgraph INTELLIGENCE_LAYER
        J
        K
        L
        M
    end

    style A fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style M fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**How to read this diagram for exams:**
- The **Foundation Layer** handles pre-linguistic cleanup.
- The **Linguistic Layer** performs grammatical and semantic analysis.
- The **Intelligence Layer** transforms linguistic structure into task-specific outputs.
- Arrows indicate a strict left-to-right flow (each stage depends on the previous).

## 4.2 Taxonomy of NLP Applications

The following block diagram categorizes NLP applications by domain and end-user purpose.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NLP[Natural Language Processing] --> A1[Conversational AI]
    NLP --> A2[Information Retrieval]
    NLP --> A3[Content Generation]
    NLP --> A4[Language Understanding]
    NLP --> A5[Speech Interfaces]

    A1 --> A1a[Chatbots]
    A1 --> A1b[Virtual Assistants]
    A1 --> A1c[Customer Support Bots]

    A2 --> A2a[Search Engines]
    A2 --> A2b[Document Search]
    A2 --> A2c[Question Answering]

    A3 --> A3a[Text Summarization]
    A3 --> A3b[Machine Translation]
    A3 --> A3c[Creative Writing AI]

    A4 --> A4a[Sentiment Analysis]
    A4 --> A4b[Named Entity Recognition]
    A4 --> A4c[Topic Modeling]

    A5 --> A5a[Speech Recognition ASR]
    A5 --> A5b[Text to Speech TTS]
    A5 --> A5c[Voice Biometrics]

    style NLP fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style A1 fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style A2 fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style A3 fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style A4 fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style A5 fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 The Ambiguity Resolution Flow

The diagram below shows how a generic NLP system resolves ambiguity at each linguistic level — a favorite KTU illustration.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Input Sentence] --> L1[Lexical Level]
    L1 --> Q1{Word Has Multiple Senses?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| L1a[Apply WSD Algorithm Lesk or BERT]
    Q1 -->|No| L1b[Accept Default Sense]
    L1a --> L2[Syntactic Level]
    L1b --> L2
    L2 --> Q2{Valid Parse Tree Exists?}
    Q2 -->|Multiple| L2a[Probabilistic Context Free Grammar PCFG Rank Parses]
    Q2 -->|Single| L2b[Accept Parse]
    L2a --> L3[Semantic Level]
    L2b --> L3
    L3 --> Q3{Meaning Consistent with World Knowledge?}
    Q3 -->|No| L3a[Apply Selectional Restrictions and Pragmatics]
    Q3 -->|Yes| L3b[Accept Interpretation]
    L3a --> L4[Pragmatic and Discourse Level]
    L3b --> L4
    L4 --> Out[Final Resolved Meaning]

    style Start fill:#17becf,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Out fill:#bcbd22,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Use-Case:** Use **Figure 4.1** for "Explain the NLP pipeline" type questions. Use **Figure 4.2** for "Applications of NLP" type questions. Use **Figure 4.3** for "Why is NLP hard?" or "Discuss ambiguity in NLP" type questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** Define Natural Language Processing. List any four major NLP tasks.

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Natural Language Processing (NLP) is a sub-field of Artificial Intelligence and Linguistics that deals with the computational processing and understanding of human language. It enables machines to read, decipher, interpret, and generate human language in a valuable and meaningful way.
>
> **Four NLP Tasks (1 mark — ¼ mark each):**
> 1. **Tokenization** — Splitting text into individual words/tokens.
> 2. **Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging** — Labeling words with grammatical categories.
> 3. **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** — Identifying proper nouns like persons, places, organizations.
> 4. **Sentiment Analysis** — Determining the emotional polarity of text.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]** What is ambiguity in NLP? Briefly explain lexical and syntactic ambiguity with examples.

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Ambiguity (1 mark):** Ambiguity in NLP refers to the property of natural language where a single expression (word, phrase, or sentence) can have multiple valid interpretations, making automated understanding challenging.
>
> **Lexical Ambiguity (1 mark):** Arises when a single word has multiple meanings. Example: *Bank* (river bank vs. financial institution).
>
> **Syntactic Ambiguity (1 mark):** Arises when a sentence can be parsed in more than one grammatical structure. Example: *"I saw the man with the telescope."* — the telescope may be held by *I* or by *the man*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern, Module 1 carries either a 14-mark full question OR a 7-mark sub-part. Below, both alternative choices are provided, each with sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each, with full model solutions.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]**

**Q.A. (a) Explain the different levels of linguistic analysis in NLP. Discuss in detail the ambiguity that arises at each level. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Levels of Linguistic Analysis (3.5 marks):**

1. **Phonological / Acoustic Level:** Deals with the sound patterns of speech. Important for speech-to-text systems.
2. **Morphological Level:** Analyzes the smallest meaningful units (morphemes). Example: *unhappiness* = *un* + *happy* + *ness*.
3. **Lexical Level:** Identifies and analyzes the structure of words and their lexicon entries.
4. **Syntactic Level:** Examines the grammatical structure of sentences using parsing techniques.
5. **Semantic Level:** Extracts the literal meaning of sentences, handling word sense disambiguation.
6. **Discourse Level:** Connects sentences to derive meaning across a paragraph or document.
7. **Pragmatic Level:** Uses world knowledge and speaker intent to interpret meaning beyond the literal.

**Ambiguity at Each Level (3.5 marks):**

| Level | Ambiguity Type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Morphological | Morphological ambiguity | *unlockable* = *"can be unlocked"* vs. *"cannot be locked"* |
| Lexical | Polysemy, homonymy | *Bank* (river vs. financial) |
| Syntactic | Multiple parse trees | *"I saw the man with the telescope"* |
| Semantic | Multiple meanings of a sentence | *"The chicken is ready to eat"* |
| Discourse | Anaphora, coreference | *"John told Mike he had won"* |
| Pragmatic | Speech acts | *"Can you pass the salt?"* (a request) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students **list the levels** but fail to **pair each level with a concrete ambiguity example**. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this omission.
> - Do not confuse **pragmatic** with **semantic** ambiguity. Pragmatics involves **intent**; semantics involves **literal meaning**.
> - Avoid vague statements like *"there is ambiguity in language"* — always **name the ambiguity type** and **quote an example**.

---

**Q.A. (b) Describe the major applications of NLP in at least six different domains. Explain how NLP transforms each domain. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

NLP has revolutionized multiple industries by automating language-driven tasks. Six key domains:

1. **Healthcare (1.2 marks):** NLP extracts structured information from unstructured clinical notes, Electronic Health Records (EHRs), and research papers. It enables automated ICD coding, clinical decision support, and early diagnosis. Example: IBM Watson Health mines medical literature.

2. **Finance (1.2 marks):** NLP analyzes earnings call transcripts, news headlines, and social media to predict market sentiment. Robo-advisors and fraud detection systems use NLP to flag suspicious claims.

3. **Legal (1.2 marks):** Contract review, e-discovery, and judgment summarization are automated using NLP, reducing lawyer hours by up to 80% in routine tasks.

4. **Customer Service (1.2 marks):** Chatbots (e.g., HDFC Eva, Kerala Police cyber-bot) handle millions of queries using intent recognition and dialogue management.

5. **Education (1.2 marks):** Auto-grading systems (e.g., Gradescope), plagiarism detection (Turnitin), and intelligent tutoring systems use NLP to evaluate and personalize learning.

6. **E-Governance & Accessibility (1 mark):** Multilingual chatbots and voice-based public service interfaces make governance accessible to non-English speakers. India's *Bhashini* mission exemplifies this.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B):**
> - Do not write **generic paragraphs** like *"NLP is used everywhere"*. Examiners expect **named applications** and **real-world tools/companies**.
> - Avoid exceeding the **six-domain** mark distribution. The 7th domain, if added, must be brief or it eats into allotted marks.
> - For full marks, **italicize or bold** the tool/company name (e.g., **Bhashini**, **Watson**, **Turnitin**). Examiners in 2024 Scheme reward specificity.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B. (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the typical NLP pipeline. Discuss each stage in detail. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The NLP pipeline processes raw text through a sequence of analytical stages. Each stage's output feeds the next.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
The block diagram should be drawn as in **Figure 4.1** (Section 4) of these notes. The expected flow is:

*Raw Text → Text Acquisition → Preprocessing → Tokenization → Stopword Removal & Stemming → POS Tagging & NER → Parsing → Semantic & Discourse Analysis → Feature Representation → Model → Application Output → Evaluation.*

**Stage-by-Stage Description (5 marks):**

| Stage | Function |
|---|---|
| **Text Acquisition** | Collecting text from sources (web, PDFs, social media). |
| **Preprocessing** | Cleaning noise (HTML tags, special characters). |
| **Tokenization** | Splitting text into words, subwords, or sentences. |
| **Stopword Removal & Stemming** | Filtering common words; reducing words to root form. |
| **POS Tagging & NER** | Assigning grammatical tags and entity labels. |
| **Parsing** | Building syntactic parse trees. |
| **Semantic Analysis** | Resolving word senses and sentence meaning. |
| **Discourse Analysis** | Linking sentences for coherent interpretation. |
| **Feature Representation** | Converting text to vectors (BoW, TF-IDF, embeddings). |
| **Model Layer** | Applying ML, DL, or Transformer models. |
| **Application Output** | Producing the final task-specific output. |
| **Evaluation** | Measuring quality with F1, BLEU, ROUGE, etc. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Do not draw the pipeline as a simple 3-box diagram. Examiners expect **at least 6–8 distinct stages**. Vague diagrams lose **1.5–2 marks**.

---

**Q.B. (b) What is a Language Model? Derive the n-gram language model from first principles using the chain rule and the Markov assumption. How is perplexity used to evaluate such a model? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Language Model (1.5 marks):**
A **language model** is a probabilistic model that assigns a probability to a sequence of words, capturing the likelihood that the sequence occurs in a given language. Formally, it computes $P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n)$.

**Derivation (3.5 marks):**
Already shown in detail in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Key steps:

1. Start with the joint probability: $P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n)$.
2. Apply the **chain rule**: $P(W) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1, \dots, w_{i-1})$.
3. Apply the **Markov assumption of order $k$**: $P(w_i \mid w_1, \dots, w_{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-k}, \dots, w_{i-1})$.
4. For a **bigram model** ($k=1$): $P(W) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})$.
5. Estimate with **MLE**: $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{\text{count}(w_{i-1})}$.
6. Generalize to **n-gram**: $P(W) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{i-(n-1)}, \dots, w_{i-1})$.

**Perplexity (2 marks):**
Perplexity is the standard evaluation metric:

$$
PP(W) = P(W)^{-1/N} = \left( \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{P(w_i \mid \text{context}_i)} \right)^{1/N}
$$

Lower perplexity indicates a better model. If $PP(W) = k$, the model is as uncertain as choosing uniformly among $k$ words at each step. Trigram models on large corpora typically achieve perplexity in the range 50–100.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:**
> - Writing the chain rule equation **without** the Markov assumption will not get full marks. The **Markov assumption is the heart of the derivation** — losing it means losing 1.5 marks.
> - Students often **forget to interpret perplexity**. Always end with *"Lower perplexity = better language model."*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 1: Introduction to NLP)**

- [x] **NLP Definition:** AI + Linguistics + Computer Science → machines that process human language.
- [x] **Three Paradigms of NLP:** Rule-based (Symbolic) → Statistical (Probabilistic) → Neural (Deep Learning).
- [x] **Seven Levels of Analysis:** Phonology, Morphology, Lexical, Syntactic, Semantic, Discourse, Pragmatic.
- [x] **Five Ambiguity Types:** Lexical, Syntactic, Semantic, Pragmatic, Discourse — must know **examples** for each.
- [x] **Lower-Level Tasks:** Tokenization, Stemming, Lemmatization, POS Tagging, NER, WSD.
- [x] **Higher-Level Tasks:** MT, Summarization, QA, Sentiment Analysis, Chatbots, Topic Modeling, Coreference.
- [x] **Bayes' Rule (MAP):** $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y} P(y \mid x)$ — used in tagging, WSD, MT.
- [x] **Chain Rule + Markov Assumption** → n-gram language model.
- [x] **MLE for n-grams:** $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{\text{count}(w_{i-1})}$.
- [x] **Perplexity:** $PP(W) = P(W)^{-1/N}$. **Lower = better.**
- [x] **Evaluation Metrics:** F1 (classification), BLEU (translation), ROUGE (summarization).
- [x] **Real-World Apps:** Healthcare, Finance, Legal, Customer Service, Education, E-Governance.
- [x] **Kerala-Specific Reference:** *Bhashini* mission, *Kerala Police cyber-bot*, Indic language MT.
- [x] **Exam Pattern:** Part A = 3 marks (definition + listing); Part B = 14 marks (sub-parts of 7 marks each).
- [x] **Mandatory Diagrams:** NLP pipeline (Fig 4.1), Applications taxonomy (Fig 4.2), Ambiguity resolution (Fig 4.3).
- [x] **Examiner Pet Peeves:** No vague statements, no missing Markov assumption, no unlabeled diagrams, no "NLP is used everywhere" answers.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When answering a 14-mark question, allocate **3 minutes** for the diagram, **2 minutes** for the equation/formula, and the rest for explanatory text. Time management is a critical differentiator in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Language Building Blocks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT601 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Language Building Blocks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are "Language Building Blocks"?

In **Natural Language Processing (NLP)**, **Language Building Blocks** refer to the fundamental hierarchical layers of linguistic analysis that a computational system must deconstruct in order to understand or generate human language. The natural language pipeline is modeled as a stack of formal levels, each operating on the output of the layer below it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Language is a structured system of communication consisting of sounds, words, sentences, and meaning. The building blocks are: **Phonology → Morphology → Lexicon → Syntax → Semantics → Pragmatics → Discourse**.*

### 1.2 The Seven Linguistic Levels (Intuitive Analogy)

Think of human language as a **multi-storey building** 🏢. Each floor depends on the one below it:

| Floor | Level | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Ground** | **Phonetics/Phonology** | The bricks themselves (raw sounds, like individual drum beats) |
| **1st** | **Morphology** | Words formed by joining bricks (e.g., "un-happy-ness") |
| **2nd** | **Lexicon** | The dictionary of meaningful word-stems |
| **3rd** | **Syntax** | The grammar rules — how to arrange words into a legal sentence |
| **4th** | **Semantics** | The literal meaning ("The cat sat on the mat") |
| **5th** | **Pragmatics** | What the speaker actually means ("Can you pass the salt?" = please pass it) |
| **6th** | **Discourse** | How sentences connect across a paragraph or conversation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in NLP:** Most classical NLP systems (parsers, translators, chatbots) were built by attacking these levels in a **bottom-up pipeline** (e.g., NLTK's `sent_tokenize` → `word_tokenize` → `pos_tag` → `parse`). Modern transformers (BERT, GPT) still implicitly learn these layers, but in a distributed, neural fashion.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Terminology

* **Phone**: An acoustic realization of a sound (continuous, physical).
* **Phoneme**: A minimal unit of sound that *distinguishes meaning* in a language. English has roughly **44 phonemes**.
* **Morpheme**: The smallest unit of language that carries meaning. English word *unbelievable* contains **3 morphemes**: *un-, believe, -able*.
* **Lemma**: The canonical/dictionary form of a word (e.g., *run* for *ran, running, runs*).
* **Token**: An instance of a word/symbol in running text.
* **Type**: A unique vocabulary entry.
* **Corpus (pl. Corpora)**: A structured collection of texts used for statistical NLP.
* **Treebank**: An annotated corpus with syntactic parse trees (e.g., the **Penn Treebank**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layer 1 — Phonetics & Phonology

* **Phonetics** = the *physical* study of speech sounds (articulators, acoustics, perception).
* **Phonology** = the *abstract* study of how sounds function as a system in a particular language (rules, contrasts).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IPA (International Phonetic Alphabet):** Every human language sound can be transcribed using a unique IPA symbol. KTU students are *not* expected to memorize IPA, but they **must** know that IPA is the standard representational scheme for phonology.

**Key Phonological Concepts for KTU:**

* **Minimal Pair**: Two words differing in **one phoneme** that change meaning.
   * Example: *bat* /bæt/ vs *pat* /pæt/ — only /b/ vs /p/ differ.
* **Allophone**: Different physical realizations of the **same phoneme** in different contexts (e.g., aspirated [pʰ] in *pin* vs. unaspirated [p] in *spin*).
* **Phonotactics**: The set of allowed sound sequences in a language. English allows *str-* at the start of a word (*strong, street*); Arabic allows *cvcc* patterns.

### 2.2 Layer 2 — Morphology

Morphology studies the **internal structure of words**.

**Two Sub-Processes:**

1. **Inflection** — grammatical variation *without changing word class*.
   * *cat → cats*, *walk → walked → walking*
2. **Derivation** — creates a *new word, often in a different class*.
   * *happy (adj) → happiness (noun)*, *modern (adj) → modernize (verb)*

**Morpheme Classification:**

| Category | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Free morpheme** | Can stand alone | *cat, run, book* |
| **Bound morpheme** | Cannot stand alone | *-ing, un-, -ly* |
| **Root** | Core meaning-bearing unit | *believe* in *unbelievable* |
| **Affix** | Attached to root | *un-, -able* |
| **Prefix** | Before the root | *un-, re-, pre-* |
| **Suffix** | After the root | *-ing, -ness, -ed* |
| **Infix** | Inserted inside root | Tagalog *-um-* in *bili → b-um-ili* |
| **Circumfix** | Wraps around root | German *ge-…-t* in *geliebt* |

**Morphological Parsing — Worked Notation:**

$$\text{unbelieveable} = \underbrace{\text{un-}}_{\text{prefix (negation)}} + \underbrace{\text{believe}}_{\text{root (verb)}} + \underbrace{\text{-able}}_{\text{suffix (adjective)}} + \underbrace{\text{-ity}}_{\text{suffix (noun)}}$$

### 2.3 Layer 3 — Lexicon

The **lexicon** is a language's mental/inventoried dictionary — every word's *lemma, part of speech, morphological features, and idiomatic usage*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A computational lexicon entry contains: (1) **lemma** form, (2) **part-of-speech (POS)**, (3) **morphological features** (tense, number, case), (4) **sense IDs** for polysemous words. WordNet (Princeton) is the most famous example, with **117,000 synsets**.

### 2.4 Layer 4 — Syntax

Syntax is the study of **sentence structure** — how words combine into legal phrases/sentences. Two dominant formal frameworks in NLP:

**A. Constituency Grammar (Phrase-Structure / CFG)**

* Treats language as a hierarchy of **nested phrases** (NP, VP, PP, S).
* Uses **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)** rules of the form:

$$A \rightarrow \alpha$$

* Generates parse **trees** (not graphs).

**B. Dependency Grammar**

* Treats syntax as a network of **binary head-dependent** relations (arrows).
* Words are nodes; arrows point from *head* → *dependent*.
* Example arrow labels: *nsubj, dobj, amod, prep, pcomp*.

**Comparison for KTU:**

| Feature | Constituency (CFG) | Dependency |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Tree of phrases | Graph of word-to-word links |
| **Non-terminals** | Yes (NP, VP) | No, only words |
| **Free word-order langs.** | Weak | Strong (Czech, Hindi) |
| **Toolkits** | NLTK, Stanford PCFG | spaCy, Universal Dependencies |
| **KTU focus** | High | High |

### 2.5 Layer 5 — Semantics

Semantics is the study of **literal, context-independent meaning**. It decomposes into:

* **Lexical Semantics** — meaning of individual words (sense, synonymy, antonymy, hypernymy).
* **Compositional Semantics** — meaning of phrases built using the **Principle of Compositionality**:

$$\text{Meaning}(A \cdot B) = \text{F}(\text{Meaning}(A), \text{Meaning}(B))$$

> *"The meaning of a whole is a function of the meanings of its parts and the way they are syntactically combined."* — Frege, then formalized by Montague.

**Key Lexical Relations:**

| Relation | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Synonymy** | Same/near meaning | *big / large* |
| **Antonymy** | Opposite meaning | *hot / cold* |
| **Hyponymy** | Sub-class | *dog ⊂ animal* |
| **Meronymy** | Part-of | *wheel ⊂ car* |
| **Polysemy** | One form, multiple related senses | *bank* (river, money) |
| **Homonymy** | One form, multiple unrelated senses | *bat* (animal, club) |

### 2.6 Layer 6 — Pragmatics

Pragmatics handles **meaning in context** — what the speaker *implies* versus what they literally *say*.

**Key Sub-topics:**

* **Speech Acts** (Austin/Searle): *assertives, directives, commissives, expressives, declaratives*.
* **Deixis**: Words whose meaning depends on context (*here, there, this, that, you, I, tomorrow*).
* **Presupposition**: What a sentence assumes to be true. *"John stopped smoking"* presupposes *John used to smoke*.
* **Implicature** (Grice): What is *conveyed* but not *said*. Example: *"Some students passed"* ⇒ not *all*.
* **Reference Resolution**: Linking pronouns/expressions to real-world entities (*she, it, the dog*).

### 2.7 Layer 7 — Discourse

Discourse is **multi-sentence analysis** — how sentences form coherent text.

* **Anaphora Resolution**: *"John picked up a book. **He** opened it."* — *He → John, it → book*.
* **Discourse Coherence Relations**: *cause, contrast, elaboration, sequence* (Rhetorical Structure Theory, RST).
* **Topic/Focus**: What a sentence is *about* vs. what is *new* information.

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Construct | Formal Notation | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Phoneme Set** | $\mathcal{P} = \{p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n\}$ | Inventory of contrastive sounds |
| 2 | **IPA Word Transcription** | $/\text{phoneme string}/$ | Phonological representation |
| 3 | **Morpheme Decomposition** | $w = m_1 + m_2 + \dots + m_k$ | Word parsing |
| 4 | **Context-Free Rule** | $A \rightarrow \beta_1 \beta_2 \dots \beta_n$ | Syntactic generation |
| 5 | **CFG Tuple** | $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ | Full grammar definition |
| 6 | **Dependency Arc** | $h \xrightarrow{\text{rel}} d$ | Head → dependent link |
| 7 | **Compositionality** | $[[A \cdot B]] = f([[A]], [[B]])$ | Meaning combination |
| 8 | **Type-Token Ratio** | $\text{TTR} = \frac{\text{unique types}}{\text{total tokens}}$ | Lexical richness metric |
| 9 | **Speech Act Categories** | $\{A,B,C,D,E\}$ | Pragmatic classification |
| 10 | **Discourse Relation** | $R \in \{\text{cause, contrast, elab, seq}\}$ | Coherence analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Every commercial NLP system (Google Search, Alexa, GPT-4, Indic-Bhashini) implicitly implements all 7 levels. The difference is only *how* — symbolic rules (older systems) vs. learned neural representations (modern systems).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Morphological Parsing of a Complex Word

**Problem:** Show the full morphological decomposition of the word **"unhappinesses"** using standard NLP morphological rules.

**Step 1 — Identify the surface form and target lemma.**

We want to map:

$$w = \text{unhappinesses} \longrightarrow \text{lemma: happy}$$

**Step 2 — Surface-level token-by-token scanning.**

* Segment 1: *un-* — recognized as the **derivational negative prefix**, attaches to adjectives (*happy → unhappy*).
* Segment 2: *happy* — the **root** (adjective, free morpheme).
* Segment 3: *-ness* — derivational suffix, converts adjective → noun (*happy → happiness*).
* Segment 4: *-es* — inflectional plural suffix on the derived noun (*happiness → happinesses*).

**Step 3 — Apply formal morphological rule cascade.**

$$\text{unhappinesses} \;\;\xrightarrow{\text{Strip -es (Inflection)}}\;\; \text{unhappiness} \;\;\xrightarrow{\text{Strip -ness (Derivation)}}\;\; \text{unhappy} \;\;\xrightarrow{\text{Strip un- (Derivation)}}\;\; \text{happy}$$

**Step 4 — Express the structure tree.**

$$\text{unhappinesses} \;=\; \underbrace{[\text{un-}]}_{\text{PFX: neg}} \;\cdot\; \underbrace{[\text{happi}]}_{\text{ROOT: adj}} \;\cdot\; \underbrace{[\text{-ness}]}_{\text{SFX: N-former}} \;\cdot\; \underbrace{[\text{-es}]}_{\text{SFX: PL}$$

**Step 5 — Tabulate the transformation for the answer sheet.**

| Step | Operation | Resulting Form | Part of Speech |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Start | unhappinesses | Noun (pl.) |
| 1 | Strip plural *-es* | unhappiness | Noun (sg.) |
| 2 | Strip *-ness* | unhappy | Adjective |
| 3 | Strip *un-* | happy | Adjective (root) |

**Result:** Lemma = **happy**, three derivational/inflectional affixes identified.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Constituency Parse for a KTU-Style Sentence

**Sentence:** *"The curious student read a long book."*

**Step 1 — Lexicon (pre-terminals).**

$$\text{The/Det} \quad \text{curious/Adj} \quad \text{student/N} \quad \text{read/V} \quad \text{a/Det} \quad \text{long/Adj} \quad \text{book/N}$$

**Step 2 — Define the CFG.**

$$G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$$

Where:
* $V = \{S, NP, VP, PP, Det, Nominal, Adj, N, V, Aux\}$
* $\Sigma = \{\text{the, curious, student, read, a, long, book}\}$
* $S$ = start symbol

**Step 3 — Write the production rules (this is what the student must derive).**

$$S \rightarrow NP \; VP$$
$$NP \rightarrow Det \; Nominal$$
$$Nominal \rightarrow Adj \; N \;\;\vert\;\; N$$
$$VP \rightarrow V \; NP$$
$$Det \rightarrow \text{the} \;\;\vert\;\; a$$
$$Adj \rightarrow \text{curious} \;\;\vert\;\; \text{long}$$
$$N \rightarrow \text{student} \;\;\vert\;\; \text{book}$$
$$V \rightarrow \text{read}$$

**Step 4 — Build the parse tree top-down.**

$$S \rightarrow NP \; VP \rightarrow (\text{Det Nominal}) \; (V \; NP)$$
$$\rightarrow ((\text{the}) (Adj \; N)) ((\text{read}) (\text{Det Nominal}))$$
$$\rightarrow ((\text{the}) (\text{curious} \; \text{student})) ((\text{read}) (\text{a} \; (\text{long} \; \text{book})))$$

**Step 5 — Verify that every leaf is a terminal.**

All leaves match $\Sigma$. The derivation is **valid**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Phonology vs. Morphology vs. Syntax vs. Semantics

A classic KTU 2024 long-answer compares two layers. Use this ready-made matrix in your answer:

| Dimension | Phonology | Morphology | Syntax | Semantics |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Unit** | Phoneme | Morpheme | Phrase/Sentence | Proposition |
| **Combinatorial rule** | Phonotactics | Affixation | CFG rules | Composition |
| **Well-formedness** | Native-like | Single word grammatical | Sentence grammatical | Sentence meaningful |
| **KTU example** | /str-/ allowed in *strong* | *un+believe+able* = 3 morphs | NP → Det N | *colorless green ideas sleep furiously* (Chomsky) |
| **Failure** | *bnik* in English | *unununhappiness* | *the student read* | *Colorless green ideas sleep* (Chomsky) |
| **Tool** | Finite-state transducer | Finite-state morphology | CKY / Earley parser | $\lambda$-calculus, First-Order Logic |

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Type-Token Ratio for a Mini-Corpus

**Mini-corpus:** *"the cat sat on the mat the cat sat"*

**Step 1 — Tokenize.**

$$\text{Tokens} = [the, cat, sat, on, the, mat, the, cat, sat]$$
$$N_{\text{token}} = 9$$

**Step 2 — Count unique types.**

$$\text{Types} = \{the, cat, sat, on, mat\}$$
$$N_{\text{type}} = 5$$

**Step 3 — Apply the TTR formula.**

$$\text{TTR} = \frac{N_{\text{type}}}{N_{\text{token}}} = \frac{5}{9} \approx 0.556$$

**Interpretation:** About **55.6 %** lexical richness in this 9-word passage. TTR is *highly sensitive to corpus size*, so KTU questions often ask for its **qualitative** meaning, not a precise number.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Speech Acts (Searle's 5-Fold Classification)

A KTU 2024 favourite for Part-A 3-mark questions:

| Speech Act | Function | Verbs | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Assertives** | State what is true | *claim, report, assert* | *"It is raining."* |
| **Directives** | Get listener to do something | *request, command, ask* | *"Close the door."* |
| **Commissives** | Commit speaker to future act | *promise, vow, offer* | *"I will help you."* |
| **Expressives** | Express psychological state | *thank, apologize, congratulate* | *"I'm sorry."* |
| **Declaratives** | Change reality by saying it | *declare, pronounce, sentence* | *"I now pronounce you married."* |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Classical NLP Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This is the **single most important diagram** for KTU 2024 Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Speech or Text Input] --> B[Acoustic or Character Signal]
    B --> C[Phonological or Orthographic Analysis]
    C --> D[Morphological Analysis: Tokenize and Stem]
    D --> E[Lexical Lookup: POS Tag and Lemma]
    E --> F[Syntactic Parsing: CFG or Dependency]
    F --> G[Semantic Interpretation: Compose Meaning]
    G --> H[Pragmatic and Discourse Integration]
    H --> I[Final Meaning Representation for Downstream Task]
```

**How to read this in an exam:** The arrow direction is strictly **bottom-up**; no layer can be skipped without losing meaning. Modern neural systems (BERT, GPT) replace each box with a *joint learned module*, but the logical function is identical.

### 4.2 Morphological Decomposition Tree of *unhappinesses*

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W[unhappinesses] --> P[un- PFX negation]
    W --> R[happi ROOT adjective]
    W --> S1[ness SFX noun-former]
    W --> S2[es SFX plural]
    P -.affixes.-> R
    S1 -.affixes.-> R
    S2 -.affixes.-> S1
```

### 4.3 Constituency Parse Tree of *"The curious student read a long book"*

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[S Sentence] --> NP1[NP Subject]
    S --> VP[VP Predicate]
    NP1 --> DET1[Det the]
    NP1 --> NOM1[Nominal]
    NOM1 --> ADJ1[Adj curious]
    NOM1 --> N1[N student]
    VP --> V[V read]
    VP --> NP2[NP Object]
    NP2 --> DET2[Det a]
    NP2 --> NOM2[Nominal]
    NOM2 --> ADJ2[Adj long]
    NOM2 --> N2[N book]
```

### 4.4 Dependency Parse of the Same Sentence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[student] -- nsubj --> R[read]
    R -- dobj --> B[book]
    S1 -- det --> T1[the]
    S1 -- amod --> C[curious]
    B -- det --> T2[a]
    B -- amod --> L[long]
```

### 4.5 Ambiguity Resolution Layer Cake

A classic KTU question: *"Show how the word BANK is ambiguous."*

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Word: bank] --> P1[Phonology: single phoneme set]
    Q --> M1[Morphology: simple noun]
    Q --> S1[Syntax: NP in both readings]
    Q --> SE1[Semantics: sense 1 river bank]
    Q --> SE2[Semantics: sense 2 money bank]
    SE1 --> PR1[Pragmatic: depends on context: river scene]
    SE2 --> PR2[Pragmatic: depends on context: finance scene]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Takeaway:** Phonology, morphology, and syntax alone *cannot* disambiguate *bank*. Only **semantic + pragmatic** context can. This is why modern word embeddings (Word2Vec, BERT) bake in contextual sense automatically.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*Define a **morpheme**. Differentiate between a **free morpheme** and a **bound morpheme** with two examples each.*

**Model Answer:**

A **morpheme** is the smallest indivisible unit of language that carries a meaning or grammatical function.

* **Free morpheme** — can stand alone as an independent word. Examples: *cat, run, book, happy*.
* **Bound morpheme** — cannot stand alone; must be attached to a free morpheme. Examples: *-ing, un-, -ness, -ed*.

> **Key point for 3 marks:** Bound morphemes can be further divided into *affixes* (prefix/suffix) and *clitics*. Always give two examples to be safe.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**
*List any **five** levels of language analysis in NLP and write one example for each.*

**Model Answer (tabular format accepted):**

| # | Level | Unit | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Phonology | Phoneme | /b/ vs /p/ in *bat* vs *pat* |
| 2 | Morphology | Morpheme | *un + believe + able* |
| 3 | Lexicon | Lemma | WordNet synset for *dog* |
| 4 | Syntax | Phrase | *NP → Det Adj N* |
| 5 | Semantics | Proposition | *"Snow is white"* = TRUE |

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
*(a)* Explain the various levels of linguistic analysis in NLP with a neat block diagram. Discuss the importance of **phonology** and **morphology** in detail. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* Define a **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)**. Write a CFG for the following sentences and show the parse tree for **"the tall boy read a book"**. **(7 marks)**
i. The boy reads a book.
ii. The tall girl kicked the red ball.

**Model Solution (a):**

* **Levels (7 layers):** Phonetics, Phonology, Morphology, Lexicon, Syntax, Semantics, Pragmatics, Discourse — explain each in 1–2 lines (refer to Section 1.2).
* **Block diagram:** reproduce the pipeline in Section 4.1.
* **Phonology** — study of sound system; mention **phoneme, allophone, minimal pair, phonotactics** with examples (*bat vs pat, strong, three*).
* **Morphology** — study of word formation; mention **morpheme, free/bound, root, affix, inflection, derivation, compounding** with examples (*unhappiness, dogs, faster*).

**Model Solution (b):**

**CFG Definition (1 mark):**
$$G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$$
where $V$ = non-terminals, $\Sigma$ = terminals, $R$ = production rules, $S$ = start symbol.

**Productions (2 marks):**

$$S \rightarrow NP \; VP$$
$$NP \rightarrow Det \; N \;\;\vert\;\; Det \; Adj \; N$$
$$VP \rightarrow V \; NP$$
$$Det \rightarrow \text{the} \;\;\vert\;\; a$$
$$Adj \rightarrow \text{tall} \;\;\vert\;\; red$$
$$N \rightarrow \text{boy} \;\;\vert\;\; \text{book} \;\;\vert\;\; \text{girl} \;\;\vert\;\; \text{ball}$$
$$V \rightarrow \text{read} \;\;\vert\;\; \text{reads} \;\;\vert\;\; \text{kicked}$$

**Parse tree for *"the tall boy read a book"* (4 marks):**

$$S \rightarrow NP \; VP \rightarrow (\text{Det } Adj \; N) \; (V \; NP)$$
$$\rightarrow ((\text{the})(\text{tall})(\text{boy})) \; ((\text{read})(\text{the})(\text{book}))$$

Draw the full tree (see Section 4.3 template). Mark all 7 nodes and 7 leaves.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]**
*(a)* Differentiate between **Constituency Grammar** and **Dependency Grammar** with neat dependency and constituency trees for the sentence *"She bought a red dress"*. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* What is **pragmatics**? Explain **speech acts** and **implicature** with examples. Briefly mention how pragmatics differs from **semantics**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

* **Constituency Grammar (3 marks):** Tree of nested phrases. Rules: $S \rightarrow NP \; VP$, $NP \rightarrow Det \; Adj \; N$, etc. Draw tree similar to Section 4.3.
* **Dependency Grammar (3 marks):** Directed arrows *head → dependent*. Draw graph:

  $$\text{she} \xrightarrow{\text{nsubj}} \text{bought} \xleftarrow{\text{dobj}} \text{dress}, \quad \text{dress} \xleftarrow{\text{det}} a, \quad \text{dress} \xleftarrow{\text{amod}} \text{red}$$
* **Comparison (1 mark):** Constituency has non-terminals; dependency is a network; dependency handles free word-order languages better.

**Model Solution (b):**

* **Pragmatics** (1 mark) — the study of meaning *in context*, beyond literal content.
* **Speech Acts** (3 marks) — Searle's 5 categories: assertives, directives, commissives, expressives, declaratives. Examples (use the table from Section 3.5).
* **Implicature** (2 marks) — Grice's theory: what is *conveyed* but not *said*. Example: *"Some students passed"* implicates *not all*.
* **Pragmatics vs. Semantics** (1 mark) — Semantics = literal; Pragmatics = intended, contextual.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for *Language Building Blocks*:**
> 1. **Confusing "phoneme" with "phone"** — A *phone* is physical/acoustic; a *phoneme* is abstract/contrastive. Students routinely swap them and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up inflection vs. derivation** — Inflection does *not* change POS; derivation often does. Examiners look for this distinction explicitly.
> 3. **Drawing parse trees as graphs** — In CFG, parse trees must be **hierarchical trees**, *not* networks. Dependency parses are networks; CFG parses are trees. Do not mix.
> 4. **Forgetting to label rule components** — When writing a CFG, you must state $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ and identify *all four* components.
> 5. **Giving only "meaning" as a semantic answer** — KTU expects **lexical relations** (synonymy, antonymy, hyponymy, polysemy) and the **Principle of Compositionality** to be mentioned.
> 6. **Confusing pragmatics and discourse** — Pragmatics = *single utterance in context*; Discourse = *multi-sentence coherence*. Examiners will deduct if you interchange them.
> 7. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark sub-parts** — A "neat block diagram" is a *mandatory* demand in KTU. Skip it and lose up to 2 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

* [x] Language is a **7-level hierarchy**: Phonetics → Phonology → Morphology → Lexicon → Syntax → Semantics → Pragmatics → Discourse.
* [x] **Phoneme** ≠ *phone*; remember **IPA, minimal pair, allophone, phonotactics**.
* [x] **Morpheme** types: **Free** (independent) vs **Bound** (affixes, clitics).
* [x] **Inflection** = grammar only; **Derivation** = new word, often new POS.
* [x] **Lexicon** entries contain *lemma + POS + features + sense IDs*; *WordNet* is the canonical example.
* [x] **CFG** = $(V, \Sigma, R, S)$; rules of form $A \rightarrow \alpha$.
* [x] **Constituency** → tree of phrases; **Dependency** → graph of head-dependent arcs.
* [x] **Principle of Compositionality** (Frege-Montague): meaning of whole = function of parts.
* [x] **Lexical relations**: synonymy, antonymy, hyponymy, meronymy, polysemy, homonymy.
* [x] **Pragmatics** = Searle's 5 **speech acts**; Grice's **implicature**; deixis; presupposition.
* [x] **Discourse** = anaphora resolution, coherence relations, topic/focus.
* [x] Always draw a **block diagram** for full-mark answers; label every CFG node; state $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ before listing rules.
* [x] **TTR** (Type-Token Ratio) is the standard lexical-richness metric in KTU problems.
* [x] Modern LLMs (BERT/GPT) **implicitly** learn all 7 layers as *distributed representations*, replacing symbolic rules.
* [x] Indic-specific NLP (Indic-Bhashini, Malayalam tokenizer) follows the *same* 7-layer model, but with Dravidian phonotactics and agglutinative morphology.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Challenges of NLP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCMT601 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Challenges of NLP # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** is a subfield of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Computational Linguistics concerned with the interaction between computers and human (natural) languages. The **Challenges of NLP** refer to the inherent difficulties, ambiguities, and complexities that arise when designing computational systems to understand, interpret, generate, and manipulate natural language data (text and speech). > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim):** Challenges of NLP encompass the structural, semantic, pragmatic, and contextual obstacles that prevent machines from achieving human-level language understanding, including ambiguity, diversity, context-dependence, and the need for common-sense reasoning. The challenges are broadly classified into: 1. **Linguistic Challenges** (Ambiguity at multiple levels) 2. **Computational Challenges** (Data sparsity, model complexity) 3. **Semantic & Pragmatic Challenges** (Meaning, intent, world knowledge) 4. **Resource Challenges** (Low-resource languages, domain adaptation) 5. **Evaluation Challenges** (Lack of gold standards, metrics) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine teaching a **foreign toddler** (the computer) your native language. The toddler can hear sounds but doesn't know: > - Whether "**bank**" means a *river bank* or a *money bank* (lexical ambiguity) > - Whether "**He saw the man with the telescope**" means HE used the telescope or THE MAN had the telescope (syntactic ambiguity) > - That "**It's raining cats and dogs**" doesn't mean pets are falling from the sky (pragmatic / figurative language) > - That "**Amazon**" can be a *river*, a *company*, or a *rainforest* depending on context (semantic ambiguity) > > Every one of these is a **Challenge of NLP**. A computer sees text as a string of characters — it has no eyes, ears, or lived experience to disambiguate meaning. ## 1.3 Core Categories of NLP Challenges — At a Glance | S.No. | Challenge Category | KTU-Critical Example | |:-----:|:-------------------|:---------------------| | 1 | **Lexical Ambiguity** | "Bank" → financial institution / river side | | 2 | **Syntactic Ambiguity** | "I saw the man with the telescope" | | 3 | **Semantic Ambiguity** | "He is at the bank" (which bank?) | | 4 | **Pragmatic Ambiguity** | "Can you pass the salt?" (request, not question) | | 5 | **Discourse Ambiguity** | Pronoun reference across sentences | | 6 | **Language Diversity** | 7000+ languages, multiple scripts | | 7 | **Low-Resource Problem** | Languages with < 1 GB of digital text | | 8 | **Domain Specificity** | Medical/legal jargon vs. casual chat | | 9 | **Common-Sense Reasoning** | "The trophy didn't fit in the suitcase **because** it was too big" | | 10 | **Sarcasm & Irony Detection** | "Great, another Monday!" | | 11 | **Data Sparsity & Bias** | Gender/racial bias in training corpora | | 12 | **Evaluation Difficulty** | No universal metric (human judgment required) | ## 1.4 Visual Schematic of Ambiguity Levels The **Zwicky Hierarchy of Ambiguity** is the foundational KTU framework. It states that ambiguity exists at **5 distinct linguistic levels** — and solving NLP requires tackling all of them simultaneously. ``` PHONETIC → PHONOLOGICAL → MORPHOLOGICAL → SYNTACTIC → SEMANTIC → PRAGMATIC → DISCOURSE (Sound) (Sound rules) (Word structure) (Grammar) (Meaning) (Intent) (Context) ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "list challenges of NLP," always mention the **5 levels of ambiguity** by name. Examiners explicitly look for this taxonomy in 14-mark answers. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Ambiguity (KTU High-Yield) ### Pillar 1: Lexical Ambiguity (Word-Level) A **single word** carries multiple valid meanings. - **Homonymy:** Same spelling, same sound, different meaning → "**Bat**" (animal / cricket bat) - **Polysemy:** Same word, related meanings → "**Head**" (of department / of body / of river) - **Homophony:** Same sound, different spelling → "**Their / There / They're**" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Disambiguation Tool Used:** Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD) using **Lesk Algorithm**, **BERT embeddings**, or **knowledge graphs (WordNet)**. ### Pillar 2: Syntactic Ambiguity (Structure-Level) A **sentence** can be parsed in more than one valid grammatical way. **Classic KTU Example:** "I saw the man with the telescope." $$ \text{Parse 1: } [I]_{NP} \, [saw]_{VP} \, [[the \, man]_{NP} \, [with \, the \, telescope]_{PP}]_{VP} \\ \text{Parse 2: } [I]_{NP} \, [saw \, [the \, man]_{NP}]_{VP} \, [with \, the \, telescope]_{PP} $$ **Other Examples:** - "**The chicken is ready to eat**" (chicken is hungry / chicken is cooked) - "**Visiting relatives can be boring**" (activity / people) - "**Time flies like an arrow**" (verb vs. noun usage) ### Pillar 3: Semantic Ambiguity (Meaning-Level) A sentence is **grammatically correct** but has **multiple interpretations** due to word meaning. - "**He went to the bank**" → river bank OR financial bank? - "**Every student read a book**" → Did every student read the SAME book or DIFFERENT books? (Quantifier scope ambiguity) ### Pillar 4: Pragmatic Ambiguity (Intent-Level) Meaning depends on **context, speaker intent, and world knowledge**. - "**Can you pass the salt?**" → A **request**, not a yes/no question. - "**It's hot in here**" → Could be a **request to open a window** (Indirect Speech Act). - Sarcasm: "**Oh wonderful, my code crashed again!**" ### Pillar 5: Discourse Ambiguity (Multi-Sentence Context) Meaning depends on **previous sentences**; often involves **coreference resolution**. - "**John told Mike that he had won the lottery.**" → Who is "**he**" — John or Mike? - "**The trophy didn't fit in the suitcase because it was too big.**" → "**it**" = trophy (size) OR "**it**" = suitcase (size issue)? (Winograd Schema Challenge) ## 2.2 Other Major Challenges — Detailed Breakdown ### Challenge A: Language Diversity and Multilingualism - **7000+** living languages worldwide - **Multiple scripts:** Latin, Devanagari, Mandarin, Arabic, Cyrillic - **Code-mixing:** "Hinglish" (Hindi + English): "Main **shop** ja raha hoon" - **Morphological richness:** Turkish, Finnish, Malayalam → agglutinative (one word = full sentence in English) ### Challenge B: Low-Resource Languages - Languages with **< 1 GB of digital text** are termed low-resource. - Examples: **Malayalam, Tamil, Swahili, Nepali**. - **Problem:** Modern LLMs (GPT, BERT) are trained primarily on English → **performance drops 30–60%** on Indic languages. ### Challenge C: Domain Specificity - A model trained on **Wikipedia** fails on **medical reports** or **legal contracts**. - **Solution:** Domain Adaptation, Transfer Learning, Fine-tuning on domain corpora. - **Domain-specific jargon:** "**STEMI**" (medical) vs. "**due diligence**" (legal) vs. "**deposition**" (legal/chemistry). ### Challenge D: Data Sparsity and Zipf's Law For any natural language corpus, the frequency of a word is **inversely proportional** to its rank in the frequency table. $$ f(r) \propto \frac{1}{r^\alpha} \quad \text{where } \alpha \approx 1 $$ This means **long-tail words** (rare, technical terms) appear very few times — making it **impossible** to learn their representations from limited data. This is the **fundamental data sparsity problem**. ### Challenge E: Common-Sense Reasoning and World Knowledge - "**The man put the cup on the table and it broke.**" → The **cup** broke, not the table. - Requires **physical reasoning** (cups are fragile) and **coreference resolution**. - The **Winograd Schema Challenge** is the standard KTU benchmark for this. ### Challenge F: Sarcasm, Irony, and Sentiment Nuance - "**I just LOVE waiting in long queues.**" → Negative sentiment (sarcastic). - Requires **prosody** (in speech), **context**, and **emotional intelligence**. ### Challenge G: Bias and Fairness - Training data reflects **societal biases** (gender, race, region). - Example: "**Doctor**" → "**he**" (gender bias); "**Programmer**" → "**he**". - Leads to **discriminatory outputs** in hiring, lending, and criminal justice AI. ### Challenge H: Evaluation Difficulty - **No universal metric** captures language quality. - Human evaluation is **expensive, slow, and subjective**. - Automated metrics (BLEU, ROUGE, METEOR, BERTScore) have known limitations. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Equations) > [!IMPORTANT] > **MASTER THESE EQUATIONS — they appear in 7-mark sub-parts.** | # | Formula | Meaning | Used For | |:-:|:--------|:--------|:---------| | 1 | $\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of predicted positives, how many correct? | Classification eval | | 2 | $\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Of actual positives, how many caught? | Classification eval | | 3 | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P & R | Balanced metric | | 4 | $\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correct predictions | Balanced datasets | | 5 | $\text{BLEU} = BP \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | Translation quality | Machine Translation | | 6 | $BP = \min\left(1, e^{1 - \frac{r}{c}}\right)$ | Brevity Penalty in BLEU | Penalizes short output | | 7 | $\text{Perplexity} = 2^{H(p)} = \prod_{t=1}^{T} \frac{1}{P(w_t \mid w_{ [!WARNING] > **Pipe Symbol Rule:** Use `\vert` instead of `|` in LaTeX equations inside markdown tables to avoid rendering breaks (e.g., $P \vert w_{ # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: F1-Score from Precision and Recall **Given:** Precision (P) and Recall (R) of a binary classifier. **Step 1 — Definition of F1 (Harmonic Mean):** $$ F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}} $$ The harmonic mean (not arithmetic) is used because it **penalizes extreme values** — a model with P = 1.0, R = 0.0 is useless, and the harmonic mean correctly returns 0. **Step 2 — Combine the fractions:** $$ F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{R + P}{P \cdot R}} $$ **Step 3 — Invert the denominator:** $$ F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} $$ **Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style):** Suppose a spam classifier has: - True Positives (TP) = 80 - False Positives (FP) = 20 - False Negatives (FN) = 40 - True Negatives (TN) = 860 **Step 5 — Compute Precision:** $$ P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{80}{80 + 20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80 $$ **Step 6 — Compute Recall:** $$ R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{80}{80 + 40} = \frac{80}{120} = 0.667 $$ **Step 7 — Compute F1:** $$ F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.80 \cdot 0.667}{0.80 + 0.667} = \frac{1.0672}{1.467} = 0.7275 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Logic Behind Step 7:** The numerator is 2PR, the denominator is P+R. Substituting: 2 × 0.80 × 0.667 = 1.0672. Adding 0.80 + 0.667 = 1.467. Dividing gives F1 = 0.7275 (≈ 72.75%). ## 3.2 Derivation: BLEU Score for Machine Translation **BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy)** measures n-gram overlap between machine translation and reference translation. **Step 1 — Modified n-gram Precision:** $$ p_n = \frac{\sum_{C \in \text{Candidates}} \sum_{\text{ngram} \in C} \text{Count}_{\text{clip}}(\text{ngram})}{\sum_{C \in \text{Candidates}} \sum_{\text{ngram} \in C} \text{Count}(\text{ngram})} $$ Where $\text{Count}_{\text{clip}}$ caps a candidate n-gram count by its maximum reference count (prevents gaming the metric). **Step 2 — Combined BLEU Formula:** $$ \text{BLEU} = BP \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right) $$ Where $w_n = \frac{1}{N}$ (uniform weights, typically N = 4). **Step 3 — Brevity Penalty (BP):** $$ BP = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } c > r \\ \exp\left(1 - \frac{r}{c}\right) & \text{if } c \le r \end{cases} $$ Here $c$ = candidate length, $r$ = reference length. **Step 4 — Worked KTU Example:** - Reference: "**The cat is on the mat**" (r = 6) - Candidate: "**cat on mat**" (c = 3) **Step 5 — Compute 1-gram precision:** - Reference 1-grams: {the, cat, is, on, the, mat} - Candidate 1-grams: {cat, on, mat} - Clipped counts: cat=1, on=1, mat=1, total clipped = 3 - Total candidate 1-grams = 3 - $p_1 = 3/3 = 1.0$ **Step 6 — Compute BP:** Since $c = 3 < r = 6$: $$ BP = e^{1 - \frac{6}{3}} = e^{-1} = 0.3679 $$ **Step 7 — Final BLEU (with $p_1 = 1.0$, $p_2 = 0$, $p_3 = 0$, $p_4 = 0$):** $$ \text{BLEU} = 0.3679 \cdot \exp(0.25 \cdot \log 1 + 0.25 \cdot \log 0 + 0.25 \cdot \log 0 + 0.25 \cdot \log 0) $$ Since $\log 0 = -\infty$, **BLEU = 0** — penalizing the brevity severely. This is the desired behavior. > [!NOTE] > **Logic of Step 7:** When any higher-order n-gram precision is 0, the entire exponential term tends to 0, making the final BLEU 0 (modulo BP). This penalizes translations that miss word-order. ## 3.3 Derivation: Perplexity of a Language Model **Perplexity (PPL)** measures how "surprised" a model is by test data. Lower PPL = better model. **Step 1 — Definition from Information Theory:** For a probability distribution $p$ over a vocabulary, the perplexity is the exponential of the cross-entropy. $$ \text{PPL} = 2^{H(p)} = 2^{-\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2 p(x_i)} $$ **Step 2 — For an Autoregressive Language Model (predicting $w_t$ from $w_{ [!NOTE] > **Logic of Step 4:** Multiplying 2 × 4 × 2 × 4 = 64. The fourth root of 64 is $\sqrt[4]{64} = 64^{0.25} = 2.828$. So PPL ≈ 2.83 — meaning the model is "as confused as choosing uniformly between 2.83 options at each step." ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Challenge-to-Solution Mapping | Challenge | Why It's Hard | Modern Solution | KTU-Relevant Technique | |:----------|:--------------|:----------------|:-----------------------| | **Lexical Ambiguity** | One word → many meanings | WSD using BERT/WordNet | Lesk Algorithm | | **Syntactic Ambiguity** | Multiple valid parse trees | Probabilistic Context-Free Grammars (PCFG) | CKY Algorithm | | **Semantic Ambiguity** | Word sense variation | Distributional Semantics (Word2Vec) | Cosine Similarity | | **Pragmatic Ambiguity** | Intent ≠ literal meaning | Speech Act Theory + RNNs | Intent Classification | | **Discourse Ambiguity** | Cross-sentence reference | End-to-end Coref Models (BERT-coref) | Mention-Pair Scoring | | **Low-Resource** | < 1 GB text | Transfer Learning, Multilingual BERT (mBERT) | XLM-R, IndicBERT | | **Domain Specificity** | Out-of-distribution jargon | Fine-tuning on domain corpus | BioBERT, FinBERT, LegalBERT | | **Common-Sense** | Requires world knowledge | Knowledge Graphs (ConceptNet, ATOMIC) | Winograd Schemas | | **Sarcasm/Irony** | Tone, context-dependent | Contextual transformers + affective features | SOTA: ~70% F1 | | **Bias** | Training data is biased | Debiasing algorithms, counterfactual data | Hard-Debias, INLP | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Taxonomy of NLP Challenges (KTU High-Yield) ```mermaid graph TD A["NLP Challenges"]:::root --> B["Linguistic Ambiguity"]:::cat A --> C["Resource Constraints"]:::cat A --> D["Semantic & Reasoning"]:::cat A --> E["Evaluation & Bias"]:::cat B --> B1["Lexical Ambiguity
e.g. Bank"]:::leaf B --> B2["Syntactic Ambiguity
e.g. Saw man with telescope"]:::leaf B --> B3["Semantic Ambiguity
e.g. He is at the bank"]:::leaf B --> B4["Pragmatic Ambiguity
e.g. Can you pass salt"]:::leaf B --> B5["Discourse Ambiguity
e.g. Coreference he"]:::leaf C --> C1["Low Resource Languages
e.g. Malayalam"]:::leaf C --> C2["Code Mixing
e.g. Hinglish"]:::leaf C --> C3["Data Sparsity
Zipf Law"]:::leaf C --> C4["Domain Specificity
e.g. Medical Legal"]:::leaf D --> D1["Common Sense Reasoning
e.g. Winograd"]:::leaf D --> D2["Sarcasm and Irony"]:::leaf D --> D3["Named Entity Disambiguation"]:::leaf D --> D4["Negation and Scope"]:::leaf E --> E1["Subjective Human Eval"]:::leaf E --> E2["Gender and Racial Bias"]:::leaf E --> E3["Metric Limitations
BLEU ROUGE"]:::leaf E --> E4["Lack of Gold Standards"]:::leaf classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:3px classDef cat fill:#4a7ba6,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px classDef leaf fill:#a8c5e0,stroke:#000,color:#000,stroke-width:1px ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Pipeline Mapping Challenges to NLP Tasks ```mermaid flowchart LR Input["Raw Text Input"]:::input Token["Tokenization"]:::step POS["POS Tagging
Syntactic Challenge"]:::task Parse["Parsing
Structural Ambiguity"]:::task Sem["Semantic Analysis
Word Sense Disambiguation"]:::task Coref["Coreference Resolution
Discourse Ambiguity"]:::task Prag["Pragmatic Layer
Intent and Sarcasm"]:::task Out["Final Interpretation"]:::output Input --> Token Token --> POS POS --> Parse Parse --> Sem Sem --> Coref Coref --> Prag Prag --> Out classDef input fill:#2d5f3f,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px classDef output fill:#8b3a3a,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px classDef step fill:#d4a017,stroke:#000,color:#000,stroke-width:2px classDef task fill:#5a4fcf,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Block-Level Architecture of an NLP System ```mermaid subgraph L1["Input Layer"] I1["Text or Speech"] I2["Multilingual Input"] I3["Noisy User Data"] end subgraph L2["Preprocessing Stage"] P1["Tokenization and Segmentation"] P2["Normalization and Stemming"] P3["Stopword Removal"] end subgraph L3["Core NLP Engine"] E1["Morphological Analyzer"] E2["Syntactic Parser PCFG"] E3["Semantic Role Labeler"] E4["Pragmatic Interpreter"] E5["Discourse Manager"] end subgraph L4["Knowledge Layer"] K1["WordNet Knowledge Graph"] K2["ConceptNet Common Sense"] K3["Domain Ontology"] end subgraph L5["Application Output"] O1["Translation"] O2["Sentiment Output"] O3["Question Answer"] O4["Chatbot Reply"] end I1 --> P1 I2 --> P1 I3 --> P1 P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> E1 E1 --> E2 E2 --> E3 E3 --> E4 E4 --> E5 E1 -. uses .-> K1 E3 -. uses .-> K2 E4 -. uses .-> K3 E5 -. uses .-> K2 E5 --> O1 E5 --> O2 E5 --> O3 E5 --> O4 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why this diagram matters for KTU:** In 14-mark questions, examiners expect you to draw a **layered architecture** showing how ambiguity is progressively resolved — from raw text (Input) → preprocessing → core NLP tasks → knowledge layer → application output. The arrows showing knowledge layer as a **dashed lateral feed** represent the **contextual reasoning** needed to solve ambiguity challenges. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Lexical Ambiguity with an example. Why is it considered a fundamental challenge in NLP? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Lexical Ambiguity occurs when a **single word** has **multiple valid meanings**, and the correct interpretation depends on context. **Example (1 mark):** The word "**bank**" can mean: - a financial institution ("I deposited money in the **bank**") - the side of a river ("We sat on the river **bank**") **Why it's a Challenge (0.5 marks):** Computers process text as character sequences without inherent world knowledge. Without sophisticated **Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)** algorithms, machines cannot determine which "bank" is intended, leading to incorrect translations, search results, or chatbot responses. --- ### Question 2: What is Pragmatic Ambiguity? How does it differ from Semantic Ambiguity? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Pragmatic Ambiguity (1.5 marks):** It occurs when the **intended meaning** of an utterance differs from its **literal meaning**, depending on context, speaker intent, and world knowledge. **Example:** "**Can you pass the salt?**" — grammatically a question about ability, but pragmatically a **request** to pass the salt. **Difference from Semantic Ambiguity (1.5 marks):** - **Semantic Ambiguity** arises from **word/phrase meaning**: "He went to the **bank**" (river vs. money). - **Pragmatic Ambiguity** arises from **intent and context**: "It's cold in here" may be a request to close a window. - Semantic deals with **what words mean**; Pragmatic deals with **what speakers mean**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` #### Part (a) — Explain the different levels of ambiguity in NLP with suitable examples. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 mark):** Ambiguity is the presence of **multiple possible interpretations** for the same linguistic expression.
