# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix: Ethical Evaluation of an NLP System

> [!NOTE]
> Since "Ethics in NLP" is a conceptual topic, this section replaces mathematical derivation with a **structured 7-step analytical framework** that students can apply to *any* NLP system. This matrix is high-yield for KTU's "discuss" and "analyze" sub-parts (7-mark questions).

## 3.1 The 7-Step Ethical Audit Framework

When asked *"Discuss the ethical issues in [an NLP application]"*, apply this matrix:

### **Step 1: Identify the NLP Task and Stakeholders**

Clearly state what the system does and **who is affected** (directly and indirectly).

*Example:* A resume-screening NLP system. Stakeholders: applicants (disadvantaged groups disproportionately), HR teams, company, society.

### **Step 2: Audit the Training Data**

Ask:
- Where did the data come from? (Web scrape? User data? Synthetic?)
- Whose voices are over/under-represented?
- Was informed consent obtained?
- Are there demographic labels for bias auditing?

*Example:* If the system is trained on 10 years of past hiring decisions that were themselves biased, it learns to **replicate historical discrimination**.

### **Step 3: Identify Possible Harms**

Classify harms into:

- **Representational harm** — Stereotyping, erasure, demeaning portrayals
- **Allocational harm** — Unequal distribution of resources/opportunities
- **Quality-of-service harm** — Worse performance for certain dialects/groups
- **Interpersonal harm** — Privacy violations, manipulation
- **Societal/systemic harm** — Erosion of trust, democratic harms

### **Step 4: Quantify the Bias (If Possible)**

Use metrics like:
- **Demographic Parity Difference:** $\vert P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0) - P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1) \vert$ where $A$ is a protected attribute.
- **Equalized Odds:** $\vert P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=1, A=0) - P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=1, A=1) \vert$
- **WEAT effect size $d$** (from Section 2.2)

### **Step 5: Trace Accountability**

Map the **chain of responsibility**:

$$\text{Data Source} \rightarrow \text{Annotators} \rightarrow \text{Model Developers} \rightarrow \text{Deployers} \rightarrow \text{End Users} \rightarrow \text{Affected Parties}$$

At each link, ask: *"Could this actor have prevented the harm?"*

### **Step 6: Propose Mitigation Strategies**

| Layer | Mitigation |
|---|---|
| **Data** | Rebalanced datasets, synthetic minority oversampling, consent mechanisms |
| **Model** | Debiasing (Bolukbasi, 2016), adversarial debiasing, constrained optimization |
| **Output** | Output filtering, fairness-aware post-processing |
| **Interface** | User warnings, explanations (LIME/SHAP), opt-out options |
| **Governance** | Model cards, datasheets, third-party audits, red-teaming |

### **Step 7: Continuous Monitoring Post-Deployment**

Ethics is **not a one-time check**. Models can **drift** — their fairness properties degrade as real-world data distributions shift. Establish:
- Periodic re-audits
- User feedback channels
- Incident response protocols
- Versioned documentation

## 3.2 Analytical Case Study (Worked Example)

**Scenario:** A hospital deploys an NLP system to auto-classify patient discharge notes for insurance claims. The system uses a fine-tuned BERT model.

> **Step 1 (Task & Stakeholders):** Automates insurance pre-authorization. Stakeholders — patients, doctors, insurers, hospital admins.

> **Step 2 (Data Audit):** Trained on 5 years of historical notes. English-only. Predominantly urban hospital notes. **Rural, regional-language, and elderly patients underrepresented.**

> **Step 3 (Harms):**
> - *Quality-of-service harm:* Misclassifies notes with non-standard grammar (regional dialects, code-mixed Hindi-English).
> - *Allocational harm:* Patients from underrepresented groups get more claim denials.
> - *Privacy harm:* Discharge notes contain sensitive diagnoses, diagnoses that could leak via model outputs.

> **Step 4 (Quantify):** Compute per-demographic F1-scores. If F1 for Standard American English notes is **0.91** but for Indian English is **0.67**, the disparity is **0.24** — clinically and ethically significant.

> **Step 5 (Accountability):** Hospital administration (deployer) chose the model; vendor (developer) trained it on biased data; doctors (users) over-rely on outputs.

> **Step 6 (Mitigation):**
> - Retrain on a regionally diverse corpus
> - Add human-in-the-loop review for low-confidence cases
> - Implement differential privacy on patient identifiers
> - Publish a model card listing performance across dialects

> **Step 7 (Monitor):** Quarterly fairness audits; patient grievance redressal linked to misclassification rates.

This 7-step template can be adapted to **resume screeners, chatbots, translation systems, content moderation AI** — any NLP application KTU may ask about.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define algorithmic bias in the context of NLP. Give one example.**
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Algorithmic bias in NLP refers to the **systematic and repeatable errors** in a language model's outputs that produce unfair outcomes favoring certain demographic groups (defined by gender, race, age, language variety, etc.) over others. It typically arises from biased training data, biased annotation, or biased model design choices.

**Example (1 mark):** A **resume-screening NLP system** trained on historical hiring data from a male-dominated tech industry may learn to rank resumes with male-associated words (e.g., "executive," "competitive") higher than equally qualified female-associated resumes (e.g., "collaborative," "supportive"), thereby perpetuating gender discrimination at scale.

*Alternate valid examples:* Gendered coreference in machine translation ("doctor" → "he", "nurse" → "she"); racial disparities in ASR accuracy for African American English.

---

### **Question 2: What is differential privacy, and how does it help in NLP?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Differential Privacy (DP) is a **mathematical framework** that provides quantifiable privacy guarantees when analyzing or training models on sensitive data. It works by adding **carefully calibrated noise** to the training process (e.g., via DP-SGD) such that the inclusion or exclusion of any single individual's data record changes the model's output by at most a factor of $e^{\varepsilon}$, where $\varepsilon$ is the **privacy budget** (smaller $\varepsilon$ = stronger privacy).

**NLP Application (1 mark):** In NLP, DP is used to **train language models on sensitive corpora** (e.g., medical records, emails, private conversations) so that the model cannot memorize or leak verbatim personal information. For example, a hospital can train a clinical NLP model with DP-SGD so that no specific patient's discharge note can be extracted from the model, even by an adversary with full access to the model's weights.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** In Part B, students must answer **one full 14-mark question** per module, with **internal choice** (i.e., Q1 OR Q2, Q3 OR Q4, etc.). Each 14-mark question typically has **two 7-mark sub-parts**. Below we model the standard pattern.

---

### **Question A: Ethical Issues in Modern NLP Systems** (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper, Module 1]`

**(a) Discuss the major sources of bias in NLP systems. Explain with examples how each source can lead to representational or allocational harm. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Bias in NLP systems arises from **multiple interacting sources**, and each can cause distinct categories of harm.

**1. Training Data Bias (2 marks)**
The dominant source of bias is the training corpus itself. Large web-scraped datasets like Common Crawl over-represent English, male, Western, and younger voices. Example: A sentiment analysis system trained primarily on movie reviews may misclassify code-mixed Indian English reviews (e.g., "movie bahut accha tha") as negative simply because the Hindi-English code-switch pattern was rare in training, causing **quality-of-service harm** to non-native English speakers.

**2. Annotation Bias (2 marks)**
Human annotators bring their own cultural and demographic biases. A landmark example: Buolamwini and Gebru's 2018 study showed commercial gender-classification systems had error rates of **0.8% for light-skinned men but 34.7% for dark-skinned women** — a stark intersectional bias in facial recognition, with direct NLP analogues in coreference resolution and named-entity recognition for under-represented names.

**3. Algorithmic / Embedding Bias (2 marks)**
Word embeddings, the geometric foundation of modern NLP, encode societal biases. Bolukbasi et al. (2016) showed that $\vec{programmer} - \vec{man} + \vec{woman} \approx \vec{homemaker}$, meaning the embedding space itself contains the stereotype. This leads to **representational harm** — every downstream task inherits the bias.

**4. Evaluation Bias (1 mark)**
Standard benchmarks (GLUE, SQuAD, SuperGLUE) are dominated by American English text. Systems tuned for these benchmarks may degrade sharply on African American English, Indian English, or low-resource languages, producing **allocational harm** in real deployments (e.g., worse loan-application NLP for regional-language speakers).

**Categorization of harms:** Following Crawford (2017), harms are of two kinds:
- **Representational harm:** The system's outputs demean, erase, or stereotype a group (e.g., autocomplete suggesting "women should stay at home").
- **Allocational harm:** The system's outputs cause unequal distribution of resources, opportunities, or information (e.g., a biased resume screener denying jobs).

---

**(b) Explain the concept of accountability in NLP. Discuss the EU AI Act's risk-based classification and how it maps to NLP applications. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Accountability in NLP (3 marks):**
Accountability refers to the **principle that there must be a clear, traceable chain of responsibility** for the outputs and consequences of an NLP system. Unlike traditional software (where bugs cause crashes), NLP systems can produce **fluent, confident, but harmful outputs** (e.g., hallucinated legal citations, defamatory summaries, biased medical advice). The chain of responsibility typically includes:

$$\text{Data Collectors} \rightarrow \text{Annotators} \rightarrow \text{Model Developers} \rightarrow \text{Deployers} \rightarrow \text{End Users} \rightarrow \text{Affected Parties}$$

The 2023 *Mata v. Avianca* case (lawyer sanctioned for submitting ChatGPT-fabricated cases) showed the legal system is still catching up on who is liable.

**EU AI Act — Risk-Based Classification (3 marks):**
The EU AI Act (2024) classifies AI systems by risk level:

| Risk Tier | Requirements | NLP Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Unacceptable Risk** (Banned) | Prohibited outright | Social-scoring chatbots, manipulative AI targeting vulnerabilities |
| **High Risk** (Strict) | Conformity assessments, human oversight, data quality, transparency, accuracy | Resume screening AI, credit-scoring NLP, medical diagnosis NLP, legal judgment assistance |
| **Limited Risk** (Transparency) | Disclosure obligations | Chatbots (must disclose they are AI), deepfake text generators (must label outputs), emotion-recognition systems |
| **Minimal Risk** (No Obligation) | Voluntary best practices | Spam filters, autocorrect, game NPCs |

**Mapping to NLP (1 mark):** An NLP-based medical triage system falls under **High Risk** — it requires extensive documentation, bias audits, human-in-the-loop, and post-market monitoring. A customer service chatbot is **Limited Risk** — it must simply disclose that users are talking to an AI. This tiered approach gives regulators a proportionate tool and forces developers to consider ethics at the design stage.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice): Privacy, Transparency, and the Future of Ethical NLP** (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper, Module 1 Alternative]`

**(a) With suitable examples, explain how privacy risks emerge in modern NLP systems. Discuss differential privacy as a mitigation strategy. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Sources of Privacy Risk in NLP (3 marks):**

1. **Training Data Memorization:** Large language models can memorize and **regurgitate verbatim training data**. Carlini et al. (2021) demonstrated that given a specific prompt, GPT-2 could be made to output **names, email addresses, phone numbers, and entire code snippets** that appeared only once in its training set. This is catastrophic when the training set contains medical records, legal documents, or private conversations.

2. **Inference Attacks:** Adversaries can craft inputs that cause the model to leak information about specific training examples (e.g., membership inference — "Was this patient's record in the training data?").

3. **Consent Deficit:** Most web-scraped corpora (Common Crawl, web forums, social media) are collected **without explicit informed consent** from the authors — a foundational ethical violation.

4. **Embedding Inversion:** Sentence embeddings can sometimes be **inverted back to near-original text** by attackers, recovering sensitive content from vector databases.

5. **Federated Learning Leakage:** Even in federated setups (where data stays on-device), gradient updates can leak information about local data if not properly protected.

**Differential Privacy as Mitigation (3 marks):**

Differential Privacy (DP) provides a **mathematically rigorous guarantee**: for any two datasets that differ in only one record, the output distributions of the model are nearly identical.

Formally, a randomized algorithm $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies $\varepsilon$-differential privacy if for all datasets $D, D'$ differing in one record and all outputs $S$:

$$P(\mathcal{M}(D) \in S) \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot P(\mathcal{M}(D') \in S)$$

The smaller $\varepsilon$, the stronger the privacy. DP-SGD (Differentially Private Stochastic Gradient Descent) achieves this by **clipping per-example gradients** and **adding Gaussian noise** calibrated to the clipping norm and a noise multiplier $\sigma$.

**Trade-off:** Stronger privacy (smaller $\varepsilon$) typically reduces model utility (accuracy). The privacy-utility frontier is a central research challenge. DP-NLG and DP-finetuning are active research areas.

**One-line exam summary (1 mark):** "Differential privacy adds calibrated noise during training so that any single individual's data has a bounded and quantifiable influence on the model, preventing memorization and leakage."

---

**(b) Discuss the concept of transparency in NLP. Explain model cards and datasheets for datasets as transparency tools. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Why Transparency Matters (2 marks):**
A user receiving a loan-rejection, a job-screening decision, or a medical-flag from an NLP system has a **right to understand the basis** of that decision. Black-box deep models (especially LLMs) make this difficult — a phenomenon called the **opacity problem**. Transparency also enables **debugging, regulatory audit, and informed consent**.

**Three Levels of Transparency (2 marks):**
1. **Algorithmic transparency** — Publishing model architecture and training procedure
2. **Data transparency** — Disclosing training data sources, curation, and limitations
3. **Output transparency** — Explaining *why* the model made a specific prediction (e.g., via LIME, SHAP, attention heatmaps)

**Model Cards (Mitchell et al., 2019) (2 marks):**
A **Model Card** is a structured, standardized document accompanying a trained model that reports:
- Intended use cases and out-of-scope uses
- Training data summary
- Quantitative performance **disaggregated by demographic subgroup** (e.g., accuracy per gender, per dialect, per age group)
- Ethical considerations and known limitations
- Recommendations for safe deployment

*Example:* A model card for a hate-speech detection system would report precision/recall for English vs. Hindi, for tweets vs. news comments, and explicitly warn that performance drops on African American English.

**Datasheets for Datasets (Gebru et al., 2021) (1 mark):**
A **Datasheet** is the dataset counterpart of a model card. It documents:
- Motivation and composition
- Collection process (who collected, how, when, from where)
- Preprocessing and labeling
- Uses, distribution, and maintenance
- **Consent, privacy, and potential biases** explicitly addressed

*Why this matters for KTU:* If a KTU question asks "How do you ensure an NLP system is ethical?", the strongest single answer is: **publish a model card AND a datasheet**. Together, they form the **documentation backbone** of ethical NLP practice.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Ethics Questions (Read Carefully!)**
>
> 1. **Vague answers lose marks.** Writing "NLP can be biased" without specifying *what kind* of bias, *where it originates*, and *what harm it causes* scores **0–1 out of 7**. Always give: **(a) source, (b) example, (c) harm type**.
>
> 2. **Don't confuse bias types.** Students often mix up *representational* and *allocational* harm. Memorize the one-line distinction: **Representational = how the system *talks about* a group; Allocational = how the system *treats* a group in resource distribution.**
>
> 3. **No "ethics" without a real example.** Abstract philosophical answers score poorly. Always anchor in a **named real-world incident** (Amazon hiring AI, Mata v. Avianca, COMPAS recidivism, Tay chatbot). Examiners reward concrete examples with **+1 to +2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Don't write Python code for conceptual questions.** Ethics questions are descriptive. The KTU 2024 scheme's Part A and B for this topic test *understanding*, not *implementation*. Code snippets waste time and earn zero marks.
>
> 5. **Memorize the 5-pillar framework.** Examiners often have a "list 5 ethical issues" sub-part. A structured 5-pillar answer (Fairness, Accountability, Transparency, Privacy, Societal Impact) scores full marks; an unstructured list of random concerns scores partial.
>
> 6. **One diagram ≠ full marks.** Drawing the 5-pillar wheel alone is not enough. Examiners expect at least **2–3 lines of explanation per pillar**. Use diagrams as **anchors**, not as substitutes for prose.
>
> 7. **Differential Privacy formula — write it carefully.** The $e^{\varepsilon}$ bound is the *definition*. Examiners will deduct marks for missing the bound or confusing it with a generic "noise" description.
>
> 8. **Model Cards vs. Datasheets — don't swap them.** Model Card = *for the model*. Datasheet = *for the dataset*. This is the most common swap error in KTU answer sheets.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **30-Second Pre-Exam Revision — Memorize This List:**

- [ ] Ethics in NLP = applying moral principles to language technology design, development, and deployment
- [ ] **Five pillars:** Fairness, Accountability, Transparency, Privacy, Societal Impact
- [ ] **Two harm types:** Representational (demeaning) + Allocational (unequal distribution)
- [ ] **Three bias sources:** Data bias, Annotation bias, Algorithmic/Embedding bias
- [ ] **One seminal example:** Bolukbasi et al. (2016) — gender bias in word embeddings ($\vec{programmer} - \vec{man} + \vec{woman} \approx \vec{homemaker}$)
- [ ] **One seminal example:** Buolamwini & Gebru (2018) — 34.7% error for dark-skinned women vs 0.8% for light-skinned men
- [ ] **One seminal example:** Carlini et al. (2021) — verbatim training data extraction from GPT-2
- [ ] **Differential Privacy definition:** $P(\mathcal{M}(D) \in S) \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot P(\mathcal{M}(D') \in S)$
- [ ] **Privacy budget $\varepsilon$:** smaller = stronger privacy, larger = better utility
- [ ] **Model Card** = documentation for the model (Mitchell et al., 2019)
- [ ] **Datasheet for Datasets** = documentation for the data (Gebru et al., 2021)
- [ ] **EU AI Act risk tiers:** Unacceptable (banned) → High (strict audit) → Limited (disclose) → Minimal (voluntary)
- [ ] **Hallucination** = fluent but factually false LLM output
- [ ] **Model collapse** = distributional degradation from training on AI-generated text
- [ ] **Right to be forgotten** = GDPR Article 17 — right to data deletion
- [ ] **Dual-use problem** = same LLM that helps can also generate phishing/misinformation
- [ ] **Anthropomorphism** = users attributing human qualities to AI (Sydney/Bing chatbot, 2023)
- [ ] **Consent deficit** = web-scraped training data often lacks informed user consent
- [ ] **WEAT effect size $d$** = quantitative measure of bias in word embeddings
- [ ] **Environmental cost** = training large LLMs emits hundreds of tons of $\text{CO}_2$
- [ ] **Counterfactual fairness** = outcome should be the same in a counterfactual world where the protected attribute is changed

> **Final Exam Tip:** A well-answered KTU ethics question always contains — **(1) a named framework or pillar, (2) a real-world example, (3) a harm type, (4) a mitigation strategy**. Master this quartet and you will score full marks regardless of the specific NLP application the examiner chooses.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pre-Processing, Text Representation & Information Extraction: NLP System Pipeline - Modern Approaches, Including Transfer Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT601 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pre-Processing, Text Representation & Information Extraction: NLP System Pipeline - Modern Approaches, Including Transfer Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The NLP System Pipeline — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Examiner Tone):**
> An **NLP System Pipeline** is a structured, sequential architecture of computational modules that transforms raw, unstructured natural language text into a machine-interpretable form, and subsequently derives semantic, syntactic, or pragmatic information from it. The 2024 scheme emphasizes a **modern, layered pipeline** incorporating **transfer learning** as the final representation layer.

The pipeline is conventionally decomposed into three macro-stages:

1. **Pre-Processing Layer** — Cleansing, normalization, and linguistic structuring of raw text.
2. **Text Representation Layer** — Encoding tokens into dense numerical vectors (embeddings).
3. **Information Extraction (IE) Layer** — Recovering structured knowledge (entities, relations, sentiments, summaries) from the representation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The phrase *"Modern Approaches, Including Transfer Learning"* explicitly signals that students **must** cover pre-trained transformer-based models (BERT, RoBERTa, GPT family, T5) — classical-only answers will be marked incomplete.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Kitchen Recipe"

Think of the NLP pipeline exactly like preparing a dish in a professional kitchen:

| Kitchen Stage | NLP Pipeline Stage | Real-World Action |
|---|---|---|
| Washing & chopping raw vegetables | **Pre-Processing** (Tokenization, Stop-word removal, Stemming) | Removes noise, makes raw material uniform |
| Marinating & seasoning | **Normalization** (Lemmatization, lowercasing) | Standardizes flavor profile |
| Cooking in standardized vessels | **Text Representation** (BoW → TF-IDF → Embeddings) | Converts ingredients into a *measurable form* |
| Plating & serving | **Information Extraction** (NER, RE, Summarization) | Extracts the final *useful knowledge* for the consumer |
| Re-using a master chef's recipe | **Transfer Learning** (Fine-tuning BERT/GPT) | Leverages pre-trained knowledge for new tasks |

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Standardized for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard evaluation metrics that must be memorized:
> - **Precision (P)** — Fraction of retrieved items that are relevant
> - **Recall (R)** — Fraction of relevant items that are retrieved
> - **F1-Score** — Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall
> - **Perplexity** — Lower is better; measures how well a probability model predicts a sample (used in transfer learning language models)
> - **BLEU Score** — For machine translation quality
> - **ROUGE-L** — For summarization quality

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stage 1 — Pre-Processing (Detailed Logical Steps)

The pre-processing layer executes these operations in order:

1. **Sentence Segmentation** — Splits a paragraph into sentences using punctuation + heuristic rules.
   *Why:* Downstream models (especially transformer-based) often perform better when sentence boundaries are explicit.

2. **Tokenization** — Splits text into atomic units (words, sub-words, or characters).
   *Why:* Neural models require discrete input indices.

3. **Lowercasing** — Converts all characters to lowercase.
   *Why:* Reduces vocabulary size; **caveat** — destroys case-sensitive signals (e.g., "US" the country vs "us" the pronoun).

4. **Stop-Word Removal** — Discards high-frequency, low-semantic words (the, is, at, which).
   *Why:* Reduces dimensionality; **caveat** — modern transformers *retain* stop-words because they carry contextual syntactic value.

5. **Stemming** — Crude suffix chopping (Porter Stemmer).
   *Why:* Maps "running", "runs", "ran" → "run".
   *How:* Rule-based stripping of suffixes like `-ing`, `-ed`, `-s`.

6. **Lemmatization** — Dictionary-based morphological reduction to root form.
   *Why:* Produces linguistically valid lemma (e.g., "better" → "good").

7. **Normalization** — Handles contractions (don't → do not), removes punctuation, handles unicode/emojis.

## 2.2 Stage 2 — Text Representation (Classical to Modern)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU expects students to know the **evolutionary path**:
> **One-Hot → Bag-of-Words (BoW) → TF-IDF → Word2Vec (Word Embeddings) → Contextual Embeddings (ELMo, BERT).**

### 2.2.1 Bag-of-Words (BoW)
A document is represented as a vector of word counts from a fixed vocabulary V. Order and grammar are discarded.

$$D = [n(w_1), n(w_2), \ldots, n(w_{\vert V \vert})]$$

### 2.2.2 TF-IDF (Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency)
Weighs a word by its importance in a document *relative* to the entire corpus.

### 2.2.3 Word Embeddings (Word2Vec — Skip-gram & CBOW)
Each word is mapped to a **dense vector of dimension d (typically d = 100, 200, 300)** such that semantically similar words occupy nearby points in the vector space.

### 2.2.4 Contextual Embeddings (Transfer Learning Era)
Unlike Word2Vec where "bank" has a single static vector, models like **BERT** produce *different* vectors for "bank" depending on whether it means *river bank* or *financial bank*. This is the cornerstone of modern NLP.

## 2.3 Stage 3 — Information Extraction (IE)

IE is the task of automatically extracting **structured information** from unstructured text. The four classical sub-tasks are:

1. **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** — Identifies and classifies proper nouns (PERSON, ORG, LOC, DATE, MONEY).
2. **Relation Extraction (RE)** — Identifies semantic relationships between entities (e.g., *CEO-of*, *located-in*).
3. **Event Extraction** — Detects triggers and arguments of events (e.g., "acquisition", "merger").
4. **Template Filling** — Populates predefined slots in a structured form.

## 2.4 Transfer Learning — The Modern Paradigm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transfer Learning Definition (KTU Standard):**
> Transfer Learning is a machine learning paradigm where a model **pre-trained on a large source task/dataset** is **reused or fine-tuned** on a smaller, task-specific target dataset. In NLP, this means pre-training a language model on billions of tokens (e.g., Wikipedia, Common Crawl), then fine-tuning the same model on small labelled datasets for downstream tasks (NER, sentiment, QA).

### Two Strategic Approaches:

| Strategy | Description | Example Models |
|---|---|---|
| **Feature-Based Transfer** | Pre-trained embeddings used as fixed features | Word2Vec, GloVe, ELMo |
| **Fine-Tuning Based Transfer** | All (or most) pre-trained weights are updated on the new task | BERT, RoBERTa, GPT-2/3/4, T5 |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Term Frequency (TF) | $\text{TF}(t,d) = \dfrac{n_{t,d}}{\sum_{w \in d} n_{w,d}}$ | How frequent is term *t* in document *d*? |
| 2 | Inverse Document Frequency (IDF) | $\text{IDF}(t, D) = \log \dfrac{\vert D \vert}{1 + \vert \{ d \in D : t \in d \} \vert}$ | Penalizes common terms across corpus |
| 3 | TF-IDF Score | $\text{TF-IDF}(t,d,D) = \text{TF}(t,d) \times \text{IDF}(t,D)$ | Weighted term importance |
| 4 | Cosine Similarity | $\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{A \cdot B}{\Vert A \Vert \cdot \Vert B \Vert}$ | Document similarity |
| 5 | Skip-gram Objective | $L = \dfrac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{-c \le j \le c, j \neq 0} \log P(w_{t+j} \vert w_t)$ | Word2Vec training |
| 6 | Softmax for Context Prediction | $P(w_O \vert w_I) = \dfrac{\exp(v'_{w_O} \cdot v_{w_I})}{\sum_{w=1}^{W} \exp(v'_w \cdot v_{w_I})}$ | Word2Vec output layer |
| 7 | Perplexity | $\text{PPL}(W) = P(w_1 w_2 \ldots w_N)^{-1/N}$ | LM evaluation |
| 8 | Precision | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | IE evaluation |
| 9 | Recall | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | IE evaluation |
| 10 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Balanced metric |
| 11 | BLEU (geometric) | $\text{BLEU} = BP \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | Translation evaluation |
| 12 | Self-Attention | $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | Transformer core |
| 13 | Scaled Dot-Product Stability | Divide by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to prevent softmax saturation | Numerical stability |
| 14 | Negative Sampling Loss | $\log \sigma(v'_{w_O} \cdot v_{w_I}) + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \mathbb{E}_{w_i \sim P_n(w)} [\log \sigma(-v'_{w_i} \cdot v_{w_I})]$ | Word2Vec efficient training |
| 15 | Transfer Learning Objective | $\theta^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta} \mathcal{L}_{\text{target}}(f_{\theta}(x); y)$ | Fine-tuning minimization |

> **Where:**
> - $n_{t,d}$ = count of term *t* in document *d*
> - $\vert D \vert$ = total number of documents in corpus
> - $c$ = context window size in Word2Vec
> - $T$ = total tokens in training corpus
> - $v_w, v'_w$ = input/output embeddings of word *w*
> - $d_k$ = key vector dimension in attention
> - $w_I$ = input (center) word, $w_O$ = output (context) word
> - $k$ = number of negative samples
> - $\theta$ = model parameters
> - $\mathcal{L}$ = task-specific loss function
> - $BP$ = brevity penalty in BLEU
> - $p_n$ = n-gram precision

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search Engines** (Google, Bing) — Use TF-IDF + BERT re-rankers.
- **Healthcare** — Clinical NER extracts diseases, drugs, dosages from electronic health records.
- **Finance** — Sentiment + NER on Reuters/Bloomberg feeds for algorithmic trading.
- **Legal Tech** — Contract clause extraction & summarization using fine-tuned T5/BART.
- **Customer Support** — Intent classification + slot filling using fine-tuned BERT.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: TF-IDF Mathematical Foundation

### Step 1 — Term Frequency (TF)

We define the raw frequency of term *t* in document *d* as $n_{t,d}$. To normalize for document length, we divide by the total number of terms in the document:

$$\text{TF}(t,d) = \frac{n_{t,d}}{\sum_{w \in d} n_{w,d}}$$

**Conversion Logic:** This converts absolute counts into a *relative frequency* in $[0,1]$, ensuring a 5-page document is not unfairly weighted over a 1-page document.

### Step 2 — Document Frequency (DF)

$$\text{DF}(t, D) = \vert \{ d \in D : t \in d \} \vert$$

**Logic:** Number of documents in corpus *D* that contain term *t*.

### Step 3 — Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)

We want a weight that is **low** when a term appears in *many* documents (e.g., "the") and **high** when a term is *rare* (e.g., "quantum"). The logarithm is applied to dampen the effect:

$$\text{IDF}(t, D) = \log \frac{\vert D \vert}{1 + \text{DF}(t, D)}$$

**Logic:** The "+1" inside the denominator is **add-one smoothing** to prevent division by zero when a term appears in *no* document during training.

### Step 4 — Combined TF-IDF

$$\text{TF-IDF}(t, d, D) = \text{TF}(t, d) \times \text{IDF}(t, D)$$

**Logic:** The product captures both **local importance** (TF — important *in this document*) and **global rarity** (IDF — important *across the corpus*).

## 3.2 Derivation: Word2Vec Skip-gram Objective Function

### Step 1 — Define the Problem

Given a center word $w_I$ at position *t* in a corpus, predict the surrounding context words $w_{t-c}, \ldots, w_{t-1}, w_{t+1}, \ldots, w_{t+c}$ within a window of size *c*.

### Step 2 — Independence Assumption

We assume each context word is conditionally independent given the center word:

$$P(w_{t-c}, \ldots, w_{t+c} \vert w_t) = \prod_{-c \le j \le c, j \neq 0} P(w_{t+j} \vert w_t)$$

**Logic:** This factorization makes the optimization tractable; the model becomes a multiclass classifier.

### Step 3 — Softmax Probability

For each pair (center, context), the probability of context word $w_O$ given center $w_I$ is:

$$P(w_O \vert w_I) = \frac{\exp(v'_{w_O} \cdot v_{w_I})}{\sum_{w=1}^{W} \exp(v'_w \cdot v_{w_I})}$$

**Logic:** The dot product $v'_{w_O} \cdot v_{w_I}$ measures compatibility; softmax converts it to a probability distribution over vocabulary of size *W*.

### Step 4 — Full Objective (Maximize Log-Likelihood)

$$L = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{-c \le j \le c, j \neq 0} \log P(w_{t+j} \vert w_t)$$

### Step 5 — Substituting Softmax

$$L = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{j, j \neq 0} \left[ v'_{w_{t+j}} \cdot v_{w_t} - \log \sum_{w=1}^{W} \exp(v'_w \cdot v_{w_t}) \right]$$

**Logic:** The first term *increases* the score of the true context word; the second term *normalizes* by competing against all vocabulary words.

### Step 6 — Optimization Challenge & Negative Sampling

The full softmax is intractable for large W (e.g., W = 1 million). We replace it with **Negative Sampling**:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{NEG}} = \log \sigma(v'_{w_O} \cdot v_{w_I}) + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \mathbb{E}_{w_i \sim P_n(w)} [\log \sigma(-v'_{w_i} \cdot v_{w_I})]$$

**Logic:** For each (center, context) pair, we sample *k* negative words $w_i$ from a unigram distribution $P_n(w) \propto U(w)^{3/4}$. The sigmoid $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$ turns scores into probabilities of being a *true* (positive) or *false* (negative) context.

## 3.3 Derivation: Self-Attention Mechanism (Transformer Backbone)

### Step 1 — Project Inputs to Q, K, V

Each input embedding $x_i$ is linearly projected using learned weight matrices:

$$Q = X W^Q, \quad K = X W^K, \quad V = X W^V$$

**Logic:** *Queries* ask "what am I looking for?", *Keys* advertise "what do I contain?", *Values* carry the actual content.

### Step 2 — Compute Compatibility Scores

$$\text{Scores} = \frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}$$

**Logic:** The dot product $QK^{\top}$ computes similarity between every query and every key. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ (the square root of key dimension) **prevents the dot products from growing too large**, which would push softmax into regions of tiny gradients.

### Step 3 — Apply Softmax Row-wise

$$\text{Attention Weights} = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)$$

**Logic:** Each row becomes a probability distribution — "how much attention should token *i* pay to token *j*?"

### Step 4 — Weighted Sum with Values

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

**Logic:** Each output vector is a *content-aware blend* of all input value vectors, weighted by attention scores.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Classical vs. Modern Approaches

| Dimension | Classical (Pre-2017) | Modern (Transfer Learning Era) |
|---|---|---|
| **Representation** | Sparse, high-dim (BoW, TF-IDF) | Dense, low-dim (BERT-base = 768-d) |
| **Context Awareness** | Static (one vector per word) | Contextual (different vectors per occurrence) |
| **Feature Engineering** | Manual, hand-crafted | Automated via self-supervised pre-training |
| **Data Requirement** | Moderate labelled data | Small labelled data (thanks to pre-training) |
| **Pre-training Data** | N/A (task-specific only) | Massive corpora (Wikipedia, CommonCrawl) |
| **Architecture** | RNN/LSTM/CNN | Transformer (Self-Attention) |
| **Parallelization** | Sequential (RNN bottleneck) | Fully parallel |
| **Long-Range Dependencies** | Limited (~200 tokens) | Long (up to 512, 1024, 2048+ tokens) |
| **Examples** | Word2Vec, GloVe, fastText | BERT, RoBERTa, GPT-2/3/4, T5, LLaMA |
| **Transfer Type** | Feature-based | Fine-tuning + Prompting |
| **Evaluation** | Accuracy, F1 | Accuracy, F1, Perplexity, BLEU, ROUGE |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — TF-IDF (KTU Exam Favorite)

**Given:**
- Corpus D contains **3 documents**:
  - $d_1$ = "the cat sat on the mat"
  - $d_2$ = "the dog sat on the log"
  - $d_3$ = "cats and dogs are animals"

**Task:** Compute TF-IDF for the word "sat" in $d_1$.

### Step 1 — Compute TF(sat, $d_1$)

$$n_{\text{sat}, d_1} = 1 \quad (\text{"sat" appears once in } d_1)$$

Total words in $d_1$ = 6.

$$\text{TF}(\text{sat}, d_1) = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.167$$

### Step 2 — Compute DF(sat, D)

"sat" appears in $d_1$ and $d_2$ only.

$$\text{DF}(\text{sat}, D) = 2$$

### Step 3 — Compute IDF(sat, D)

Using base-e logarithm (or base-10 as per KTU convention):

$$\text{IDF}(\text{sat}, D) = \log \frac{3}{1 + 2} = \log(1) = 0$$

**Interpretation:** "sat" appears in 2/3 documents, so it is *not* a discriminative term — IDF = 0 (in log base 10 or natural log with this exact ratio).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for Examiner:** In the natural log (base *e*), $\ln(1) = 0$. With base-10, $\log_{10}(1) = 0$. KTU students should clearly state the base used.

### Step 4 — Final TF-IDF

$$\text{TF-IDF}(\text{sat}, d_1, D) = 0.167 \times 0 = 0$$

**Insight:** This demonstrates that frequent-across-corpus words receive zero weight — a foundational concept in IE.

### Step 5 — Contrast with "cat" in $d_1$

- $\text{TF}(\text{cat}, d_1) = 1/6 \approx 0.167$
- "cat" appears only in $d_1$, so $\text{DF} = 1$
- $\text{IDF}(\text{cat}, D) = \log(3/2) \approx 0.405$ (natural log) or $0.176$ (base 10)
- $\text{TF-IDF}(\text{cat}, d_1, D) \approx 0.167 \times 0.405 \approx 0.068$

**Conclusion:** "cat" is far more informative for $d_1$ than "sat" — exactly the desired behavior for IE and search.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Modern NLP System Pipeline — End-to-End Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawText[Raw Unstructured Text] --> seg[Sentence Segmentation]
    seg --> tok[Tokenization]
    tok --> norm[Normalization Lowercase Contractions Unicode]
    norm --> stop[Stop Word Removal Optional]
    stop --> stem[Stemming or Lemmatization]
    stem --> clean[Cleaned Text Corpus]

    clean --> bow[Bag of Words Sparse]
    clean --> tfidf[TF IDF Weighting]
    clean --> w2v[Word2Vec Static Embeddings]
    clean --> trans[Transformer BERT GPT T5]

    bow --> ieCls[Information Extraction Layer]
    tfidf --> ieCls
    w2v --> ieCls
    trans --> ieCls

    ieCls --> ner[Named Entity Recognition]
    ieCls --> re[Relation Extraction]
    ieCls --> summ[Summarization]
    ieCls --> sa[Sentiment Analysis]
    ieCls --> qa[Question Answering]

    trans --> ft[Transfer Learning Fine Tuning]
    ft --> ieCls

    ieCls --> out[Structured Knowledge Output]
```

## 4.2 Transfer Learning Workflow — Pre-training to Fine-tuning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    corpus1[Massive Unlabelled Corpus Wikipedia Books CommonCrawl] --> pretrain[Self Supervised Pre Training MLM Next Token Prediction]
    pretrain --> pmodel[Pre Trained Language Model BERT GPT T5]
    pmodel --> freeze[Freeze Base Layers Optional]
    pmodel --> ftune[Fine Tune on Small Labelled Task Data]
    freeze --> ftune
    ftune --> finalmodel[Task Specific Model NER Sentiment QA]
    finalmodel --> deploy[Deployment Inference]
```

## 4.3 Information Extraction Sub-System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input[Embedded Text Vectors] --> tokTag[Token Level Tagger]
    tokTag --> nerOut[PER ORG LOC DATE MONEY]
    input --> pairCls[Pair Classifier]
    pairCls --> relOut[Subject Predicate Object Triples]
    input --> evtDet[Event Detector]
    evtDet --> evtOut[Event Trigger Arguments]
    input --> tmplFill[Template Slot Filler]
    tmplFill --> tmplOut[Structured Records]
```

## 4.4 Text Representation Evolution Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[One Hot Encoding 1950s] --> B[Bag of Words 1970s]
    B --> C[TF IDF 1988]
    C --> D[Word2Vec GloVe 2013]
    D --> E[ELMo Contextual 2018]
    E --> F[BERT Bidirectional 2018]
    F --> G[GPT 2 3 4 Generative 2019 Onwards]
    G --> H[T5 BART Unified 2020]
    H --> I[LLaMA Mistral Open Models 2023]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between stemming and lemmatization with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Stemming | Lemmatization |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Rule-based chopping of word suffixes to obtain a stem | Dictionary + morphological analysis to obtain the root lemma |
| **Linguistic Validity** | May produce non-words (e.g., "studi" from "studying") | Always produces a valid word (e.g., "study") |
| **Method** | Heuristic rules (Porter, Snowball) | Lookup in WordNet + POS tagging |
| **Speed** | Faster (rule-based) | Slower (dictionary lookup + POS tagging) |
| **Example** | "running" → "run", "better" → "better" (incorrect) | "running" → "run", "better" → "good" (correct) |

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is transfer learning? Why is it critical for modern NLP?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition:** Transfer learning is a paradigm where a model **pre-trained on a large source task** is reused and **fine-tuned on a smaller target task**.
- **Criticality for NLP:**
  1. **Data Efficiency** — Annotated NLP data is scarce and expensive; pre-trained models reduce the need for millions of labels.
  2. **Performance Boost** — Pre-trained language models (BERT, GPT) capture universal linguistic knowledge, lifting downstream task accuracy by 10–30%.
  3. **Reduced Training Time** — Fine-tuning a 110M-parameter BERT takes minutes compared to weeks for training from scratch.
  4. **Generalization** — Transfer learning prevents overfitting on small datasets, improving cross-domain robustness.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Explain the various stages of a modern NLP system pipeline with a neat block diagram. Discuss each stage with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With neat diagrams and mathematical formulations, explain the **TF-IDF** text representation technique. Compute the TF-IDF score for a sample corpus. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

The modern NLP pipeline has three major layers:

**Stage 1: Pre-Processing (2 marks)**
Operations applied to raw text in sequence:
1. **Tokenization** — splitting "NLP is fun" → ["NLP", "is", "fun"].
2. **Normalization** — lowercasing, expanding contractions.
3. **Stop-word removal** — discarding "is", "the", "a".
4. **Stemming/Lemmatization** — "running" → "run".
5. **Sentence segmentation** — using punctuation + heuristic rules.

**Stage 2: Text Representation (3 marks)**
- Classical: **One-Hot, BoW, TF-IDF** (sparse, high-dimensional, no context).
- Static Embeddings: **Word2Vec, GloVe** (dense, low-dim, fixed per word).
- Contextual Embeddings: **BERT, ELMo** (dense, dynamic per context).
*Example:* In "I went to the **bank** to deposit money" vs. "I sat on the **river bank**", BERT produces **two different vectors** for "bank", whereas Word2Vec produces the *same* vector.

**Stage 3: Information Extraction (2 marks)**
- **NER** — Extract entities (PERSON, ORG, LOC).
- **Relation Extraction** — Find *CEO-of*, *located-in* relations.
- **Event Extraction** — Detect events with triggers and arguments.
- **Transfer Learning Fine-Tuning** — Adjust BERT/GPT weights on labelled IE data.

*(Block diagram as in Section 4.1 to be drawn — 1 mark reserved.)*

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Mathematical Formulation (3 marks):**

$$\text{TF-IDF}(t, d, D) = \text{TF}(t,d) \times \text{IDF}(t, D)$$

Where:
- $\text{TF}(t,d) = \frac{n_{t,d}}{\sum_{w \in d} n_{w,d}}$ — relative frequency of term *t* in document *d*.
- $\text{IDF}(t, D) = \log \frac{\vert D \vert}{1 + \text{DF}(t, D)}$ — global rarity weight.
- $\vert D \vert$ = total documents, $\text{DF}(t, D)$ = number of documents containing *t*.

**Diagrammatic Workflow (2 marks):** A flowchart showing: Tokenization → Vocabulary Construction → TF Computation → IDF Computation → TF-IDF Matrix.

**Numerical Example (2 marks):**
Given:
- $d_1$ = "data science is amazing"
- $d_2$ = "machine learning is a part of data science"
- $d_3$ = "deep learning is amazing"

Compute TF-IDF for "data" in $d_1$:
- $n_{\text{data}, d_1} = 1$, total words in $d_1 = 4$, so $\text{TF} = 1/4 = 0.25$
- $\text{DF(data)} = 2$ ($d_1$ and $d_2$)
- $\text{IDF(data)} = \log(3/3) = 0$ (in base 10) or $\ln(1) = 0$
- $\text{TF-IDF(data}, d_1) = 0.25 \times 0 = 0$

*Result:* "data" loses its discriminative power because it appears in 2/3 documents.

Compute TF-IDF for "amazing" in $d_1$:
- $\text{TF} = 1/4 = 0.25$
- $\text{DF} = 2$ ($d_1, d_3$)
- $\text{IDF} = \log(3/3) = 0$
- $\text{TF-IDF} = 0$

Compute TF-IDF for "science" in $d_1$:
- $\text{TF} = 1/4 = 0.25$
- $\text{DF} = 2$ ($d_1, d_2$)
- $\text{IDF} = \log(3/3) = 0$
- $\text{TF-IDF} = 0$

Compute TF-IDF for "is" in $d_1$:
- $\text{TF} = 1/4 = 0.25$
- $\text{DF} = 3$ (all documents)
- $\text{IDF} = \log(3/4) \approx -0.125$ (natural log)
- $\text{TF-IDF} \approx -0.031$

**Insight:** Stop-words and frequent terms receive very low or negative TF-IDF scores, validating the technique.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Explain the architecture and working of **Word2Vec** with the **Skip-gram** model. Derive the objective function and discuss **Negative Sampling**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **transfer learning** in NLP? Compare **feature-based** and **fine-tuning** approaches. Explain how **BERT** is fine-tuned for a Named Entity Recognition task. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**Word2Vec Overview (1 mark):**
Word2Vec, proposed by Mikolov et al. (2013), maps each word in a vocabulary to a dense **d-dimensional vector** (typically d = 100–300). Two architectures:
- **CBOW (Continuous Bag-of-Words)** — Predicts center word from context.
- **Skip-gram** — Predicts context words from a center word.

**Skip-gram Architecture (2 marks):**
A shallow two-layer neural network:
- **Input layer:** One-hot vector of the center word $w_I$ (size = $\vert V \vert$).
- **Hidden layer:** Dense embedding $v_{w_I} = W \cdot x$ (size = d).
- **Output layer:** Softmax over vocabulary predicting each context word $w_O$.

**Objective Function Derivation (3 marks):**

Given a corpus with *T* tokens and a context window of size *c*, maximize:

$$L = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{-c \le j \le c, j \neq 0} \log P(w_{t+j} \vert w_t)$$

Using the softmax definition:

$$P(w_O \vert w_I) = \frac{\exp(v'_{w_O} \cdot v_{w_I})}{\sum_{w=1}^{W} \exp(v'_w \cdot v_{w_I})}$$

Substituting yields the expanded form:

$$L = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{j \neq 0} \left[ v'_{w_{t+j}} \cdot v_{w_t} - \log \sum_{w=1}^{W} \exp(v'_w \cdot v_{w_t}) \right]$$

**Negative Sampling Solution (1 mark):**
The denominator $\sum_{w=1}^{W} \exp(\ldots)$ is computationally expensive for large *W*. Negative Sampling transforms the problem into *k* binary classifications:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{NEG}} = \log \sigma(v'_{w_O} \cdot v_{w_I}) + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \mathbb{E}_{w_i \sim P_n(w)} [\log \sigma(-v'_{w_i} \cdot v_{w_I})]$$

where negative samples $w_i$ are drawn from $P_n(w) \propto U(w)^{3/4}$ (unigram distribution raised to 3/4 power to down-weight very frequent words).

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Transfer Learning Definition (1 mark):**
Transfer learning in NLP is the paradigm of **pre-training a language model on a massive unlabelled corpus** and then **adapting it to a downstream task** (with or without fine-tuning) using a small labelled dataset.

**Feature-Based vs. Fine-Tuning Comparison (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Feature-Based | Fine-Tuning |
|---|---|---|
| **Strategy** | Pre-trained embeddings extracted and fed as fixed features to a task-specific model | All (or most) pre-trained weights are updated during downstream training |
| **Parameters Tuned** | Only the new task head | Entire network (or top layers + task head) |
| **Computational Cost** | Low (no backprop through LM) | Higher (backprop through all layers) |
| **Performance** | Moderate | State-of-the-art |
| **Examples** | Word2Vec, GloVe, ELMo (with frozen BiLSTM) | BERT, RoBERTa, GPT-2, T5 |
| **Use Case** | When target task data is extremely small | When target task data is small-to-moderate |

**BERT Fine-Tuning for NER (3 marks):**

BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) is fine-tuned for NER as follows:

1. **Input Layer:** Tokenize sentence using **WordPiece tokenization** (e.g., "playing" → "play" + "##ing"). Prepend [CLS], append [SEP]. Add segment & positional embeddings.

2. **BERT Encoder:** Pass through *L* transformer layers (L=12 for BERT-base, 24 for BERT-large). Each token receives a **contextual embedding** $h_i \in \mathbb{R}^{768}$ (base) or $\mathbb{R}^{1024}$ (large).

3. **Classification Head:** Add a linear layer + softmax on top of each token's hidden state:

$$y_i = \text{softmax}(W \cdot h_i + b)$$

where $y_i$ is a distribution over NER tags using **BIO tagging** scheme (B-PER, I-PER, B-ORG, I-ORG, B-LOC, I-LOC, O).

4. **Loss Function:** Cross-entropy loss summed over all tokens:

$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{c=1}^{C} y_i^{(c)} \log \hat{y}_i^{(c)}$$

5. **Fine-Tuning Hyperparameters:** Learning rate $\in [2 \times 10^{-5}, 5 \times 10^{-5}]$, batch size 16 or 32, epochs 2–4. Low learning rate is critical to avoid *catastrophic forgetting* of pre-trained knowledge.

6. **Output:** A sequence of NER tags aligned with input tokens, e.g., "[B-PER, I-PER, O, B-ORG, O, B-LOC]".

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing stemming with lemmatization** — Examiners deduct 1 mark if the difference is not made explicit.
> 2. **Forgetting the "+1" smoothing in IDF** — This is a frequent 1-mark loss in numerical problems.
> 3. **Stating TF-IDF for one document only** — KTU expects the *corpus-wide* IDF component to be clearly mentioned.
> 4. **Omitting the diagram in Q.A(a)** — A 1-mark reserved for the pipeline block diagram; missing it loses an easy mark.
> 5. **Not stating the logarithm base** in TF-IDF — Always mention base *e* (natural) or base 10.
> 6. **Writing Word2Vec without distinguishing CBOW vs. Skip-gram** — Examiners expect the student to *choose* one and justify.
> 7. **Skipping the derivation steps** — Just stating the final softmax equation without derivation loses 2–3 marks.
> 8. **Ignoring the "+1" in TF-IDF denominator** and **forgetting the $\sqrt{d_k}$ factor in attention** are the two most common mathematical slips.
> 9. **In transfer-learning answers, failing to mention "pre-training corpus size" and "fine-tuning learning rate"** — these are KTU 2024 favourite sub-points.
> 10. **Mixing up "feature-based" with "fine-tuning"** transfer learning — these are *distinct* approaches, not synonyms.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:**

- **Pipeline order (must be memorized):** Raw Text → Sentence Segmentation → Tokenization → Normalization → Stop-word Removal → Stemming/Lemmatization → Representation → IE.
- **Classical vs. Modern:** Classical (BoW, TF-IDF, Word2Vec) are **static** and **shallow**; Modern (BERT, GPT, T5) are **contextual** and **deep**.
- **TF-IDF formula:** $\text{TF} \times \log(\vert D \vert / (1 + \text{DF}))$ — remember the **+1 smoothing** to avoid division by zero.
- **Word2Vec objective:** Maximize $\sum \log P(w_O \vert w_I)$ using softmax; **Negative Sampling** replaces full softmax with *k* binary classifications.
- **Cosine similarity** is the standard metric for comparing two text vectors; range $\in [-1, 1]$.
- **Transfer Learning** has two flavours: **Feature-based** (frozen embeddings) and **Fine-tuning** (all weights updated).
- **BERT** = Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers, pre-trained using **Masked Language Modeling (MLM)** and **Next Sentence Prediction (NSP)**.
- **Fine-tuning learning rate for BERT** is typically $2 \times 10^{-5}$ to $5 \times 10^{-5}$ — much lower than for training from scratch.
- **IE sub-tasks (4):** NER, Relation Extraction, Event Extraction, Template Filling.
- **Self-Attention** is the core of the Transformer; **scaled** by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to maintain stable gradients.
- **Pre-trained language models** (BERT, RoBERTa, GPT, T5, LLaMA) form the **backbone of all modern NLP** — KTU 2024 will definitely test this.
- **Evaluation metrics to memorize:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, Perplexity, BLEU, ROUGE-L.
- **Perplexity formula:** $\text{PPL}(W) = P(w_1 \ldots w_N)^{-1/N}$ — **lower is better**.
- **F1-Score** is the harmonic mean of precision and recall — preferred over accuracy on imbalanced IE datasets.
- **Modern NLP Pipeline Block Diagram** must include a **Transfer Learning fine-tuning arrow** feeding back into the IE layer (this is the 2024 scheme's defining feature).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Text Representation: Word Embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe, FastText), Transformer-Based Embeddings (BERT, GPT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT601 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Text Representation: Word Embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe, FastText), Transformer-Based Embeddings (BERT, GPT)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Text Representation?

**Text Representation** is the foundational process in Natural Language Processing (NLP) of converting symbolic, discrete language units (words, sub-words, sentences) into continuous, dense numerical vectors that machine learning models can ingest and process. The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes modern text representation into two evolutionary tiers: **Classical Word Embeddings** (static, context-independent) and **Transformer-Based Embeddings** (dynamic, context-dependent).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Computers do not understand text — they understand numbers. Text Representation is the *bridge* that translates human meaning into machine-computable geometry.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Library of Meaning"

Imagine a vast library where every word in the English language is a book, and instead of placing books on shelves alphabetically, the librarian places each book in a **multi-dimensional coordinate room** based on its *meaning*. Books with similar meanings (e.g., *king* and *queen*) are placed close together. Books with related topics (e.g., *king* and *royalty*) are placed on the same floor. 

In this analogy:
- The **room coordinates** are the **embedding vectors** $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$.
- The **distance between books** is the **semantic similarity** (often measured by cosine similarity).
- The **librarian's placement rules** are the **training algorithms** (Word2Vec, GloVe, FastText, BERT, GPT).

This is precisely what an embedding does: it maps a discrete token $w$ to a dense, real-valued vector $\mathbf{e}_w \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ where **$d$** is the embedding dimension (commonly **$d = 300$** for Word2Vec/GloVe, **$d = 768$** for BERT-base).

### 1.3 Formal Definition of Word Embedding

A **Word Embedding** is a parameterized function:

$$f: \mathcal{V} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{d}$$

where $\mathcal{V}$ is the vocabulary of size $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert$ (typically 30,000–200,000 tokens), and $d$ is the embedding dimensionality. The mapping $f$ is *learned* from a large unlabeled corpus using distributional semantics — the hypothesis that **"a word is characterized by the company it keeps"** (Firth, 1957).

### 1.4 The Five Major Models — At a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU syllabus groups these models into two families. Memorize this taxonomy for direct 3-mark definitions.

| Family | Model | Year | Key Innovation | Context Handling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Static Embedding | **Word2Vec** | 2013 (Mikolov) | Shallow neural net with CBOW / Skip-gram | Context-independent |
| Static Embedding | **GloVe** | 2014 (Pennington) | Global co-occurrence matrix factorization | Context-independent |
| Static Embedding | **FastText** | 2016 (Bojanowski) | Subword (character n-gram) information | Context-independent |
| Contextual Embedding | **BERT** | 2018 (Devlin) | Bidirectional Transformer + Masked LM | Context-dependent |
| Contextual Embedding | **GPT** | 2018 (Radford) | Autoregressive (left-to-right) Transformer | Context-dependent |

### 1.5 Why Did We Move From Static to Contextual Embeddings?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The critical limitation of Word2Vec/GloVe/FastText: each word has **exactly ONE vector**, regardless of its meaning in a sentence.

For example, the word **"bank"** in *"I sat on the river bank"* and *"I went to the bank to deposit money"* receives the **same vector** in Word2Vec — even though the meanings are completely different. BERT and GPT solve this by generating **a different vector for the same word in every different sentence**, depending on the surrounding context.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Word2Vec — Architecture and Variants

Word2Vec (Mikolov et al., 2013) is a **shallow two-layer neural network** trained to reconstruct linguistic contexts of words. It comes in two flavors:

#### 2.1.1 Continuous Bag-of-Words (CBOW)
- **Goal:** Predict the **target (center) word** from a window of surrounding **context words**.
- **Direction:** Many-to-one (context $\rightarrow$ target).
- **Best for:** Small datasets, frequent words.

#### 2.1.2 Skip-Gram
- **Goal:** Predict the surrounding **context words** given a single **target (center) word**.
- **Direction:** One-to-many (target $\rightarrow$ context).
- **Best for:** Large datasets, rare words, high-quality embeddings.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Skip-gram is mathematically symmetric to CBOW. Skip-gram treats each (target, context) pair as a new sample, so it works better for rare words. CBOW averages context, so it smooths over rare words.

### 2.2 The Word2Vec Objective Function

For a sequence of training words $w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_T$ and a context window of size $c$, the **Skip-gram objective** is to maximize the average log-probability:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{-c \leq j \leq c, \, j \neq 0} \log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t; \theta)$$

The basic softmax formulation defines the conditional probability as:

$$P(w_O \mid w_I) = \frac{\exp\left(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}\right)}{\sum_{w=1}^{\vert \mathcal{V} \vert} \exp\left(\mathbf{v}_{w}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}\right)}$$

where $\mathbf{v}_{w_I}$ and $\mathbf{v}_{w_O}$ are the **input** and **output** vector representations of the input and output words respectively.

### 2.3 Negative Sampling — Making Word2Vec Tractable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Computing the full softmax over a vocabulary of 100,000+ words is computationally prohibitive. **Negative Sampling** (NEG) approximates it by converting the multi-class problem into a binary classification problem.

The NEG objective transforms the log probability into:

$$\log \sigma\left(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}\right) + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \mathbb{E}_{w_i \sim P_n(w)} \left[ \log \sigma\left(-\mathbf{v}_{w_i}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}\right) \right]$$

where $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$ is the **sigmoid function**, and $P_n(w)$ is the **noise distribution** typically chosen as the unigram distribution raised to the 3/4 power:

$$P_n(w) = \frac{U(w)^{3/4}}{Z}$$

The exponent **3/4** was empirically shown by Mikolov to outperform the standard unigram distribution by slightly down-weighting extremely frequent words.

### 2.4 GloVe — Global Vectors for Word Representation

GloVe (Pennington et al., 2014) combines the strengths of **global matrix factorization** (like LSA) and **local context window methods** (like Word2Vec). It uses a **global co-occurrence matrix** $X$ where $X_{ij}$ counts how often word $j$ appears in the context of word $i$ within a corpus.

#### 2.4.1 Co-occurrence Probability
The **co-occurrence probability** of word $j$ given word $i$ is:

$$P_{ij} = P(j \mid i) = \frac{X_{ij}}{X_i}$$

where $X_i = \sum_k X_{ik}$ is the total number of times any word appears in the context of word $i$.

#### 2.4.2 The GloVe Objective
GloVe's least-squares objective is:

$$J = \sum_{i,j=1}^{\vert \mathcal{V} \vert} f(X_{ij}) \left( \mathbf{v}_i^{\top} \mathbf{v}_j + b_i + b_j - \log X_{ij} \right)^2$$

The **weighting function** $f(X_{ij})$ is defined as:

$$f(x) = \begin{cases} \left( \frac{x}{x_{\max}} \right)^{\alpha} & \text{if } x < x_{\max} \\ 1 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

with typical hyperparameters $x_{\max} = 100$ and $\alpha = 3/4$. This function ensures that **(a)** rare co-occurrences are not over-weighted, and **(b)** very frequent co-occurrences are capped.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it works:** GloVe is built on the **ratio-based insight** that $P_{ik}/P_{jk}$ encodes meaning better than raw probabilities. For example, $P(\text{ice} \mid \text{solid}) / P(\text{ice} \mid \text{gas})$ is large, while $P(\text{water} \mid \text{solid}) / P(\text{water} \mid \text{gas})$ is closer to 1. This ratio discrimination is the foundation of the GloVe loss.

### 2.5 FastText — Subword Embeddings

FastText (Bojanowski et al., 2016), developed at Facebook AI, extends Word2Vec by representing each word as a **bag of character n-grams** plus the whole word itself.

For a word $w$ of length $L$, we add special boundary symbols $\langle$ and $\rangle$ and extract all subword n-grams of lengths 3 to 6. The **scoring function** becomes:

$$s(w, c) = \sum_{g \in \mathcal{G}_w} \mathbf{z}_g^{\top} \mathbf{v}_c$$

where $\mathcal{G}_w$ is the set of n-grams in word $w$, and $\mathbf{z}_g$ is the vector for subword $g$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Advantage of FastText:** It can generate embeddings for **out-of-vocabulary (OOV) words** because the word vector is composed of its character n-grams, which often overlap with known words. This is critical for morphologically rich languages (e.g., Malayalam, German, Turkish).

### 2.6 Transformer-Based Embeddings — The Paradigm Shift

#### 2.6.1 The Self-Attention Mechanism
The core of BERT and GPT is the **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** (Vaswani et al., 2017):

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left( \frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} \right) V$$

where:
- $Q$ (Query) = $X W_Q$ — what information am I looking for?
- $K$ (Key) = $X W_K$ — what information do I contain?
- $V$ (Value) = $X W_V$ — what information do I actually pass forward?
- $d_k$ is the key dimension (scaling prevents softmax saturation).
- $X$ is the input embedding matrix.

The **Multi-Head Attention** runs $h$ parallel attention heads:

$$\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h) W_O$$

where each head is $\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(X W_Q^i, X W_K^i, X W_V^i)$.

#### 2.6.2 Positional Encoding
Since self-attention is **permutation-invariant** (it has no notion of word order), positional information is added via **sinusoidal positional encodings**:

$$PE_{(pos, 2i)} = \sin\left( \frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}} \right)$$

$$PE_{(pos, 2i+1)} = \cos\left( \frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}} \right)$$

### 2.7 BERT — Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers

BERT (Devlin et al., 2018) is an **encoder-only** Transformer pre-trained with two objectives:

**(a) Masked Language Modeling (MLM):** 15% of input tokens are randomly masked, and the model predicts the original token. Of these masked tokens:
- 80% are replaced with the `[MASK]` token.
- 10% are replaced with a random word.
- 10% are left unchanged.

**(b) Next Sentence Prediction (NSP):** Given two sentences A and B, the model predicts whether B is the actual next sentence after A (binary classification).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bidirectionality is BERT's defining feature.** Unlike left-to-right models, BERT's self-attention layers allow **every position to attend to every other position** (both left and right), making it deeply bidirectional.

### 2.8 GPT — Generative Pre-trained Transformer

GPT (Radford et al., 2018) is a **decoder-only** Transformer that uses **masked self-attention** (also called *causal attention*) where each position can only attend to previous positions and itself.

The GPT pre-training objective is **standard left-to-right language modeling**:

$$L_{\text{GPT}}(\theta) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_{i-1}; \theta)$$

This makes GPT fundamentally an **autoregressive** model — ideal for text generation.

### 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **This table is the single most important summary for KTU Module 2. Memorize every row.**

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Key Parameters |
|---|---|---|
| **Skip-gram Objective** | $J = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\sum_{-c \leq j \leq c, j \neq 0} \log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t)$ | $T$ = sequence length, $c$ = window |
| **Word2Vec Softmax** | $P(w_O \mid w_I) = \frac{\exp(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\top}\mathbf{v}_{w_I})}{\sum_{w} \exp(\mathbf{v}_w^{\top}\mathbf{v}_{w_I})}$ | $\mathbf{v}_w$ = word vector |
| **Sigmoid** | $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$ | Used in NEG, BERT |
| **Negative Sampling** | $\log\sigma(\mathbf{v}_O^{\top}\mathbf{v}_I) + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \mathbb{E}_{w_i \sim P_n}[\log\sigma(-\mathbf{v}_{w_i}^{\top}\mathbf{v}_I)]$ | $k$ = neg samples (5–20) |
| **Noise Distribution** | $P_n(w) = U(w)^{3/4} / Z$ | $U(w)$ = unigram freq, $3/4$ smoothing |
| **GloVe Objective** | $J = \sum_{i,j} f(X_{ij})(\mathbf{v}_i^{\top}\mathbf{v}_j + b_i + b_j - \log X_{ij})^2$ | $X_{ij}$ = co-occurrence count |
| **GloVe Weighting** | $f(x) = (x/x_{\max})^{\alpha}$ for $x < x_{\max}$, else 1 | $x_{\max}=100$, $\alpha=3/4$ |
| **FastText Score** | $s(w,c) = \sum_{g \in \mathcal{G}_w} \mathbf{z}_g^{\top}\mathbf{v}_c$ | $\mathcal{G}_w$ = subword n-grams |
| **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** | $\text{Attn}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}(QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k})V$ | $d_k$ = key dim |
| **Multi-Head Attention** | $\text{MultiHead} = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,\ldots,\text{head}_h)W_O$ | $h$ = num heads (12 in BERT-base) |
| **Sinusoidal PE (even)** | $PE_{(pos,2i)} = \sin(pos / 10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}})$ | $pos$ = position index |
| **Sinusoidal PE (odd)** | $PE_{(pos,2i+1)} = \cos(pos / 10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}})$ | $2i$ = even dim index |
| **BERT MLM Loss** | Cross-entropy over masked positions only | 15% mask rate |
| **GPT Autoregressive Loss** | $L = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(w_i \mid w_1,\ldots,w_{i-1})$ | $n$ = sequence length |
| **Cosine Similarity** | $\text{sim}(A,B) = \frac{A \cdot B}{\vert\vert A \vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert B \vert\vert}$ | Range: $[-1, 1]$ |

### 2.10 Real-World Utility

- **Word2Vec/GloVe/FastText:** Power search engines, recommendation systems, sentiment analysis, named entity recognition. FastText is used heavily for **multilingual NLP** and **low-resource languages**.
- **BERT:** The backbone of Google Search (since 2019), question answering (SQuAD benchmark), medical NLP (BioBERT), legal NLP.
- **GPT:** The engine of ChatGPT, Copilot, automated content generation, code synthesis, dialogue systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Skip-Gram Softmax Probability From First Principles

We start with the fundamental modeling assumption: each word is associated with two learned vectors — $\mathbf{v}_w$ (as a *center* word) and $\mathbf{u}_w$ (as a *context* word). The model treats the dot product $\mathbf{u}_w^{\top} \mathbf{v}_w$ as the **logit** (unnormalized score) of how likely word $w$ appears in the context of the center word $I$.

**Step 1 — Begin with the raw score (logit):**
We define the score for a word $O$ appearing in the context of word $I$ as:
$$\text{score}(w_O, w_I) = \mathbf{u}_{w_O}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}$$

**Step 2 — Apply exponentiation to make scores strictly positive:**
Exponentiating maps any real-valued score into a positive real number:
$$e^{\text{score}(w_O, w_I)} = \exp(\mathbf{u}_{w_O}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I})$$

**Step 3 — Normalize over the entire vocabulary:**
To convert scores into a valid probability distribution, we divide by the sum of exponentials of all word scores:
$$P(w_O \mid w_I) = \frac{\exp(\mathbf{u}_{w_O}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I})}{\sum_{w=1}^{\vert \mathcal{V} \vert} \exp(\mathbf{u}_w^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I})}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
The denominator acts as a **partition function** $Z(w_I) = \sum_w \exp(\mathbf{u}_w^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I})$, ensuring $\sum_w P(w \mid w_I) = 1$.

> [!NOTE]
> **The bottleneck:** Computing $Z(w_I)$ requires a sum over $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert$ words (≈ 100,000+). For a corpus of 1 billion tokens, this is computationally infeasible. This motivates Negative Sampling.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: The Negative Sampling Loss Function

**Step 1 — Restate the original problem as a binary classification:**
Instead of predicting which word is the *correct* context out of $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert$ options, we reframe the problem: **Is the word-context pair $(w, c)$ observed in the corpus, or is it a random (negative) sample?**

**Step 2 — Define the sigmoid of the true pair:**
For a *true* (observed) pair $(w_I, w_O)$, we want $P(D = 1 \mid w_I, w_O)$ to be large:
$$P(D = 1 \mid w_I, w_O) = \sigma(\mathbf{u}_{w_O}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) = \frac{1}{1 + \exp(-\mathbf{u}_{w_O}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I})}$$

**Step 3 — Define the sigmoid of the negative (noise) pair:**
For a *false* (random) pair $(w_I, w_k)$ drawn from noise distribution $P_n(w)$, we want $P(D = 0 \mid w_I, w_k)$ to be large:
$$P(D = 0 \mid w_I, w_k) = 1 - \sigma(\mathbf{u}_{w_k}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) = \sigma(-\mathbf{u}_{w_k}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I})$$

**Step 4 — Combine into a single log-likelihood objective:**
For each true pair, we include $k$ negative samples. The objective to maximize becomes:
$$J_{\text{NEG}} = \log \sigma(\mathbf{u}_{w_O}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \mathbb{E}_{w_i \sim P_n(w)} \left[ \log \sigma(-\mathbf{u}_{w_i}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) \right]$$

**Step 5 — Take the gradient with respect to $\mathbf{v}_{w_I}$ for stochastic gradient descent (SGD):**
The gradient of $\log \sigma(x) = \log(1/(1+e^{-x}))$ with respect to $x$ is:
$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x} \log \sigma(x) = \sigma(x) - 1 = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}} - 1$$

Applying the chain rule:
$$\frac{\partial J_{\text{NEG}}}{\partial \mathbf{v}_{w_I}} = [\sigma(\mathbf{u}_{w_O}^{\top}\mathbf{v}_{w_I}) - 1] \, \mathbf{u}_{w_O} + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \left[ \sigma(\mathbf{u}_{w_i}^{\top}\mathbf{v}_{w_I}) \right] \mathbf{u}_{w_i}$$

This gradient is then used in the SGD update rule:
$$\mathbf{v}_{w_I} \leftarrow \mathbf{v}_{w_I} - \eta \frac{\partial J_{\text{NEG}}}{\partial \mathbf{v}_{w_I}}$$

where $\eta$ is the learning rate. The complexity drops from $O(\vert \mathcal{V} \vert)$ per update to $O(k+1)$, where $k \in [5, 20]$ — a massive speedup.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: GloVe's Ratio-Based Insight

**Step 1 — Start with raw co-occurrence probabilities:**
For probe words $k$ (e.g., *solid*, *gas*, *water*, *fashion*) and target words $i, j$ (e.g., *ice*, *steam*):
$$P_{ik} = P(k \mid i) = \frac{X_{ik}}{X_i}, \quad P_{jk} = P(k \mid j) = \frac{X_{jk}}{X_j}$$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio $P_{ik} / P_{jk}$:**
This ratio is the key discriminative signal:
$$R_{i,j,k} = \frac{P_{ik}}{P_{jk}}$$

**Step 3 — Analyze three cases:**
- If $k$ is related to $i$ but not $j$ (e.g., $k=\text{solid}$, $i=\text{ice}$, $j=\text{steam}$): $R \gg 1$.
- If $k$ is related to $j$ but not $i$ (e.g., $k=\text{gas}$, $i=\text{ice}$, $j=\text{steam}$): $R \ll 1$.
- If $k$ is related to both or neither: $R \approx 1$.

**Step 4 — Encode this as a vector function:**
The ratio $R$ should depend on three words. We seek a function $F$:
$$F(\mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_j, \mathbf{v}_k) = \frac{P_{ik}}{P_{jk}}$$

**Step 5 — Restrict $F$ to depend only on the difference $\mathbf{v}_i - \mathbf{v}_j$:**
$$F((\mathbf{v}_i - \mathbf{v}_j)^{\top} \mathbf{v}_k) = \frac{P_{ik}}{P_{jk}}$$

**Step 6 — Require $F$ to be a homomorphism $(\mathbb{R}, +) \rightarrow (\mathbb{R}_{>0}, \times)$:**
The left side must be additive; the right side is multiplicative. The exponential satisfies this:
$$F = \exp, \quad \text{so} \quad (\mathbf{v}_i - \mathbf{v}_j)^{\top} \mathbf{v}_k = \log P_{ik} - \log P_{jk}$$

**Step 7 — Reorganize to isolate terms:**
$$(\mathbf{v}_i - \mathbf{v}_j)^{\top} \mathbf{v}_k = \mathbf{v}_i^{\top}\mathbf{v}_k - \mathbf{v}_j^{\top}\mathbf{v}_k = \log(X_{ik}) - \log(X_i) - \log(X_{jk}) + \log(X_j)$$

**Step 8 — Absorb $\log(X_i)$ and $\log(X_j)$ into bias terms $b_i, b_j$:**
Since $\log(X_i)$ depends only on $i$, it can be absorbed:
$$\mathbf{v}_i^{\top}\mathbf{v}_k + b_i + b_j = \log X_{ik}$$

This is the **exact form** of the GloVe objective's inner term, with the squared error providing robustness to noise.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Scaled Dot-Product Attention

**Step 1 — Define the raw attention scores as a dot product:**
For each query vector $\mathbf{q}_i$ and key vector $\mathbf{k}_j$:
$$\text{score}(i, j) = \mathbf{q}_i^{\top} \mathbf{k}_j$$

**Step 2 — Stack all queries and keys into matrices:**
Let $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$ and $K \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$ be the stacked matrices. The full score matrix is:
$$S = Q K^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$$

**Step 3 — Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to control variance:**
If each component of $Q$ and $K$ has mean 0 and variance 1, then:
$$\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{q}_i^{\top} \mathbf{k}_j] = 0, \quad \text{Var}[\mathbf{q}_i^{\top} \mathbf{k}_j] = d_k$$

To restore unit variance:
$$\text{scaled score} = \frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why scale?** Without $\sqrt{d_k}$, for large $d_k$ the dot products grow large in magnitude, pushing softmax into regions with tiny gradients (saturation). Scaling prevents vanishing gradients during backpropagation.

**Step 4 — Apply row-wise softmax to get attention weights:**
$$A = \text{softmax}\left( \frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} \right), \quad A_{ij} = \frac{\exp(\mathbf{q}_i^{\top} \mathbf{k}_j / \sqrt{d_k})}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \exp(\mathbf{q}_i^{\top} \mathbf{k}_k / \sqrt{d_k})}$$

**Step 5 — Weight the value matrix $V$:**
$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = A V = \text{softmax}\left( \frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} \right) V$$

Each row of the output is a **weighted sum of value vectors**, where weights come from the softmax-normalized attention scores.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Static vs. Contextual Embeddings

| Property | Word2Vec / GloVe / FastText | BERT / GPT |
|---|---|---|
| **Embedding type** | Static (one vector per word) | Contextual (different vector per context) |
| **OOV handling** | FastText: yes (via subwords); others: no | Subword tokenization (BPE, WordPiece) |
| **Context window** | Local (Word2Vec, FastText) / Global (GloVe) | Full bidirectional (BERT) / Causal (GPT) |
| **Architecture** | Shallow 2-layer neural net | Deep Transformer (12–96+ layers) |
| **Training objective** | Word co-occurrence prediction | Masked LM (BERT) / Causal LM (GPT) |
| **Dimensionality** | Typically 100–300 | Typically 768–12288 |
| **Best use case** | Fast prototyping, small datasets, retrieval | High-accuracy downstream tasks, NLU |
| **Computational cost** | Low | Very high (GPU/TPU required) |
| **Compositionality** | Limited (linear arithmetic only) | Strong (multi-layer non-linear mixing) |

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example: Word2Vec Cosine Similarity

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical Pattern:** Examiner may give two 3-D embedding vectors and ask for cosine similarity.

**Problem:** Given the embedding vectors for two words:
$$\mathbf{v}_{\text{king}} = (1.0, \ 0.5, \ -0.2), \quad \mathbf{v}_{\text{queen}} = (0.9, \ 0.6, \ 0.1)$$

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the dot product:**
$$\mathbf{v}_{\text{king}} \cdot \mathbf{v}_{\text{queen}} = (1.0)(0.9) + (0.5)(0.6) + (-0.2)(0.1) = 0.9 + 0.30 - 0.02 = 1.18$$

**Step 2 — Compute the L2 norm of each vector:**
$$\vert\vert \mathbf{v}_{\text{king}} \vert\vert = \sqrt{1.0^2 + 0.5^2 + (-0.2)^2} = \sqrt{1.00 + 0.25 + 0.04} = \sqrt{1.29} \approx 1.1358$$

$$\vert\vert \mathbf{v}_{\text{queen}} \vert\vert = \sqrt{0.9^2 + 0.6^2 + 0.1^2} = \sqrt{0.81 + 0.36 + 0.01} = \sqrt{1.18} \approx 1.0863$$

**Step 3 — Compute cosine similarity:**
$$\cos(\theta) = \frac{1.18}{1.1358 \times 1.0863} = \frac{1.18}{1.2338} \approx 0.9564$$

**Interpretation:** Cosine similarity of **0.9564** (close to 1) indicates that "king" and "queen" are semantically very similar in the embedding space, as expected.
