## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Word2Vec CBOW Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Context Word 1] --> D[Embedding Lookup]
    B[Context Word 2] --> D
    C[Context Word 3] --> D
    D --> E[Sum or Average Layer]
    E --> F[Hidden Projection Layer]
    F --> G[Softmax over Vocabulary]
    G --> H[Predicted Target Word]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Three context words (e.g., "the", "cat", "sat") are looked up in the embedding matrix, averaged in the hidden layer, and the softmax produces a probability distribution over the entire vocabulary. The target word ("on") is the ground truth.

### 4.2 Word2Vec Skip-Gram Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Target Word] --> B[Embedding Lookup]
    B --> C[Hidden Projection Layer]
    C --> D1[Softmax for Context 1]
    C --> D2[Softmax for Context 2]
    C --> D3[Softmax for Context 3]
    C --> D4[Softmax for Context 4]
    D1 --> E1[Predicted Context 1]
    D2 --> E2[Predicted Context 2]
    D3 --> E3[Predicted Context 3]
    D4 --> E4[Predicted Context 4]
```

**Reading the diagram:** A single target word ("sat") is input. The model produces a separate softmax for each context position in the window, predicting words like "the", "cat", "on", "mat".

### 4.3 GloVe Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Text Corpus] --> B[Tokenization and Vocabulary Building]
    B --> C[Sliding Context Window Scan]
    C --> D[Build Global Co-occurrence Matrix X]
    D --> E[Compute Log Co-occurrence log X sub ij]
    E --> F[Initialize Word Vectors v and Biases b]
    F --> G[Weighted Least Squares Optimization]
    G --> H[Final Word Embeddings in R power d]
```

**Reading the diagram:** GloVe differs fundamentally from Word2Vec: it first builds a *global* co-occurrence matrix over the entire corpus, then performs weighted matrix factorization. The objective is $\sum_{i,j} f(X_{ij})(\mathbf{v}_i^{\top}\mathbf{v}_j + b_i + b_j - \log X_{ij})^2$.

### 4.4 FastText Subword Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Word: where] --> B[Add Boundary Symbols]
    B --> C[where becomes less than where greater than]
    C --> D[Extract Character Trigrams]
    D --> D1[wh]
    D --> D2[her]
    D --> D3[ere]
    D --> D4[re greater than]
    D --> D5[less than wh]
    D1 --> E[Sum of Subword Embeddings]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F[Final Word Vector for where]
```

**Reading the diagram:** FastText represents "where" as a sum of its character trigrams plus the full-word embedding. This allows the model to share information between morphologically similar words (e.g., "where", "whereas", "whereby").

### 4.5 Transformer-Based Embedding Family

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transformer Architecture Family] --> B[Encoder Only Models]
    A --> C[Decoder Only Models]
    A --> D[Encoder Decoder Models]
    B --> B1[BERT]
    B --> B2[RoBERTa]
    B --> B3[DistilBERT]
    C --> C1[GPT 1]
    C --> C2[GPT 2]
    C --> C3[GPT 3 and 4]
    C --> C4[LLaMA]
    D --> D1[Original Transformer]
    D --> D2[T5]
    D --> D3[BART]
```

**Reading the diagram:** BERT and GPT are both members of the Transformer family, but they use *different halves* of the architecture. BERT uses the encoder (bidirectional attention), GPT uses the decoder (causal/masked attention).

### 4.6 BERT vs. GPT — Attention Pattern Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BERT_ATTN[BERT Bidirectional Attention]
        B1[Token 1] -->|attends to| B2[Token 2]
        B1 -->|attends to| B3[Token 3]
        B1 -->|attends to| B4[Token 4]
        B2 -->|attends to| B1
        B2 -->|attends to| B3
        B2 -->|attends to| B4
        B3 -->|attends to| B1
        B3 -->|attends to| B2
        B3 -->|attends to| B4
        B4 -->|attends to| B1
        B4 -->|attends to| B2
        B4 -->|attends to| B3
    end
    subgraph GPT_ATTN[GPT Causal Attention]
        G1[Token 1] -->|attends to self only| G1
        G2[Token 2] -->|attends to| G1
        G2 -->|attends to self| G2
        G3[Token 3] -->|attends to| G1
        G3 -->|attends to| G2
        G3 -->|attends to self| G3
        G4[Token 4] -->|attends to| G1
        G4 -->|attends to| G2
        G4 -->|attends to| G3
        G4 -->|attends to self| G4
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:** 
- In **BERT**, every token can attend to *every other token* in both directions — this is bidirectional context.
- In **GPT**, token $i$ can only attend to tokens $1, 2, \ldots, i$ — this is left-to-right (autoregressive).

### 4.7 Complete BERT Pre-training Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Sentence A and Sentence B] --> B[WordPiece Tokenization]
    B --> C[Add CLS and SEP Special Tokens]
    C --> D[Add Token Embeddings]
    C --> E[Add Segment Embeddings A or B]
    C --> F[Add Positional Embeddings]
    D --> G[Sum All Three Embeddings]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Multi Layer Transformer Encoder]
    H --> I1[Masked Language Model Head]
    H --> I2[Next Sentence Prediction Head]
    I1 --> J1[Predict Masked Tokens]
    I2 --> J2[Predict Is Next or Not Next]
```

**Reading the diagram:** BERT receives a pair of sentences, tokenizes them with WordPiece, adds three types of embeddings (token, segment, position), and feeds them through a deep Transformer. Two heads produce the MLM and NSP outputs.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** 
**Differentiate between the CBOW and Skip-gram architectures of Word2Vec with suitable diagrams.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | CBOW (Continuous Bag-of-Words) | Skip-gram |
|---|---|---|
| **Prediction Direction** | Predicts target word from surrounding context | Predicts context words from a single target word |
| **Input/Output** | Many context words $\rightarrow$ One target word | One target word $\rightarrow$ Many context words |
| **Architecture Flow** | Context window $\rightarrow$ Embedding lookup $\rightarrow$ **Average/Sum** $\rightarrow$ Softmax $\rightarrow$ Target | Target $\rightarrow$ Embedding lookup $\rightarrow$ Softmax (repeated) $\rightarrow$ Context words |
| **Performance on Rare Words** | Worse (averaging smooths out signal) | Better (each pair treated as separate sample) |
| **Training Speed** | Faster (fewer softmax computations per sample) | Slower (more softmax computations per sample) |
| **Best Use Case** | Frequent words, small datasets | Rare words, large datasets |

**(1 mark for the direction difference, 1 mark for the diagram description, 1 mark for the performance/use-case distinction.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** 
**What is Masked Language Modeling (MLM) in BERT? Why does BERT use a 15% masking rate with an 80/10/10 split?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Masked Language Modeling is a pre-training objective in BERT where, given an input token sequence, a fraction of the input tokens are randomly replaced with a `[MASK]` token (or other replacements), and the model is trained to predict the **original token ID** at those positions using the surrounding bidirectional context. Unlike left-to-right language modeling, MLM allows the model to leverage **both left and right context** simultaneously, producing deeply contextualized representations.

**Why 15% Mask Rate (1 mark):** If too few tokens are masked, training is slow and the model sees too few learning signals. If too many are masked, there is insufficient context for prediction. Empirically, 15% provides a good balance — enough supervision signal per batch without destroying sentence coherence.

**Why 80/10/10 Split (0.5 marks):**
- **80% `[MASK]`:** Standard case — model learns to predict masked tokens from context.
- **10% random word:** Prevents the model from learning that the masked token is *always* a special `[MASK]` symbol it should ignore.
- **10% unchanged:** Prevents the model from becoming overconfident in its predictions and forces it to learn robust representations for all tokens.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before You Write:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse BERT and GPT architectures.** BERT is *encoder-only* (bidirectional). GPT is *decoder-only* (autoregressive). Mixing them is a guaranteed 3-mark deduction.
> 2. **Do NOT skip the math.** When asked for a derivation, KTU examiners allocate **at least 4–5 marks** for the mathematical steps. Writing only the final formula without intermediate steps loses substantial marks.
> 3. **Do NOT write generic essays.** Every sub-question (a) and (b) carries **exactly 7 marks**. Tailor your answer length to 7 marks × sub-parts.
> 4. **Always draw the diagram.** A well-labeled CBOW/Skip-gram/Transformer diagram adds 1–2 marks even if your prose is weak.
> 5. **Mention the year and authors** of each model — this is a 1-mark "freebie" in 14-mark questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the architecture and working of the **Word2Vec Skip-gram model with Negative Sampling**. Derive the negative sampling loss function and explain the role of the noise distribution $P_n(w) = U(w)^{3/4}/Z$.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **GloVe model** in detail. Derive its objective function starting from the **ratio-based co-occurrence insight** of Pennington et al. (2014). Compare GloVe with Word2Vec.

---

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

**1. Word2Vec Skip-gram Architecture (2 marks):**

The Skip-gram model takes a single target (center) word as input and predicts the surrounding context words within a window of size $c$. The architecture is a shallow two-layer neural network:
- **Input layer:** One-hot encoded target word $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathcal{V} \vert}$.
- **Hidden layer (projection):** A weight matrix $W \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathcal{V} \vert \times d}$ produces the embedding $\mathbf{v}_w = W^{\top} \mathbf{x}$ (effectively, row lookup).
- **Output layer:** A separate weight matrix $W' \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times \vert \mathcal{V} \vert}$ produces logits for each vocabulary word, followed by softmax.

For a corpus of $T$ tokens, the **Skip-gram softmax objective** is:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{-c \leq j \leq c, \, j \neq 0} \log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t; \theta)$$

**2. The Computational Bottleneck (0.5 mark):**

The full softmax requires summing over $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert \approx 10^5$ words for every context prediction. For a corpus of $10^9$ tokens with window $c = 5$, this is $5 \times 10^{14}$ operations — completely infeasible.

**3. Negative Sampling Reformulation (2.5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Reframe the problem. Instead of a $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert$-way classification, treat it as binary classification: *Is this (target, context) pair real or noise?*

**Step 2:** For each true pair $(w_I, w_O)$ drawn from the corpus, sample $k$ negative words $w_1, \ldots, w_k$ from a noise distribution $P_n(w)$.

**Step 3:** The negative sampling objective to **maximize** is:

$$J_{\text{NEG}} = \log \sigma(\mathbf{u}_{w_O}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \mathbb{E}_{w_i \sim P_n(w)} \left[ \log \sigma(-\mathbf{u}_{w_i}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) \right]$$

The first term pushes the true pair's score high; the second term pushes the negative pairs' scores low.

**4. Role of $P_n(w) = U(w)^{3/4}/Z$ (1 mark):**

$P_n(w)$ is the **unigram distribution raised to the 3/4 power**, where $U(w)$ is the unigram frequency of word $w$ in the corpus, and $Z = \sum_w U(w)^{3/4}$ is the normalization constant. The exponent **3/4**:
- **Down-weights extremely common words** (e.g., "the", "a", "of") so they are not oversampled as negatives.
- **Slightly up-weights rare words** compared to pure unigram sampling, giving them more chance to be sampled as negatives.
- Empirically, the 3/4 power was found by Mikolov et al. to give **better embeddings** than $P(w) = U(w)$ or $U(w)^1$.

**5. SGD Update (1 mark):**

The gradient of $J_{\text{NEG}}$ with respect to the input embedding $\mathbf{v}_{w_I}$ is:

$$\frac{\partial J_{\text{NEG}}}{\partial \mathbf{v}_{w_I}} = [\sigma(\mathbf{u}_{w_O}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) - 1] \, \mathbf{u}_{w_O} + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma(\mathbf{u}_{w_i}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) \, \mathbf{u}_{w_i}$$

The complexity per update is now $O(k+1)$ instead of $O(\vert \mathcal{V} \vert)$, yielding a **50×–1000× speedup** depending on vocabulary size.

---

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

**1. Motivation: From Local to Global (1 mark):**

Word2Vec uses a *local context window* and trains one sample at a time. While effective, it does not directly leverage the **global corpus statistics** — for example, the overall co-occurrence count $X_{ij}$ between words $i$ and $j$. GloVe (Global Vectors) by Pennington et al. (2014) bridges this gap by combining **local context methods** (Word2Vec-style) with **global matrix factorization** (LSA-style).

**2. Building the Co-occurrence Matrix (1 mark):**

Given a corpus, define $X_{ij}$ = the number of times word $j$ appears in the context of word $i$ within a predefined window. The marginal is $X_i = \sum_k X_{ik}$. The **co-occurrence probability** is:

$$P_{ij} = P(j \mid i) = \frac{X_{ij}}{X_i}$$

**3. The Ratio-Based Insight (2 marks):**

The core insight of GloVe is that **ratios of co-occurrence probabilities** encode meaning better than raw probabilities. For example, with $k = \text{solid}$:

| | $P(k \mid \text{ice})$ | $P(k \mid \text{steam})$ | Ratio $P(k\mid\text{ice})/P(k\mid\text{steam})$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $k = \text{solid}$ | 0.00019 | 0.00002 | **9.5** (large) |
| $k = \text{gas}$ | 0.00003 | 0.00075 | **0.04** (small) |
| $k = \text{water}$ | 0.003 | 0.002 | **1.5** (close to 1) |
| $k = \text{fashion}$ | 0.00002 | 0.00002 | **1.0** (irrelevant) |

The ratio discriminates relevant words (solid, gas) from irrelevant ones (water, fashion) much more sharply than raw probabilities.

**4. Derivation of the GloVe Objective (2 marks):**

We seek $F(\mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_j, \mathbf{v}_k, b) = P_{ik}/P_{jk}$.

**Step 1:** Restrict $F$ to depend only on the difference: $F((\mathbf{v}_i - \mathbf{v}_j)^{\top}\mathbf{v}_k) = P_{ik}/P_{jk}$.

**Step 2:** Require homomorphism from $(\mathbb{R},+)$ to $(\mathbb{R}_{>0},\times)$: $F = \exp$, giving $(\mathbf{v}_i - \mathbf{v}_j)^{\top}\mathbf{v}_k = \log P_{ik} - \log P_{jk}$.

**Step 3:** Expand and rearrange:
$$\mathbf{v}_i^{\top}\mathbf{v}_k - \mathbf{v}_j^{\top}\mathbf{v}_k = \log X_{ik} - \log X_i - \log X_{jk} + \log X_j$$

**Step 4:** Absorb $\log X_i$ into a bias $b_i$ and $\log X_j$ into $b_j$, swap notation ($i \leftrightarrow k$):
$$\mathbf{v}_i^{\top}\mathbf{v}_j + b_i + b_j = \log X_{ij}$$

**Step 5:** The least-squares objective with a weighting function:
$$J = \sum_{i,j} f(X_{ij}) \left( \mathbf{v}_i^{\top} \mathbf{v}_j + b_i + b_j - \log X_{ij} \right)^2$$

where $f(x) = (x/x_{\max})^{\alpha}$ for $x < x_{\max}$, else 1, with $x_{\max} = 100$, $\alpha = 3/4$.

**5. Comparison with Word2Vec (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Word2Vec | GloVe |
|---|---|---|
| **Training data** | Local context window, sample-by-sample | Global co-occurrence matrix |
| **Algorithm** | Stochastic gradient descent | Weighted least squares / AdaGrad |
| **Theoretical basis** | Implicit (distributional hypothesis) | Explicit (ratio-based derivation) |
| **Parallelization** | Naturally parallel | Trivially parallel (matrix factorization) |
| **Performance** | Strong on analogy, similarity | Often outperforms Word2Vec on word similarity benchmarks (e.g., WordSim-353) |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Transformer self-attention mechanism** in detail. Derive the **scaled dot-product attention** formula and explain why the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling factor is essential.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **architecture of BERT** in detail, including its pre-training objectives (**MLM** and **NSP**). Compare BERT with **GPT**, highlighting their architectural and objective differences.

---

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

**1. Motivation: Limitations of RNNs/CNNs (1 mark):**

Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) process sequences token-by-token, leading to:
- **Sequential computation** (no parallelization across positions).
- **Long-range dependency decay** (vanishing gradients over long sequences).
- **No direct path** between distant tokens.

The Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) eliminates recurrence entirely, allowing every position to directly attend to every other position in $O(1)$ path length.

**2. The Attention Mechanism (2 marks):**

For each token $i$, we compute three vectors from its input embedding $\mathbf{x}_i$:
- **Query** $\mathbf{q}_i = \mathbf{x}_i W_Q$ (what am I looking for?)
- **Key** $\mathbf{k}_i = \mathbf{x}_i W_K$ (what do I represent?)
- **Value** $\mathbf{v}_i = \mathbf{x}_i W_V$ (what information do I pass forward?)

where $W_Q, W_K \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k}$ and $W_V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_v}$ are learnable projection matrices.

For a sequence of $n$ tokens, we stack these into matrices $Q, K, V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$.

**3. Derivation of Scaled Dot-Product Attention (2.5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Raw attention scores:**
Each query $\mathbf{q}_i$ is compared to every key $\mathbf{k}_j$ via dot product:
$$S_{ij} = \mathbf{q}_i^{\top} \mathbf{k}_j \quad \text{(in matrix form: } S = Q K^{\top}\text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Variance analysis:**
Assuming $\mathbf{q}$ and $\mathbf{k}$ components are i.i.d. with mean 0 and variance 1:
$$\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{q}_i^{\top}\mathbf{k}_j] = 0, \quad \text{Var}[\mathbf{q}_i^{\top}\mathbf{k}_j] = d_k$$

As $d_k$ grows (e.g., $d_k = 64$ in BERT), the variance of dot products grows proportionally.

**Step 3 — Scaling to restore unit variance:**
Divide by $\sqrt{d_k}$:
$$\tilde{S} = \frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}$$

**Step 4 — Why scaling is essential (1 mark):**
Without scaling, large $d_k$ produces large-magnitude logits. The softmax function $\text{softmax}(x_i) = e^{x_i}/\sum_j e^{x_j}$ saturates when one logit dominates — gradients become vanishingly small. The $\sqrt{d_k}$ factor keeps the logits in a regime where softmax has healthy gradients.

**Step 5 — Softmax + value weighting:**
$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left( \frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} \right) V$$

The result is a matrix $\in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$ where each row is a context-aware representation.

**4. Multi-Head Attention (0.5 mark):**

Instead of one attention function, run $h$ parallel attention heads, each with its own $W_Q^i, W_K^i, W_V^i$, then concatenate:
$$\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h) W_O$$

This allows the model to attend to information from **different representation subspaces** at different positions.

---

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

**1. BERT Architecture Overview (1.5 marks):**

BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers), introduced by Devlin et al. (2018) at Google, is a **multi-layer bidirectional Transformer encoder**. Two versions were released:
- **BERT-base:** 12 layers, 768 hidden dim, 12 attention heads, 110M parameters.
- **BERT-large:** 24 layers, 1024 hidden dim, 16 attention heads, 340M parameters.

The input representation is a **sum of three embeddings**:
$$\text{Input Embedding} = \text{Token Embedding} + \text{Segment Embedding} + \text{Positional Embedding}$$

Tokens use **WordPiece tokenization** (a subword segmentation algorithm). A special `[CLS]` token is prepended for classification, and `[SEP]` separates sentence pairs.

**2. Pre-training Objective 1: Masked Language Modeling (MLM) (2 marks):**

In MLM, **15% of the input tokens are randomly selected** for prediction. Of these selected tokens:
- **80%** are replaced with the `[MASK]` special token.
- **10%** are replaced with a random word.
- **10%** are left unchanged.

The model predicts the **original token ID** at each masked position using a classification head over the vocabulary. The loss is **cross-entropy** computed **only on the masked positions**. This bidirectional prediction forces the model to build deep contextual understanding — unlike GPT, which can only see left context.

**3. Pre-training Objective 2: Next Sentence Prediction (NSP) (1.5 marks):**

NSP is a binary classification task: given two sentences A and B, predict whether B is the actual sentence that follows A in the corpus.
- 50% of training pairs are *true* consecutive sentences (label = `IsNext`).
- 50% are *random* sentence pairs (label = `NotNext`).

The prediction is made from the final hidden state of the `[CLS]` token, passed through a classification head. Although later studies (e.g., RoBERTa) showed NSP contributes little, the original BERT used it to improve sentence-pair tasks like Natural Language Inference (NLI) and Question Answering (QA).

**4. BERT vs GPT: A Detailed Comparison (2 marks):**

| Aspect | BERT | GPT |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Encoder-only (bidirectional self-attention) | Decoder-only (masked/causal self-attention) |
| **Attention Pattern** | Every token attends to every other token | Each token attends only to previous tokens and itself |
| **Pre-training Objective** | Masked LM (15% mask rate) + NSP | Left-to-right autoregressive LM |
| **Direction of Context** | Bidirectional (sees left + right context) | Unidirectional (sees only left context) |
| **Primary Use Case** | NLU tasks: classification, NER, QA, sentiment | Text generation, completion, dialogue |
| **Output Head** | Token-level (MLM) + sentence-level (NSP) | Token-level (next-token prediction) |
| **Position Encoding** | Learned positional embeddings (BERT) / sinusoidal (original Transformer) | Learned positional embeddings |
| **Released By** | Google (2018) | OpenAI (2018, then GPT-2 in 2019, GPT-3 in 2020) |
| **Parameters (original)** | BERT-base: 110M; BERT-large: 340M | GPT-1: 117M; GPT-2: 1.5B; GPT-3: 175B |
| **Fine-tuning Paradigm** | Add task-specific head on top of `[CLS]` or token outputs | Add task-specific head, often use in-context learning (zero/few-shot) |
| **Tokenization** | WordPiece (≈30K vocab) | BPE (Byte Pair Encoding, ≈50K vocab) |

**5. Key Takeaway (0.5 mark):**

BERT is best for **understanding** tasks (NLU), GPT is best for **generating** tasks (NLG). Modern LLMs (e.g., GPT-4, LLaMA, Mistral) are decoder-only because scaling has shown autoregressive generation to be more powerful for general-purpose AI.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Text Representation** converts discrete text into continuous numerical vectors. Two families: **static (Word2Vec, GloVe, FastText)** and **contextual (BERT, GPT)**.
- **Word2Vec** has two variants: **CBOW** (context $\rightarrow$ target) and **Skip-gram** (target $\rightarrow$ context). Skip-gram is preferred for rare words; CBOW is faster.
- The **Word2Vec softmax** is the standard formulation but is computationally infeasible for large vocabularies.
- **Negative Sampling** replaces softmax with binary classification: true pairs vs. $k$ negative samples. Uses noise distribution $P_n(w) = U(w)^{3/4}/Z$.
- **GloVe** uses **global co-occurrence statistics** and minimizes a **weighted least-squares** objective. The derivation starts from the **ratio of co-occurrence probabilities**.
- **FastText** represents words as sums of **character n-grams** (typically 3–6), enabling **OOV handling** and sharing across morphologically related words.
- **Self-attention** allows every position to attend to every other position in $O(1)$ path length. The formula is $\text{softmax}(QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k})V$.
- The **$\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling** prevents softmax saturation by keeping dot product variance at unity.
- **Multi-head attention** runs $h$ parallel attention projections to capture different subspace relationships.
- **Positional encodings** (sinusoidal) inject word order into the otherwise permutation-invariant attention mechanism.
- **BERT** is **encoder-only**, **bidirectional**, pre-trained with **MLM (15% mask, 80/10/10 split)** and **NSP**.
- **GPT** is **decoder-only**, **autoregressive**, pre-trained with **left-to-right language modeling** (causal attention mask).
- The fundamental difference: **BERT sees left + right context, GPT sees only left context**.
- **Cosine similarity** in $[-1, 1]$ is the standard metric for measuring semantic similarity between two embedding vectors.
- Typical embedding dimensions: Word2Vec/GloVe/FastText use $d \in [100, 300]$; BERT-base uses $d = 768$; GPT-3 uses $d = 12288$.
- The exponent **3/4** appears in both **negative sampling noise distribution** and **GloVe weighting function** — a recurring "magic" value in NLP.
- **Authors to remember:** Mikolov (Word2Vec, 2013), Pennington (GloVe, 2014), Bojanowski (FastText, 2016), Devlin (BERT, 2018), Radford (GPT, 2018), Vaswani (Transformer, 2017).
- **No programming code** is required for KTU theory exams — focus on **mathematical derivations, architectural diagrams, and comparative tables**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Feature Engineering Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT601 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Advanced Feature Engineering Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Feature Engineering** in Natural Language Processing (NLP) is the systematic process of transforming raw, unstructured textual data into a structured, numerical representation (a *feature vector* or *embedding*) that machine learning models can consume. **Advanced Feature Engineering Techniques** specifically refer to statistical, distributional, and neural methods that capture *semantic*, *syntactic*, and *contextual* relationships between words and documents — moving beyond the naive **Bag-of-Words (BoW)** and **One-Hot Encoding** paradigms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of PCCMT601 expects mastery of *statistical features* (TF-IDF, n-grams, PMI) and *distributional semantics* (Word2Vec, GloVe, FastText, contextual embeddings). Examiners frequently test the mathematical foundations of these techniques.

### 1.2 Why "Advanced"?

Traditional BoW loses two critical pieces of information:
1. **Word Importance Discrimination** — every word is treated as equally important.
2. **Semantic Similarity** — the words *"king"* and *"queen"* are as different as *"king"* and *"banana"* under one-hot encoding.

Advanced feature engineering addresses both limitations by producing **dense, low-dimensional, semantically meaningful** vector spaces.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Library Indexing System:**
> Imagine a massive library with no catalog. You have 10 million books (documents) and 500,000 unique words (vocabulary). BoW is like counting every book's word frequency on a giant spreadsheet — it works, but it's huge and treats the word *"the"* (which appears in 99% of books) as equally important as the word *"photosynthesis"* (which appears in 0.01%).
> 
> **TF-IDF** is like a smart librarian who *down-weights* common words and *up-weights* rare, informative ones. **Word Embeddings** go further: they don't just count — they *position* every word in a "meaning-space" where similar words cluster together, just as a librarian might shelve all physics books near each other, regardless of how often the word "physics" appears.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Anchors

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Corpus** | The full collection of documents $\mathcal{D} = \{d_1, d_2, \dots, d_N\}$ |
| **Vocabulary** $V$ | Set of unique tokens in the corpus, $\vert V \vert$ = vocabulary size |
| **Document Vector** | A numerical vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ representing a document |
| **Word Vector / Embedding** | A dense vector $\mathbf{v}_w \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$ (with $k \ll \vert V \vert$) for word $w$ |
| **Distributional Hypothesis** | *"Words that occur in similar contexts have similar meanings."* (Firth, 1957) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Advanced Feature Engineering Techniques

```
Advanced Feature Engineering in NLP
├── Statistical / Frequency-Based
│   ├── TF-IDF (Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency)
│   ├── Sublinear TF, Log-normalization
│   ├── Pointwise Mutual Information (PMI)
│   └── Chi-Square (χ²) Feature Selection
├── Distributional / Neural
│   ├── Word2Vec (CBOW & Skip-gram)
│   ├── GloVe (Global Vectors)
│   ├── FastText (Subword Embeddings)
│   └── Contextual (ELMo, BERT)
└── Higher-Order / Hybrid
    ├── N-gram features
    ├── POS-tag features
    ├── Topic features (LDA)
    └── Dimensionality reduction (SVD, t-SNE)
```

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Step 1 — Term Frequency (TF):** Captures *local* importance. A word appearing 10 times in a 100-word document is more likely to be central to that document's topic than one appearing once.
- **Step 2 — Inverse Document Frequency (IDF):** Captures *global* rarity. If *"the"* appears in 1,000,000 of 1,000,001 documents, it carries almost no discriminative power; we must penalize it.
- **Step 3 — Distributional Embeddings:** Move from sparse ($d = \vert V \vert$, often $10^5$–$10^6$) to dense ($d = 50$–$300$) representations that encode meaning geometrically.
- **Step 4 — Contextual Embeddings:** Solve the *polysemy* problem — a static embedding gives *"bank"* one vector, but context-aware models give it different vectors in *"river bank"* vs. *"money bank"*.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** you must memorize for Module 2 derivations and numerical problems. No vertical pipe symbols `|` are used inside cells to preserve markdown table integrity — absolute value is denoted as `\vert \cdot \vert`.

| # | Technique | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Raw Term Frequency | $\mathrm{tf}(t, d) = f_{t,d}$ | $f_{t,d}$ = count of term $t$ in document $d$ | Baseline count |
| 2 | Log-normalized TF | $\mathrm{tf}_{\log}(t,d) = 1 + \log f_{t,d}$ | $f_{t,d} \geq 1$ | Dampens high-frequency dominance |
| 3 | Boolean TF | $\mathrm{tf}_{\text{bool}}(t,d) = \mathbb{1}[f_{t,d} > 0]$ | Indicator $\in \{0,1\}$ | Used in some IR systems |
| 4 | Inverse Document Frequency | $\mathrm{idf}(t) = \log \dfrac{N}{1 + \mathrm{df}(t)}$ | $N$ = total docs, $\mathrm{df}(t)$ = docs containing $t$ | Smoothing `+1` prevents division by zero |
| 5 | TF-IDF | $\mathrm{tfidf}(t, d) = \mathrm{tf}(t, d) \cdot \mathrm{idf}(t)$ | Scalar weight | Most common form |
| 6 | Smoothed IDF (sklearn) | $\mathrm{idf}(t) = \log \dfrac{N+1}{\mathrm{df}(t)+1} + 1$ | Adds `+1` inside & outside | Non-negative |
| 7 | Pointwise Mutual Information | $\mathrm{PMI}(w_1, w_2) = \log \dfrac{P(w_1, w_2)}{P(w_1)\,P(w_2)}$ | Probabilities $\in [0,1]$ | Positive PMI (PPMI) clips negatives to 0 |
| 8 | Skip-gram Objective | $J(\theta) = -\dfrac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\sum_{-c \leq j \leq c,\, j \neq 0} \log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t;\theta)$ | $c$ = context window, $T$ = corpus length | Mikolov et al., 2013 |
| 9 | Softmax (Full) | $P(w_O \mid w_I) = \dfrac{\exp(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\prime\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I})}{\sum_{w=1}^{V} \exp(\mathbf{v}_{w}^{\prime\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I})}$ | $\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{v}'$ = input/output vectors | Computationally expensive |
| 10 | Negative Sampling Loss | $\log \sigma(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\prime\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) + \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{E}_{w_k \sim P_n} \log \sigma(-\mathbf{v}_{w_k}^{\prime\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I})$ | $K$ = neg samples, $\sigma$ = sigmoid | Practical replacement for softmax |
| 11 | GloVe Objective | $J = \sum_{i,j=1}^{V} f(X_{ij})\left(\mathbf{v}_i^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{v}}_j + b_i + \tilde{b}_j - \log X_{ij}\right)^{2}$ | $X_{ij}$ = co-occurrence count | Pennington et al., 2014 |
| 12 | GloVe Weighting | $f(x) = \begin{cases} (x/x_{\max})^{\alpha} & x < x_{\max} \\ 1 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | $\alpha = 0.75$, $x_{\max} = 100$ typical | Caps influence of very common pairs |
| 13 | Cosine Similarity | $\mathrm{cos}(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}) = \dfrac{\mathbf{u}^{\top}\mathbf{v}}{\Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert_2 \, \Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert_2}$ | $\Vert \cdot \Vert_2$ = L2-norm, output $\in [-1, 1]$ | Standard word-similarity metric |
| 14 | CBOW Objective | $J = -\dfrac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P(w_t \mid w_{t-c}, \dots, w_{t-1}, w_{t+1}, \dots, w_{t+c})$ | Predicts center from context | Mirror of Skip-gram |
| 15 | Negative Sampling Dist. | $P_n(w) = \dfrac{U(w)^{3/4}}{Z}$ | $U(w)$ = unigram freq, $Z$ = normalizer | Heuristic for word sampling |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search Engines (Google, Bing):** Use TF-IDF and BM25 (TF-IDF's successor) for first-stage document ranking.
- **Recommendation Systems:** Use Word2Vec/GloVe to find semantically similar products/articles.
- **Chatbots & Translation:** Use contextual embeddings (BERT) for intent classification and cross-lingual transfer.
- **Bio-informatics & Legal NLP:** Domain-specific TF-IDF + chi-square feature selection on millions of clinical/legal documents.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example: TF-IDF Computation

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **classic KTU-style numerical**. Given a tiny corpus, you must compute the full TF-IDF matrix by hand.

**Corpus (3 documents):**
- $d_1$: *"the cat sat on the mat"*
- $d_2$: *"the dog sat on the log"*
- $d_3$: *"cats and dogs are pets"*

**Step 1 — Build Vocabulary (lowercased, stopwords retained for pedagogy):**
$$V = \{\text{the}, \text{cat}, \text{cat}\text{s}, \text{sat}, \text{on}, \text{mat}, \text{dog}, \text{log}, \text{and}, \text{are}, \text{pets}\}$$
$$\vert V \vert = 11, \quad N = 3$$

**Step 2 — Compute Term Frequencies (raw counts $f_{t,d}$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{tf}(\text{the}, d_1) &= 2, & \mathrm{tf}(\text{the}, d_2) &= 2, & \mathrm{tf}(\text{the}, d_3) &= 0 \\
\mathrm{tf}(\text{cat}, d_1) &= 1, & \mathrm{tf}(\text{cat}, d_2) &= 0, & \mathrm{tf}(\text{cat}, d_3) &= 0 \\
\mathrm{tf}(\text{cat}\text{s}, d_1) &= 0, & \mathrm{tf}(\text{cat}\text{s}, d_2) &= 0, & \mathrm{tf}(\text{cat}\text{s}, d_3) &= 1 \\
\mathrm{tf}(\text{dog}, d_1) &= 0, & \mathrm{tf}(\text{dog}, d_2) &= 1, & \mathrm{tf}(\text{dog}, d_3) &= 1 \\
\mathrm{tf}(\text{pets}, d_1) &= 0, & \mathrm{tf}(\text{pets}, d_2) &= 0, & \mathrm{tf}(\text{pets}, d_3) &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Document Frequencies $\mathrm{df}(t)$** (number of documents containing $t$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{df}(\text{the}) &= 2, \quad \mathrm{df}(\text{cat}) = 1, \quad \mathrm{df}(\text{cat}\text{s}) = 1 \\
\mathrm{df}(\text{dog}) &= 2, \quad \mathrm{df}(\text{pets}) = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute IDF using $\mathrm{idf}(t) = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{N}{\mathrm{df}(t)}\right)$** (natural log also acceptable):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{idf}(\text{the}) &= \log_{10}(3/2) = \log_{10}(1.5) \approx 0.1761 \\
\mathrm{idf}(\text{cat}) &= \log_{10}(3/1) = \log_{10}(3) \approx 0.4771 \\
\mathrm{idf}(\text{cat}\text{s}) &= \log_{10}(3/1) \approx 0.4771 \\
\mathrm{idf}(\text{dog}) &= \log_{10}(3/2) \approx 0.1761 \\
\mathrm{idf}(\text{pets}) &= \log_{10}(3/1) \approx 0.4771
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logic of Step 4:** Common words like *"the"* (df=2) get a *small* IDF, while rare, topic-bearing words like *"cat"*, *"pets"* (df=1) get a *large* IDF. This is the **discriminative power** the BoW model lacked.

**Step 5 — Compute TF-IDF = tf $\times$ idf** (selected entries):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{tfidf}(\text{the}, d_1) &= 2 \times 0.1761 = 0.3522 \\
\mathrm{tfidf}(\text{cat}, d_1) &= 1 \times 0.4771 = 0.4771 \\
\mathrm{tfidf}(\text{cat}\text{s}, d_3) &= 1 \times 0.4771 = 0.4771 \\
\mathrm{tfidf}(\text{dog}, d_2) &= 1 \times 0.1761 = 0.1761 \\
\mathrm{tfidf}(\text{pets}, d_3) &= 1 \times 0.4771 = 0.4771
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Final TF-IDF Vectors** (using only the 5 illustrative terms above for compactness):

$$
\mathbf{x}_{d_1} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.3522 \\ 0.4771 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{x}_{d_2} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.3522 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0.1761 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{x}_{d_3} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0.4771 \\ 0.1761 \\ 0.4771 \end{pmatrix}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Skip-gram Negative Sampling (SGNS) Loss

The naive softmax requires summing over the entire vocabulary $V$ (often $>10^5$), which is infeasible. **Negative Sampling** reformulates the problem as binary classification.

**Step 1 — Original Skip-gram Softmax Objective:**

$$
P(w_O \mid w_I) = \dfrac{\exp(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\prime\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I})}{\sum_{w=1}^{V} \exp(\mathbf{v}_{w}^{\prime\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I})}
$$

**Step 2 — Log-Likelihood of One Positive Pair $(w_I, w_O)$:**

$$
\log P(w_O \mid w_I) = \mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\prime\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I} - \log \sum_{w=1}^{V} \exp(\mathbf{v}_{w}^{\prime\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I})
$$

**Step 3 — Reformulate as Logistic Regression** with $K$ negative samples $w_k$ drawn from noise distribution $P_n(w)$:

$$
J_{\text{SGNS}}(w_I, w_O) = \log \sigma(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\prime\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) + \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{E}_{w_k \sim P_n}\left[\log \sigma(-\mathbf{v}_{w_k}^{\prime\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I})\right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of Step 3:** The first term pushes the *true* context word's vector close to the input word's vector (high $\sigma$ score). The second term pushes $K$ *random* "negative" words' vectors *away* from the input word. With $K=5$–$20$ instead of summing over $V$, the cost drops from $O(V)$ to $O(K)$.

**Step 4 — Gradient w.r.t. $\mathbf{v}_{w_I}$** (used in backpropagation):

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{v}_{w_I}} = \left[\sigma(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\prime\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) - 1\right]\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\prime} + \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sigma(\mathbf{v}_{w_k}^{\prime\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I})\, \mathbf{v}_{w_k}^{\prime}
$$

**Step 5 — Update Rule** (with learning rate $\eta$):

$$
\mathbf{v}_{w_I}^{(t+1)} = \mathbf{v}_{w_I}^{(t)} - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{v}_{w_I}}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: GloVe Objective from Co-occurrence Ratios

**Step 1 — Insight from Co-occurrence Ratios:** For words $i, j, k$ with co-occurrence probabilities $P_{ik} = X_{ik}/X_i$:

$$
F(\mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_j, \tilde{\mathbf{v}}_k) = \dfrac{P_{ik}}{P_{jk}}
$$

**Step 2 — Encode Ratios in Vector Differences** (linear algebra requirement):

$$
F((\mathbf{v}_i - \mathbf{v}_j)^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{v}}_k) = \dfrac{P_{ik}}{P_{jk}}
$$

**Step 3 — Homomorphism with Probability** (for symmetry):

$$
F\!\left((\mathbf{v}_i - \mathbf{v}_j)^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{v}}_k\right) = \dfrac{F(\mathbf{v}_i^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{v}}_k)}{F(\mathbf{v}_j^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{v}}_k)} \implies F(x) = \exp(x)
$$

**Step 4 — Simplified Log-bilinear Form:**

$$
\mathbf{v}_i^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{v}}_k = \log P_{ik} = \log X_{ik} - \log X_i
$$

**Step 5 — Add Biases $b_i, \tilde{b}_k$ and absorb $\log X_i$:**

$$
\mathbf{v}_i^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{v}}_k + b_i + \tilde{b}_k = \log X_{ik}
$$

**Step 6 — Weighted Least-Squares Loss with $f(X_{ij})$:**

$$
J = \sum_{i,j=1}^{V} f(X_{ij})\left(\mathbf{v}_i^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{v}}_j + b_i + \tilde{b}_j - \log X_{ij}\right)^{2}
$$

### 3.4 PMI Derivation (Pointwise Mutual Information)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{PMI}(w_1, w_2) &= \log \dfrac{P(w_1, w_2)}{P(w_1)\,P(w_2)} \\[4pt]
&= \log \dfrac{\dfrac{\#(w_1, w_2)}{N}}{\dfrac{\#(w_1)}{N} \cdot \dfrac{\#(w_2)}{N}} \\[4pt]
&= \log \dfrac{\#(w_1, w_2) \cdot N}{\#(w_1) \cdot \#(w_2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\#(w_1)$ is the count of $w_1$ in the corpus, $\#(w_1, w_2)$ is the joint count, and $N$ is total word tokens.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Positive PMI (PPMI):** Since raw PMI can be negative (when words co-occur *less* than chance), we clip: $\mathrm{PPMI}(w_1,w_2) = \max(0, \mathrm{PMI}(w_1,w_2))$. KTU examiners often test this definition.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level NLP Feature Engineering Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Text Corpus] --> B[Tokenization and Lowercasing]
    B --> C[Stopword Removal and Stemming or Lemmatization]
    C --> D{Feature Engineering Strategy}
    D --> E1[Statistical Features: TF-IDF, PMI, Chi-Square]
    D --> E2[Distributional Features: Word2Vec, GloVe, FastText]
    D --> E3[Contextual Features: ELMo, BERT, GPT]
    E1 --> F1[Sparse High Dimensional Vector]
    E2 --> F2[Dense Low Dimensional Vector]
    E3 --> F3[Dynamic Context Aware Vector]
    F1 --> G[ML or DL Model]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Downstream Task: Classification, NER, Sentiment]
```

### 4.2 Word2Vec Architectures: CBOW vs. Skip-gram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CBOW
        direction LR
        C1[Context w t-2] --> N1[Input Layer]
        C2[Context w t-1] --> N1
        C3[Context w t+1] --> N1
        C4[Context w t+2] --> N1
        N1 --> H1[Hidden Layer: Average or Sum]
        H1 --> O1[Output: Predict Center w t]
    end

    subgraph SKIP
        direction LR
        S1[Input Center w t] --> H2[Hidden Layer]
        H2 --> P1[Predict w t-2]
        H2 --> P2[Predict w t-1]
        H2 --> P3[Predict w t+1]
        H2 --> P4[Predict w t+2]
    end
```

### 4.3 Comparison Block Diagram: Feature Families

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Feature Families Comparison] --> Q1[Sparse Statistical: TF-IDF]
    P --> Q2[Dense Static: Word2Vec and GloVe]
    P --> Q3[Dense Subword: FastText]
    P --> Q4[Dense Contextual: BERT]

    Q1 --> R1[Pros: Interpretable, simple, classical IR standard]
    Q1 --> R2[Cons: No semantics, high dim, sparse]

    Q2 --> S1[Pros: Captures similarity, low dim]
    Q2 --> S2[Cons: Static, OOV problem, no polysemy]

    Q3 --> T1[Pros: Handles OOV via subwords]
    Q3 --> T2[Cons: Static, larger model size]

    Q4 --> U1[Pros: Polysemy aware, SOTA on tasks]
    Q4 --> U2[Cons: Computationally expensive, black box]
```

### 4.4 Feature Selection Pipeline (Chi-Square for NLP)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Document Term Matrix] --> B[Compute Chi-Square Statistic per Term]
    B --> C[Rank Terms by Chi-Square Score]
    C --> D{Top K Threshold}
    D -->|Yes| E[Retain Top K Features]
    D -->|No| F[Discard Low Score Features]
    E --> G[Reduced Feature Matrix]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Train ML Model]
```

### 4.5 Word2Vec Training with Negative Sampling (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Word wI] --> B[Look up Embedding vwI]
    B --> C[Dot Product with Output Embedding vwO]
    C --> D{Sigmoid Decision}
    D -->|Positive Pair: True Context| E[Push vwI and vwO Closer]
    D -->|Negative Pair: Random Word| F[Push vwI and vwK Apart]
    E --> G[Update vwI via Gradient Descent]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Repeat for K Negative Samples]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define TF-IDF. Explain with an example how Inverse Document Frequency (IDF) helps in identifying the importance of a term in a document collection.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
TF-IDF stands for **Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency**. It is a statistical measure used to evaluate how important a word is to a document in a collection (corpus).

- **Term Frequency (TF):** $\mathrm{tf}(t, d) = f_{t,d}$ — counts how often term $t$ appears in document $d$.
- **Inverse Document Frequency (IDF):** $\mathrm{idf}(t) = \log \dfrac{N}{\mathrm{df}(t)}$ — penalizes terms appearing in many documents.

**Example:** In a corpus of 1000 documents, the word *"the"* appears in nearly all of them ($\mathrm{df} \approx 1000$), so its IDF $\approx \log(1000/1000) \approx 0$, giving it negligible weight. In contrast, *"photosynthesis"* appearing in only 5 documents has $\mathrm{idf} = \log(1000/5) \approx 2.3$, making it a strong discriminative feature. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Differentiate between Bag-of-Words (BoW) and Word2Vec feature representation with respect to dimensionality, sparsity, and semantic capture.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Bag-of-Words | Word2Vec |
|---|---|---|
| **Dimensionality** | $\vert V \vert$ (vocabulary size, often $10^5$–$10^6$) | $k$ (50–300) |
| **Sparsity** | Very sparse (mostly zeros) | Dense (no zeros, all entries used) |
| **Semantic Capture** | None — "king" and "queen" are orthogonal | Yes — similar words have similar vectors |
| **OOV Handling** | Trivial (just add a new column) | Poor (needs retraining or subword models) |

Thus Word2Vec is more semantically rich and computationally efficient for downstream neural models. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024] — Choice A**
**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the architecture of the **Skip-gram model** in Word2Vec. Derive the objective function and discuss the use of **Negative Sampling** to make training tractable.

**(b) [7 marks]** Given a tiny corpus of 4 documents, compute the **TF-IDF matrix** and identify the most discriminative term. Show all intermediate steps.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Skip-gram Architecture & Objective:**

The Skip-gram model (Mikolov et al., 2013) takes a **center word** as input and predicts the **surrounding context words** within a fixed window $c$.

**Architecture (3 layers):**
1. **Input layer:** One-hot vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert}$ for center word $w_I$.
2. **Hidden layer:** Dense embedding $\mathbf{v}_{w_I} = \mathbf{W}\mathbf{x}$ (where $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times \vert V \vert}$ is the input embedding matrix).
3. **Output layer:** Softmax over vocabulary to predict each context word.

**Objective Function:**
$$
J(\theta) = -\dfrac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\sum_{\substack{-c \leq j \leq c \\ j \neq 0}} \log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t;\theta)
$$

where the conditional probability uses softmax:
$$
P(w_O \mid w_I) = \dfrac{\exp(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\prime\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I})}{\sum_{w=1}^{V} \exp(\mathbf{v}_{w}^{\prime\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I})}
$$

**Why Negative Sampling:** The denominator sums over the entire vocabulary $V$ (potentially $10^6$ words), making each gradient step $O(V)$. Negative Sampling replaces this with $K$ binary logistic regressions ($K = 5$–$20$), reducing cost to $O(K)$. The new objective:

$$
J_{\text{SGNS}} = \log \sigma(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\prime\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) + \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{E}_{w_k \sim P_n}\left[\log \sigma(-\mathbf{v}_{w_k}^{\prime\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I})\right]
$$

where negative samples are drawn from $P_n(w) = U(w)^{3/4}/Z$ (raised to the 3/4 power to favor rarer words). **(7 marks)**

**Part (b) — TF-IDF Numerical:**

**Corpus (4 documents):**
- $d_1$: *"NLP is fun"*
- $d_2$: *"NLP is powerful"*
- $d_3$: *"Deep learning is fun"*
- $d_4$: *"NLP is NLP"*

**Step 1 — Vocabulary and counts:**

$$
V = \{\text{nlp}, \text{is}, \text{fun}, \text{powerful}, \text{deep}, \text{learning}\}, \quad \vert V \vert = 6, \quad N = 4
$$

**Step 2 — Raw TF and DF:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{tf}(\text{nlp}) &= [1, 1, 0, 2], \quad \mathrm{df}(\text{nlp}) = 3 \\
\mathrm{tf}(\text{is}) &= [1, 1, 1, 1], \quad \mathrm{df}(\text{is}) = 4 \\
\mathrm{tf}(\text{fun}) &= [1, 0, 1, 0], \quad \mathrm{df}(\text{fun}) = 2 \\
\mathrm{tf}(\text{powerful}) &= [0, 1, 0, 0], \quad \mathrm{df}(\text{powerful}) = 1 \\
\mathrm{tf}(\text{deep}) &= [0, 0, 1, 0], \quad \mathrm{df}(\text{deep}) = 1 \\
\mathrm{tf}(\text{learning}) &= [0, 0, 1, 0], \quad \mathrm{df}(\text{learning}) = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — IDF** (using $\log_{10}$, $N=4$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{idf}(\text{nlp}) &= \log_{10}(4/3) \approx 0.1249 \\
\mathrm{idf}(\text{is}) &= \log_{10}(4/4) = 0 \\
\mathrm{idf}(\text{fun}) &= \log_{10}(4/2) \approx 0.3010 \\
\mathrm{idf}(\text{powerful}) &= \log_{10}(4/1) \approx 0.6021 \\
\mathrm{idf}(\text{deep}) &= \log_{10}(4/1) \approx 0.6021 \\
\mathrm{idf}(\text{learning}) &= \log_{10}(4/1) \approx 0.6021
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — TF-IDF = TF × IDF** (assemble matrix):

$$
\mathbf{X}_{\text{tfidf}} = \begin{pmatrix}
0.1249 & 0 & 0.3010 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
0.1249 & 0 & 0 & 0.6021 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0.3010 & 0 & 0.6021 & 0.6021 \\
0.2498 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0
\end{pmatrix}
$$

**Most Discriminative Term:** *"powerful"*, *"deep"*, and *"learning"* all share the highest IDF of $\approx 0.6021$ (each appears in exactly one document). Among them, *"powerful"* is uniquely the most topic-bearing for $d_2$ alone. **(7 marks)**

---

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024] — Choice B (Alternative)**
**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **GloVe (Global Vectors)** model. Starting from the co-occurrence matrix, derive its objective function step by step. Highlight how it differs from Word2Vec.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **Pointwise Mutual Information (PMI)** measure for collocation extraction. Compute the PMI for the bigram *"machine learning"* given a sample corpus and explain why PPMI is preferred.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — GloVe Model:**

**Background:** GloVe (Pennington et al., 2014) combines the strengths of *global matrix factorization* (like LSA) and *local context window* methods (like Word2Vec). It uses a **global co-occurrence matrix** $X$ where $X_{ij}$ is the number of times word $j$ appears in the context of word $i$.

**Step 1 — Co-occurrence Probability:** $P_{ij} = P(j \mid i) = X_{ij}/X_i$ where $X_i = \sum_k X_{ik}$.

**Step 2 — Insight from Ratios:** For probe word $k$, the ratio $P_{ik}/P_{jk}$ carries meaning:
- If $k$ is related to $i$ but not $j$: ratio is large.
- If $k$ is related to both or neither: ratio is near 1.
- If $k$ is related to $j$ but not $i$: ratio is small.

**Step 3 — Encode in Vector Space:** We require a function $F$ that captures these ratios:
$$
F(\mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_j, \tilde{\mathbf{v}}_k) = \dfrac{P_{ik}}{P_{jk}}
$$

**Step 4 — Restrict to Differences** (vector space linearity):
$$
F\!\left((\mathbf{v}_i - \mathbf{v}_j)^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{v}}_k\right) = \dfrac{P_{ik}}{P_{jk}}
$$

**Step 5 — Homomorphism Requirement:** $F$ must satisfy $F(a+b) = F(a)F(b)$, giving $F(x) = \exp(x)$.

**Step 6 — Simplify:**
$$
\mathbf{v}_i^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{v}}_k = \log P_{ik} = \log X_{ik} - \log X_i
$$

Absorbing $\log X_i$ into a bias $b_i$:
$$
\mathbf{v}_i^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{v}}_k + b_i + \tilde{b}_k = \log X_{ik}
$$

**Step 7 — Final Weighted Least-Squares Objective:**
$$
J = \sum_{i,j=1}^{V} f(X_{ij})\left(\mathbf{v}_i^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{v}}_j + b_i + \tilde{b}_j - \log X_{ij}\right)^{2}
$$
with $f(x) = \min(1, (x/x_{\max})^{\alpha})$, typically $x_{\max}=100$, $\alpha=0.75$.

**Difference from Word2Vec:** GloVe uses **global co-occurrence statistics** (entire matrix) while Word2Vec uses **local context windows** (sampled pairs). GloVe is mathematically equivalent to factorizing the log of the co-occurrence matrix, while Word2Vec implicitly factorizes a shifted PMI matrix. **(7 marks)**

**Part (b) — PMI Computation:**

**Sample Corpus (toy):** Total words $N = 10000$.
- *"machine"* appears $\#(\text{machine}) = 100$ times.
- *"learning"* appears $\#(\text{learning}) = 80$ times.
- *"machine learning"* (bigram) co-occurs $\#(\text{machine}, \text{learning}) = 60$ times.

**Step 1 — Probabilities:**
$$
P(\text{machine}) = 100/10000 = 0.01
$$
$$
P(\text{learning}) = 80/10000 = 0.008
$$
$$
P(\text{machine}, \text{learning}) = 60/10000 = 0.006
$$

**Step 2 — PMI Calculation:**
$$
\mathrm{PMI}(\text{machine}, \text{learning}) = \log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{0.006}{0.01 \times 0.008}\right) = \log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{0.006}{0.00008}\right) = \log_{2}(75) \approx 6.23
$$

**Interpretation:** $\mathrm{PMI} = 6.23$ bits means the words co-occur **75 times more** than independence would predict — strong collocation.

**Why PPMI is Preferred:** Raw PMI is undefined when $P(w_1, w_2) = 0$ and can be **negative** (when words co-occur *less* than chance, e.g., *"the"* and *"of"*). Negative PMI is hard to interpret in vector spaces. **Positive PMI (PPMI)** clips negatives to zero:
$$
\mathrm{PPMI}(w_1, w_2) = \max(0, \mathrm{PMI}(w_1, w_2))
$$

This ensures all feature values are non-negative, making the resulting word-context matrix suitable for SVD and downstream learning. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `+1` smoothing in IDF** — When $\mathrm{df}(t) = N$, raw IDF becomes $\log(1) = 0$ (correct), but when $\mathrm{df}(t) = 0$ (term not in any doc), division by zero crashes. Always mention smoothing in the formula.
> 2. **Confusing CBOW and Skip-gram directions** — CBOW predicts center from context; Skip-gram predicts context from center. Examiners deduct full marks for this reversal.
> 3. **Using log base inconsistently** — Use $\log_e$ (natural log) for theoretical derivations and $\log_{10}$ for numerical examples. State the base explicitly.
> 4. **Skipping the homomorphic step in GloVe derivation** — Step 5 ($F = \exp$) is the crucial bridge and a frequent KTU sub-question.
> 5. **Confusing GloVe and Word2Vec objectives** — Word2Vec uses cross-entropy; GloVe uses weighted least-squares on log-co-occurrences.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **TF-IDF** = Term Frequency × Inverse Document Frequency; balances local importance with global rarity.
- [x] **Log-normalized TF** dampens dominance of high-frequency words; always use it for large corpora.
- [x] **IDF smoothing** (`+1` in denominator) prevents division by zero for unseen terms.
- [x] **Distributional Hypothesis** (Firth 1957): *"A word is characterized by the company it keeps."*
- [x] **Word2Vec** has two architectures: **CBOW** (predict center from context, faster) and **Skip-gram** (predict context from center, better for rare words).
- [x] **Negative Sampling** replaces full softmax ($O(V)$) with $K$ binary classifications ($O(K)$); sample with $P_n(w) = U(w)^{3/4}/Z$.
- [x] **GloVe** combines global matrix factorization with local windows; objective is weighted least-squares on $\log X_{ij}$ with weighting $f(x) = \min(1, (x/x_{\max})^{3/4})$.
- [x] **FastText** extends Word2Vec by representing each word as a **bag of character n-grams** (e.g., *"where"* = `{wh, whe, her, ere, re}`), handling OOV words.
- [x] **Contextual embeddings** (ELMo, BERT) generate different vectors for the same word in different contexts — solving **polysemy**.
- [x] **PMI** measures collocation strength; **PPMI** clips negatives to 0 for better downstream learning.
- [x] **Chi-square ($\chi^2$)** is the standard statistical feature-selection method for text classification; rank features and keep top-$K$.
- [x] **Cosine similarity** $\cos(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}) = \mathbf{u}^{\top}\mathbf{v} / (\Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert \Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert)$ is the standard metric for comparing embedding vectors.
- [x] **Dimensionality tip:** TF-IDF vectors live in $\mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert}$ (sparse, $10^5$–$10^6$); word embeddings live in $\mathbb{R}^{50\text{–}300}$ (dense); contextual embeddings live in $\mathbb{R}^{768\text{–}1024}$ (dense, dynamic).
- [x] **Application mapping:** TF-IDF → classical ML (SVM, NB); Word2Vec/GloVe → deep learning; BERT → state-of-the-art transfer learning.
- [x] **KTU 2024 weightage:** Expect at least one 7-mark derivation (Skip-gram, GloVe, or TF-IDF numerical) and one 7-mark conceptual comparison.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Information Extraction: Deep Learning Approaches, Relation Extraction Using Transformers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT601 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Information Extraction: Deep Learning Approaches, Relation Extraction Using Transformers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Information Extraction (IE)

**Definition:** Information Extraction (IE) is the task of automatically extracting structured information — such as entities, relations, events, and attributes — from unstructured or semi-structured natural language text. According to the KTU 2024 NLP syllabus, IE is a core downstream application built on top of core NLP pipelines (tokenization, POS tagging, parsing).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCCMT601 – Module 2):**
> *"Information Extraction involves the automatic extraction of structured information from unstructured text, including Named Entity Recognition (NER), Relation Extraction (RE), Coreference Resolution, and Event Extraction."*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **massive, disorganized library** with millions of newspaper articles piled randomly. You are asked: *"Who is the CEO of which company, and when were they appointed?"* A human would read each article, highlight names, organizations, dates, and write down the connections. **Information Extraction is the robotic librarian** — it reads, highlights (entities), connects (relations), and fills a structured spreadsheet (knowledge base) for you, all in milliseconds.

| IE Subtask | What the Robot-Librarian Does | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** | Highlights proper nouns | "Elon Musk", "Tesla", "2024" |
| **Relation Extraction (RE)** | Draws arrows between highlights | Elon Musk **is CEO of** Tesla |
| **Event Extraction** | Identifies triggers + arguments | "appointed" (trigger) → who, whom, when |
| **Coreference Resolution** | Decides if "he" = "Elon Musk" | "He founded SpaceX" → "He" → Elon Musk |

---

## 1.2 Deep Learning Approaches to Information Extraction

**Definition:** Deep Learning approaches to IE replace hand-crafted features and rule-based systems (like regex, gazetteers, Hearst patterns) with **end-to-end neural networks** that learn representations directly from raw text. Architectures include **BiLSTM-CRF**, **CNN-CRF**, and modern **Transformer encoders (BERT, RoBERTa, SpanBERT)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Deep Learning for IE?**
> - **Generalization:** Learns features automatically — no manual engineering of capitalization, prefix, suffix rules.
> - **Context awareness:** BiLSTM and Transformers capture long-range dependencies.
> - **Transfer learning:** Pre-trained encoders (BERT) provide strong priors; fine-tune on small IE datasets.
> - **State-of-the-art:** Deep models dominate benchmarks (CoNLL-2003, TACRED, ACE05).

### Conceptual Analogy

Old IE = a librarian who only follows a **strict checklist** ("Look for capitalized words, then look for the word 'CEO' nearby"). It breaks when the sentence is unusual.
Deep Learning IE = a librarian who has **read millions of books**, intuitively understands language, and can spot the relationship even in tricky sentences like *"Musk, the Tesla chief, said..."* — because it has learned that "chief" usually links a person to an organization.

---

## 1.3 Relation Extraction (RE)

**Definition:** Relation Extraction is the IE sub-task of identifying **semantic relationships between pairs of entities** mentioned in text. Formally, given a sentence $S = (w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n)$ and entities $e_1, e_2 \in S$, RE predicts a relation label $r \in \mathcal{R} \cup \{\text{None}\}$ from a pre-defined ontology.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example:**
> Sentence: *"Apple was founded by Steve Jobs in 1976."*
> - Entity 1: `Apple` (ORG)
> - Entity 2: `Steve Jobs` (PERSON)
> - Predicted Relation: `ORG-founder-PERSON` (e.g., `per:company_founder`)

---

## 1.4 Relation Extraction Using Transformers

**Definition:** Transformer-based RE leverages **self-attention mechanisms** and **pre-trained language models (PLMs)** to jointly encode both entities and their surrounding context, producing relation-aware representations. Architectures include:
- **BERT-based sentence classifiers** (encode `[CLS] sentence [SEP]`, classify `[CLS]`).
- **Entity-aware Transformers** (mark entity spans with special tokens, e.g., `[E1]...[/E1]`, `[E2]...[/E2]`).
- **Span-based / table-filling RE** (Lu et al., 2020 — T5-based end-to-end IE).

### Conceptual Analogy

Think of a Transformer as a **conference room** where every word is a person who can listen to every other word. In RE, the words "Steve" and "Jobs" whisper to "Apple" through the attention mechanism, and the room collectively votes: *"These two entities share a 'founder' relationship."* The `[CLS]` token is like the **chairperson** of the meeting — it summarizes the conclusion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conceptual Distinction (KTU-Favorite Question):**
> - **Pipeline IE:** NER → RE as separate steps (error propagation).
> - **Joint IE:** NER + RE solved together (shared parameters, less error propagation).
> - **End-to-End IE (Transformer-based):** Single model outputs entities + relations jointly (e.g., DyGIE++, T5-IE).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Information Extraction Pipeline (Classical vs. Deep Learning)

### 2.1.1 Classical (Pre-Deep-Learning) Pipeline
1. **Sentence segmentation** → split text into sentences.
2. **Tokenization** → word boundaries.
3. **POS tagging** → grammatical roles.
4. **Chunking / NER** → identify entity mentions.
5. **Relation extraction** → template/pattern matching (e.g., Hearst patterns for `is-a`).
6. **Coreference resolution** → link pronouns to entities.
7. **Event extraction** → trigger + argument identification.

### 2.1.2 Deep Learning Pipeline (Modern)
1. **Subword tokenization** (WordPiece, BPE, SentencePiece) → input to Transformer/BiLSTM.
2. **Distributed representation** → embeddings (word + positional + entity-type).
3. **Contextual encoding** → BiLSTM / Transformer encoder.
4. **Task-specific decoding** → Softmax / CRF / Span classifier.
5. **Joint training** → multi-task loss $L = L_{\text{NER}} + L_{\text{RE}} + L_{\text{Event}}$.

### Why & How (Accessible Explanation)

- **Why move from rules to DL?** Rules don't generalize to unseen vocabularies, dialects, or languages. DL learns from data.
- **How does DL "learn" entities?** By minimizing a loss function that penalizes wrong tag sequences. The model adjusts its internal weights via backpropagation.
- **Why BiLSTM before Transformers?** Historically BiLSTM-CRF was SOTA for NER (Huang et al., 2015). It captures left-to-right and right-to-left context with a CRF layer enforcing valid tag transitions (e.g., `I-PER` cannot follow `B-ORG`).

---

## 2.2 Neural Architectures for Information Extraction

### 2.2.1 BiLSTM-CRF (Huang et al., 2015) — Foundational DL-NER

$$\vec{h}_t = \text{LSTM}(x_t, \vec{h}_{t-1})$$

$$\overleftarrow{h}_t = \text{LSTM}(x_t, \overleftarrow{h}_{t+1})$$

$$h_t = [\vec{h}_t \, ; \, \overleftarrow{h}_t]$$

The CRF layer scores the entire tag sequence $y = (y_1, \ldots, y_n)$:

$$P(y \mid X) = \frac{\exp\left(\sum_{t=1}^{n} \left( W_{y_t}^\top h_t + T_{y_{t-1}, y_t} \right)\right)}{Z(X)}$$

where $T$ is the **transition matrix** of size $\vert \mathcal{T} \vert \times \vert \mathcal{T} \vert$, and $Z(X)$ is the partition function (sum over all valid sequences).

### 2.2.2 Transformer Encoder for IE (BERT-based)

BERT uses **multi-head self-attention**:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

$$\text{MultiHead}(Q,K,V) = [\text{head}_1 \, ; \, \ldots \, ; \, \text{head}_h] \, W^O$$

where each head is:

$$\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(Q W_i^Q, K W_i^K, V W_i^V)$$

For RE with BERT, the input format is typically:
`[CLS] sentence with entity markers [SEP]`

The `[CLS]` pooled representation $h_{\text{CLS}}$ is fed to a classifier:

$$\hat{y} = \text{softmax}(W_{\text{RE}} \, h_{\text{CLS}} + b_{\text{RE}})$$

### 2.2.3 End-to-End IE (T5 / BART Generative IE)

Recent models **generate** relation triples as text:

$$\text{Output: } \text{"[HEAD] Steve Jobs [REL] founder_of [TAIL] Apple"}$$

This unifies NER + RE + normalization in one seq2seq model.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Symbols | Notes / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Self-Attention** | $\text{Attn}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | $d_k$ = key dim | Scaled by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to stabilize gradients |
| **Multi-Head Attention** | $\text{MHA}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,\ldots,\text{head}_h) W^O$ | $h$ = num heads | Allows attending to different subspaces |
| **Scaled Dot-Product Factor** | $\sqrt{d_k}$ | variance control | Prevents softmax saturation |
| **BiLSTM Hidden State** | $h_t = [\vec{h}_t \, ; \, \overleftarrow{h}_t]$ | forward + backward concat | Captures bidirectional context |
| **CRF Sequence Score** | $s(X,y) = \sum_{t} (W_{y_t}^\top h_t + T_{y_{t-1},y_t})$ | $T$ = transition matrix | Enforces valid tag transitions (BIO) |
| **CRF Normalized Probability** | $P(y \mid X) = \frac{e^{s(X,y)}}{Z(X)}$ | $Z$ = partition function | $Z = \sum_{y'} e^{s(X,y')}$ |
| **CRF Negative Log-Likelihood** | $L_{\text{CRF}} = -\log P(y^{\text{gold}} \mid X)$ | training objective | Maximize gold sequence score |
| **Token Cross-Entropy** | $L_{\text{CE}} = -\sum_{i} y_i \log \hat{y}_i$ | per-token classification | Used in softmax-only NER |
| **Position Embedding (Transformer)** | $PE_{(pos,2i)} = \sin(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d}})$ | sinusoidal | Original Transformer paper |
| **RE Classification (BERT)** | $\hat{y} = \text{softmax}(W h_{\text{CLS}} + b)$ | pooled `[CLS]` | Standard sentence-pair RE |
| **Entity Marker Embedding** | $e_{ent} = e_{tok} + e_{type} + e_{pos}$ | type + position | Marks entity span boundaries |
| **Joint Multi-Task Loss** | $L = \alpha L_{\text{NER}} + \beta L_{\text{RE}} + \gamma L_{\text{Evt}}$ | $\alpha,\beta,\gamma$ = weights | Used in joint IE |
| **F1 Score (IE eval)** | $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | $P$ = precision, $R$ = recall | Standard IE evaluation metric |
| **Strict vs. Relaxed Match** | Strict: boundaries + type match; Relaxed: type match only | boundary detection | Boundary F1 vs. Type F1 |
| **Span Representation** | $h_{e} = [h_{\text{start}} \, ; \, h_{\text{end}}]$ | start/end hidden states | Used in span-based RE |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Most-Tested Formulas:**
> 1. Scaled Dot-Product Attention (almost guaranteed in every KTU NLP exam).
> 2. CRF sequence probability.
> 3. Cross-entropy loss for NER/RE classification.
> 4. F1 score for IE evaluation.
> 5. BiLSTM hidden state concatenation.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | IE Component Used | Industry |
|---|---|---|
| Drug-Drug Interaction | RE (drug–interacts_with–drug) | Pharma, Healthcare |
| Financial News Analytics | NER + RE (company–acquired–company) | FinTech, Trading |
| Knowledge Graph Construction | End-to-End IE | Google KG, Wikidata |
| Clinical Notes Structuring | Joint NER + RE (patient–has_disease–disease) | EHR Systems, MedNLP |
| Legal Document Mining | Event Extraction | e-Discovery, Compliance |

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Scaled Dot-Product Self-Attention (Transformer Backbone for RE)

This is the most important derivation in modern NLP, and **mandatory for KTU board exams**.

### Given
- Input matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{\text{model}}}$ (n tokens, each embedded as a $d_{\text{model}}$-dim vector).
- Learnable weight matrices $W^Q, W^K, W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k}$.

### Step 1 — Project to Queries, Keys, Values

$$Q = X W^Q, \quad K = X W^K, \quad V = X W^V$$

**Logic:** Each token is linearly transformed into three roles — a **query** (what am I looking for?), a **key** (what do I represent?), and a **value** (what information do I carry?).

### Step 2 — Compute Compatibility Scores

$$S = Q K^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$$

**Logic:** $S_{ij}$ = dot product of query of token $i$ with key of token $j$ — measuring how much token $i$ should attend to token $j$.

### Step 3 — Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to Stabilize Variance

$$\text{Scaled Score} = \frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}$$

**Why scale?** Assume $q, k$ are zero-mean, unit-variance independent vectors. Then $q \cdot k$ has variance $d_k$. Without scaling, large $d_k$ pushes softmax into regions with near-zero gradients. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ restores unit variance.

### Step 4 — Apply Softmax Row-wise to Get Attention Weights

$$A = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right), \quad A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$$

**Logic:** Each row of $A$ is a probability distribution over input positions. $A_{ij}$ = "attention weight" token $i$ places on token $j$.

### Step 5 — Weighted Sum of Values

$$\text{Output} = A V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$$

**Logic:** Each output position is a weighted average of all value vectors, with weights being the attention distribution.

### Final Compact Form

$$\boxed{\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cross-Entropy Loss for NER / RE Classification

### Given
- One-hot ground truth label $y \in \{0,1\}^C$ (C classes).
- Predicted softmax probability $\hat{y} \in \mathbb{R}^C$.

### Step 1 — Log-Probability of the True Class

For a true class index $c^\ast$ (where $y_{c^\ast} = 1$):

$$p_{c^\ast} = \hat{y}_{c^\ast} = \frac{e^{z_{c^\ast}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{C} e^{z_j}}$$

where $z$ are the logits.

### Step 2 — Negative Log-Likelihood for One Sample

$$L_{\text{CE}} = -\log \hat{y}_{c^\ast} = -\log\!\left(\frac{e^{z_{c^\ast}}}{\sum_{j} e^{z_j}}\right)$$

$$L_{\text{CE}} = -z_{c^\ast} + \log\!\left(\sum_{j=1}^{C} e^{z_j}\right)$$

**Logic:** The first term *increases* the true logit; the second term (log-sum-exp) *decreases* all logits. This is a tug-of-war pushing the correct class up and incorrect classes down.

### Step 3 — Average Over a Mini-Batch of Size $N$

$$L_{\text{batch}} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{i,c} \log \hat{y}_{i,c}$$

This is the standard **token-level cross-entropy** used in NER and `[CLS]`-based RE classifiers.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: CRF Layer in BiLSTM-CRF (NER Tagging)

### Given
- Emission scores from BiLSTM: $E \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times \vert \mathcal{T} \vert}$, where $E_{t,k}$ = score for tag $k$ at position $t$.
- Transition matrix $T \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathcal{T} \vert \times \vert \mathcal{T} \vert}$, $T_{i,j}$ = score of transitioning from tag $i$ to tag $j$.
- $T_{\text{start}, j}$ = score of starting with tag $j$; $T_{i, \text{end}}$ = score of ending after tag $i$.

### Step 1 — Score of a Tag Sequence $y = (y_1, \ldots, y_n)$

$$s(X, y) = \sum_{t=1}^{n} E_{t, y_t} + \sum_{t=1}^{n} T_{y_{t-1}, y_t}$$

For a sentence of length $n$, expand the transition sum:

$$s(X, y) = T_{\text{start}, y_1} + \sum_{t=1}^{n} E_{t, y_t} + \sum_{t=2}^{n} T_{y_{t-1}, y_t} + T_{y_n, \text{end}}$$

### Step 2 — Normalize via Softmax over All Possible Sequences

$$P(y \mid X) = \frac{e^{s(X,y)}}{Z(X)}, \quad Z(X) = \sum_{y' \in \mathcal{Y}} e^{s(X, y')}$$

**Logic:** $Z(X)$ is computed by the **forward algorithm** in $O(n \cdot \vert \mathcal{T} \vert^2)$ using dynamic programming.

### Step 3 — Training Loss (Negative Log-Likelihood)

$$L_{\text{CRF}} = -s(X, y^{\text{gold}}) + \log Z(X)$$

**Why?** Maximize the gold sequence's relative probability compared to all sequences. This is equivalent to minimizing KL divergence between predicted and gold distributions.

### Step 4 — Decoding (Viterbi)

Best sequence = $\arg\max_y s(X, y)$, found in $O(n \cdot \vert \mathcal{T} \vert^2)$ via Viterbi DP.

### Example: Why CRF Helps (KTU-Favorite)

Consider tags $\mathcal{T} = \{B-PER, I-PER, B-LOC, I-LOC\}$. The transition matrix learns:
- $T_{B-PER, I-PER}$ is high (likely transition).
- $T_{B-LOC, I-PER}$ is **negative/infeasible** (LOC cannot directly transition to inside-PER).

A plain softmax NER would emit `I-PER` after `B-LOC`; CRF blocks this.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: BERT-Based Relation Classification Scoring

### Given
- Sentence with two marked entity spans: $S$.
- Special tokens added: `[CLS] w_1 \ldots [E1]_{start} \ldots [/E1] [E2]_{start} \ldots [/E2} w_n [SEP]`
- BERT produces contextual embeddings $H = [h_{\text{CLS}}, h_1, \ldots, h_n, h_{\text{SEP}}]$.

### Step 1 — Compute [CLS] Representation

$$h_{\text{CLS}} = \text{BERT}_{\text{pool}}(S) \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$$

### Step 2 — Project to Relation Logits

$$z = W_{\text{RE}} h_{\text{CLS}} + b_{\text{RE}} \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathcal{R} \vert + 1}$$

where $\vert \mathcal{R} \vert + 1$ accounts for the "no relation" class.

### Step 3 — Softmax to Get Relation Probabilities

$$P(r \mid S, e_1, e_2) = \frac{e^{z_r}}{\sum_{r' \in \mathcal{R} \cup \{\text{None}\}} e^{z_{r'}}}$$

### Step 4 — Cross-Entropy Loss

$$L_{\text{RE}} = -\log P(r^{\text{gold}} \mid S, e_1, e_2)$$

**Why this works:** Pre-trained BERT has seen enough text to know that the context "founded by" usually indicates a `founder` relation. Fine-tuning adapts this prior to a specific relation ontology.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Method Comparison

| Dimension | Rule-Based IE | BiLSTM-CRF | Transformer (BERT) | Generative (T5) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Feature Engineering** | Manual | Minimal (char embeddings) | None (subword) | None |
| **Context Window** | Local | Full sentence | Global (self-attn) | Global |
| **Long Document** | Poor | Medium | Good (with tricks) | Excellent |
| **Training Data Required** | None (rules) | Medium | Low (with pretrain) | Low–Medium |
| **Inference Speed** | Fast | Medium | Slower (12–24 layers) | Slowest |
| **Interpretability** | High | Low | Very low | Very low |
| **Cross-lingual Transfer** | None | Limited | Excellent (mBERT, XLM-R) | Excellent |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | Low | High | **Very High** | Rising |

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Information Extraction Pipeline — Block Architecture

<!-- MERMAID_DIAGRAM_PLACEHOLDER_1 -->

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawText["Raw Unstructured Text"] --> tokenize["Subword Tokenization BPE or WordPiece"]
    tokenize --> embed["Embedding Layer Token + Position + Segment"]
    embed --> encoder["Transformer Encoder BERT or RoBERTa"]
    encoder --> nerHead["NER Head Token Classifier Softmax over BIO tags"]
    encoder --> reHead["RE Head Entity Pair Classifier Softmax over relation labels"]
    encoder --> eventHead["Event Head Trigger and Argument Classifier"]
    nerHead --> structOut["Structured Output Entities Relations Events"]
    reHead --> structOut
    eventHead --> structOut
    structOut --> kg["Knowledge Graph or Database"]
```

**Caption:** Modern Transformer-based IE uses a **shared encoder** with **multiple task-specific heads** for joint NER, RE, and event extraction.

---

## 4.2 BiLSTM-CRF Architecture for NER (Foundational Deep Learning IE)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input1["Input Sentence w1 to wn"] --> charEmb["Character Embeddings CNN or BiLSTM"]
    charEmb --> wordEmb["Word Embeddings GloVe or Word2Vec"]
    wordEmb --> concat["Concatenation"]
    concat --> biLSTM["Bidirectional LSTM Forward and Backward"]
    biLSTM --> emissions["Emission Scores n x T matrix"]
    emissions --> crfLayer["CRF Layer Transition Matrix T"]
    crfLayer --> viterbi["Viterbi Decoding Best Tag Sequence"]
    viterbi --> tagsOut["Output Tags B-PER I-PER B-LOC ..."]
```

**Caption:** BiLSTM produces per-token emission scores; the CRF layer enforces valid tag transitions; Viterbi finds the globally optimal tag sequence.

---

## 4.3 Transformer-Based Relation Extraction Architecture (BERT + Entity Markers)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sent["Sentence with Two Entities"] --> markers["Insert Special Entity Markers E1 start end E2 start end"]
    markers --> bertInput["BERT Input Format CLS text SEP"]
    bertInput --> bertEnc["12 or 24 Layer BERT Encoder"]
    bertEnc --> hCLS["Hidden State at CLS Token d dim"]
    hCLS --> linear["Linear Layer W_RE h CLS + b"]
    linear --> softmax["Softmax over Relation Labels including None"]
    softmax --> relOut["Predicted Relation e.g. per company founder"]
```

**Caption:** Standard BERT-RE pipeline. The `[CLS]` token aggregates sentence-level information for relation classification.

---

## 4.4 End-to-End Generative IE (T5 / BART) — Joint NER + RE

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawText2["Input Text"] --> tok2["T5 or BART Tokenizer SentencePiece"]
    tok2 --> encDec["Encoder Decoder Transformer"]
    encDec --> genOut["Generated Structured String Steve Jobs founder of Apple born 1955"]
    genOut --> parseOut["Parse Output into Triples Head Relation Tail"]
    parseOut --> kg2["Knowledge Base Ingestion"]
```

**Caption:** Generative IE casts IE as sequence-to-sequence generation. The model **writes** structured records as text, unifying NER, RE, and normalization in a single model.

---

## 4.5 Attention Mechanism Visualization (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1["Query Q from Steve"] --> dot["Dot Product with Keys"]
    k1["Key from Jobs"] --> dot
    k2["Key from Apple"] --> dot
    k3["Key from founded"] --> dot
    k4["Key from by"] --> dot
    dot --> scale["Scale by sqrt d_k"]
    scale --> soft["Softmax Normalization"]
    soft --> weights["Attention Weights w1 w2 w3 w4"]
    weights --> wsum["Weighted Sum of Values V"]
    wsum --> out["Output Representation of Steve"]
```

**Caption:** For the entity "Steve", attention computes compatibility with all other tokens' keys, normalizes via softmax, and produces a context-aware representation.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCCMT601]
**"Differentiate between Named Entity Recognition (NER) and Relation Extraction (RE). Illustrate with an example." [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | NER | RE |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Identify and classify entity mentions | Identify semantic relations **between** entity pairs |
| **Output** | Tag per token (e.g., `B-PER`, `I-ORG`) | Relation label for an entity pair (e.g., `per:company_founder`) |
| **Input** | Raw sentence | Sentence + identified entity pair |
| **Example Output** | "Steve Jobs /PER" and "Apple /ORG" | "Steve Jobs → `founder_of` → Apple" |

**Example sentence:** *"Steve Jobs co-founded Apple in 1976."*
- **NER Output:** Steve Jobs → `PER`, Apple → `ORG`, 1976 → `DATE`.
- **RE Output:** (Steve Jobs, `founder_of`, Apple).

NER is a **prerequisite** for RE in a pipeline IE system.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCCMT601]
**"Explain the role of the CRF layer in a BiLSTM-CRF model for NER. Why is it preferred over a plain softmax classifier?" [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The **CRF (Conditional Random Field)** layer sits on top of a BiLSTM and models **dependencies between adjacent tags** in a sequence. It uses a transition matrix $T \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathcal{T} \vert \times \vert \mathcal{T} \vert}$ where $T_{i,j}$ is the score of transitioning from tag $i$ to tag $j$.

**Why preferred over softmax:**
