# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Relation Detection and Classification (RDC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Conceptual Mapping):**
> **Relation Detection and Classification (RDC)** is the Natural Language Processing (NLP) task of identifying semantic relationships that exist between two or more entities mentioned in unstructured or semi-structured natural language text. Formally, given a sentence $S$ containing a pair of marked entity mentions $e_1$ and $e_2$ (often called the *arguments* or *participants* of the relation), the system must (a) **detect** whether a predefined relation $r \in R$ holds between them, and (b) **classify** the detected relation into one of $k$ predefined relation types $R = \{r_1, r_2, \dots, r_k\}$, or assign the special "No-Relation" (or `NONE`) class if no valid relation exists.

In KTU parlance and ACE (Automatic Content Extraction) terminology:
- **Relation Trigger**: The textual context that signals a semantic connection.
- **Relation Arguments**: The participating entities (typically Entity Type 1 and Entity Type 2).
- **Relation Type**: The semantic label assigned (e.g., `PER-SOC` for person-social, `ORG-AFF` for organization-affiliation, `PHYS` for physical).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Family Tree Detective" Analogy:**
> Imagine you are a detective given a stack of newspaper clippings. Your job is to read each article and fill out a structured family tree. When you see the sentence *"Apple Inc., founded by Steve Jobs in 1976, is headquartered in Cupertino,"* you must:
> 1. **Spot the entities**: "Apple Inc." (a company), "Steve Jobs" (a person), "Cupertino" (a location).
> 2. **Decide if they are related**: Yes, "Steve Jobs" and "Apple Inc." are related.
> 3. **Classify the relation type**: The relationship is "Founder-Of" (an `ORG-AFF` relation), and "Apple Inc." and "Cupertino" are related by "Located-In" (a `PHYS` relation).
>
> **RDC is the NLP system's version of this detective work** — turning raw, messy text into a structured knowledge graph of who did what to whom, where, and when.

## 1.3 The Two-Half Problem: Detection + Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight — Why two halves?**
> Relation Detection answers the binary question: *"Is there A relation between these two entities?"*
> Relation Classification answers the multi-class question: *"If yes, WHICH type of relation?"*
>
> Mathematically, RDC is a function $f: \mathcal{E} \times \mathcal{E} \times S \rightarrow R \cup \{\text{NONE}\}$, where $\mathcal{E}$ is the entity set, $S$ is the sentence space, and $R$ is the finite set of relation types.

## 1.4 The Supervision Spectrum

| Supervision Level | Data Required | Typical Use Case |
|-------------------|---------------|------------------|
| **Fully Supervised** | Hand-labeled sentences with entities + relation tags | High-accuracy domain-specific systems |
| **Lightly Supervised** | A small set of seed examples or a knowledge base | Bootstrapping across new domains |
| **Distantly Supervised** | A knowledge base (e.g., Wikipedia infoboxes) + raw text | Large-scale web-scale extraction |
| **Unsupervised** | None (raw text only) | Open Information Extraction (OpenIE) |

## 1.5 Standard Relation Taxonomies Used in KTU/Exams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ACE 2005 (Automatic Content Extraction) — 7 Major Relation Types:**
> 1. `PHYS` — Physical (Located, Near)
> 2. `PER-SOC` — Personal/Social (Business, Family, Other)
> 3. `EMP-ORG` — Employment/Organization (Employ-Exec, Employ-Staff, Member-Of, Subsidiary, Other)
> 4. `ART` — User-Owner-Inventor-Manufacturer (User, Owner, Inventor, Manufacturer)
> 5. `GPE-AFF` — Geo-Political Entity-Affiliation (Citizen, Resident, Religious-Affiliation, Org-Location)
> 6. `DISC` — Discourse (Message-Topic)
> 7. `NONE` — No relation

**TAC-KBP** (Knowledge Base Population) defines relation slots (e.g., `per:spouse`, `org:city_of_headquarters`).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Supervised Approaches — The Three Families

### A. Feature-Based Classifiers
The classical pipeline:
1. Parse the sentence (shallow or deep).
2. Extract **lexical, syntactic, and semantic features** between entity pairs.
3. Train a multi-class classifier (SVM, MaxEnt/Logistic Regression, Random Forest).

**Step-by-step Logic:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why each feature matters (Intuition):**
> - **Entity types** ($E_1$ type, $E_2$ type): `PERSON` cannot be `Located-In` a `PERSON` — type pairs prune impossible relations.
> - **Words between entities** ("Bag-of-Words in between"): captures lexical cues like *"founded"* for `Founder-Of`.
> - **Entity mention level** (Name, Nominal, Pronoun): a pronoun in subject position is a strong cue.
> - **Dependency path**: shortest path in the parse tree between $e_1$ and $e_2$.
> - **Entity distance**: number of tokens between $e_1$ and $e_2$.
> - **Constituency path**: path in the phrase-structure tree.
> - **Nested relation features**, **gazetteer features** (country lists, company lists).

### B. Kernel-Based Classifiers
Kernels sidestep explicit feature engineering by computing similarity between two structured objects (parse trees, sequences).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Kernels You MUST Know for KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Convolution Kernel (Zelenko et al., 2003)** — computes similarity over shallow parse trees using subsequence kernels.
> 2. **Subsequence Kernel (Bunescu & Mooney, 2005)** — extracts the shortest dependency path between two entities and computes a subsequence kernel over the words and dependency types on this path.
>
> **Mathematical Form (Conceptual):**
> $$K(T_1, T_2) = \sum_{n_1 \in T_1} \sum_{n_2 \in T_2} C(n_1, n_2)$$
> where $C$ counts the number of common substructures weighted by their size and depth.

### C. Deep Learning / Neural Approaches
The modern state-of-the-art (post-2015). The dominant architecture is:
1. **Input**: Sentence + entity position markers.
2. **Embedding layer**: Word embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe, BERT).
3. **Encoder**: CNN / BiLSTM / Transformer.
4. **Relation classifier**: Softmax over relation labels.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU must-know architecture — CNN for Relation Classification ( Zeng et al., 2014):**
> - Lexical features (entity mentions + their left/right context tokens) are concatenated.
> - Sentence is converted to a matrix via embeddings.
> - Convolutional filters of varying widths extract n-gram patterns.
> - Max-pooling + softmax for classification.

## 2.2 Lightly Supervised Approaches

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Idea:** Hand-labeling thousands of sentences is expensive. Light supervision uses *small seed sets* or *distant knowledge bases* to bootstrap.

### A. Bootstrapping (Seed-Based)
**Algorithm: DIPRE (Brin, 1998) / Snowball (Agichtein & Gravano, 2000)**
1. Start with a small set of seed tuples: $\{(e_1, r, e_2)\}$.
2. Find sentences containing these tuples in a large corpus.
3. Learn *patterns* from the surrounding context.
4. Apply patterns to extract new tuples.
5. Iterate until convergence or noise threshold.

**Example:** Seeds `("Steve Jobs", Founder-Of, "Apple")` → Pattern `<PER>, *founder of* <ORG>` → Extract `("Bill Gates", Founder-Of, "Microsoft")`.

### B. Distant Supervision (Mintz et al., 2009)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The key assumption:** *"If two entities participate in a known relation in a knowledge base, then any sentence containing both entities likely expresses that relation."*
>
> **Process:**
> 1. Take a KB (e.g., Freebase, Wikipedia infoboxes) with known `(e1, relation, e2)` triples.
> 2. For each triple, find ALL sentences in a corpus that contain both $e_1$ and $e_2$.
> 3. Aggregate features across all matching sentences.
> 4. Train a multi-class classifier.
>
> **Problem — Wrong Label Problem:** A sentence containing `("Obama", born-in, "Hawaii")` might actually be about Obama's vacation, not his birth. The label is *noisy*.

### C. Unsupervised / Open IE
**Reverb / OpenIE 4 / Stanford OpenIE** extract relation tuples without predefined types:
> *"Apple was founded by Steve Jobs in 1976."* $\rightarrow$ `(Apple, was-founded-by, Steve Jobs)`

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — every formula appears in KTU board questions.**

| Concept | Formula / Definition | KTU Exam Use |
|---------|---------------------|--------------|
| **Precision (P)** | $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | How many predicted relations are correct? |
| **Recall (R)** | $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | How many gold relations did we find? |
| **F1-Score** | $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean — the **default KTU metric** |
| **Micro-F1** | Aggregate TP/FP/FN globally, then compute F1 | Class-imbalanced datasets |
| **Macro-F1** | Average F1 per class equally | Class-balanced view |
| **P@N (Precision at N)** | $P@N = \frac{\text{Relevant in top } N}{N}$ | Bootstrapping evaluation |
| **AUC** | Area Under Precision-Recall or ROC curve | Threshold-free quality |
| **Bag-of-Words vector** | $\vec{v}(s) = \sum_{w \in s} \vec{e}(w)$ | Sentence representation |
| **Softmax classifier** | $P(r \mid x) = \frac{\exp(W_r \cdot h)}{\sum_{r' \in R} \exp(W_{r'} \cdot h)}$ | Output layer of neural RDC |
| **Cross-Entropy Loss** | $L = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{r \in R} y_i^{(r)} \log \hat{y}_i^{(r)}$ | Training objective |
| **Distant Supervision Confidence** | $P(r \mid e_1, e_2) = 1 - (1 - p_r)^N$ | At-least-one noisy label (Riedel et al., 2010) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where RDC is deployed in industry:**
> - **Knowledge Graph Construction** (Google KG, Amazon Product Graph, Wikidata auto-fill)
> - **Biomedical NLP** (drug–gene–protein–disease relations in PubMed)
> - **Financial Intelligence** (company–acquisition–merger relations in news)
> - **Question Answering** (Factoid QA like Watson, search engines)
> - **Legal Tech** (contract clause relationship extraction)
> - **Intelligence & Defense** (entity–event–location linking in OSINT)

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why F1 is the Harmonic Mean (and not the Arithmetic Mean)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Frequently Asked Question:** *"Why do we use F1 instead of a simple average of Precision and Recall?"*

**Step 1 — The Limitation of Arithmetic Mean:**
Consider a relation extractor with $P = 1.0$ but $R = 0.01$ (it makes only one correct prediction out of 100 gold relations).
Arithmetic mean: $\frac{P + R}{2} = \frac{1.0 + 0.01}{2} = 0.505$ — a deceptively high score.

**Step 2 — The Harmonic Mean's Penalty:**
The harmonic mean penalizes extreme imbalances.

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$$

Plugging in $P = 1.0, R = 0.01$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot (1.0) \cdot (0.01)}{1.0 + 0.01} \\
&= \frac{0.02}{1.01} \\
&\approx 0.0198
\end{aligned}
$$

The F1 is correctly low, exposing the poor system. ✅

**Step 3 — The General $\beta$-weighted F-Measure:**

$$
F_\beta = \frac{(1 + \beta^2) \cdot P \cdot R}{\beta^2 \cdot P + R}
$$

- $\beta = 1$: Equal weight (default in KTU).
- $\beta > 1$: Recall-weighted (penalize missing positive relations more).
- $\beta < 1$: Precision-weighted.

## 3.2 Derivation: The Distant Supervision Aggregation

> [!NOTE]
> **At-Least-One Label Model (Riedel, Yao & McCallum, 2010):**
> Given a knowledge-base triple $(e_1, r^*, e_2)$ and a corpus of $N$ sentences mentioning both $e_1$ and $e_2$, we model the probability that *at least one* sentence expresses the true relation $r^*$.

**Assumption:** Each sentence independently expresses the true relation with probability $p_r$.

**Probability that NO sentence expresses it:** $(1 - p_r)^N$

**Probability that AT LEAST ONE sentence expresses it:** $1 - (1 - p_r)^N$

$$
P(\text{label} = r \mid e_1, e_2, S) = 1 - (1 - p_r)^N
$$

**Numerical example for KTU exam practice:**
Let $p_r = 0.3$, $N = 5$ sentences.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 1 - (1 - 0.3)^5 \\
&= 1 - (0.7)^5 \\
&= 1 - 0.16807 \\
&\approx 0.8319
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** With 5 sentences and a 30% per-sentence expression rate, there is an **83.2% chance** that at least one sentence correctly expresses the KB relation.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example: Feature-Based Relation Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample sentence (ACE corpus style):**
> *"John Smith, the CEO of Acme Corp, lives in New York."*

**Entities to consider for relation extraction:**

| Entity 1 | Entity 2 | Candidate Relation |
|----------|----------|---------------------|
| John Smith (PERSON) | Acme Corp (ORG) | `EMP-ORG` (employ-exec) |
| John Smith (PERSON) | New York (GPE) | `PHYS` (resides in) |
| Acme Corp (ORG) | New York (GPE) | `GPE-AFF` (org-location) |

**Step 1 — Lexical Features extracted for the (John Smith, Acme Corp) pair:**

- **E1 type**: `PERSON`
- **E2 type**: `ORG`
- **E1 mention level**: `NAME`
- **E2 mention level**: `NAME`
- **Words between E1 and E2**: `{the, CEO, of, Acme, Corp}` → bag-of-words: `the:1, CEO:1, of:1, Acme:1, Corp:1`
- **Left of E1**: empty
- **Right of E2**: `, lives in New York`
- **Chunk path**: `NP → VP → PP → NP`

**Step 2 — Classifier Decision (illustrative SVM with linear kernel):**
The classifier has learned that the word "CEO" is a strong indicator of `employ-exec` relation.
Output: `EMP-ORG.Employ-Exec` with confidence `0.94`.

**Step 3 — Apply to second pair (John Smith, New York):**
- "lives in" is a strong cue for `PHYS.Near` or `PHYS.Located`.
- Output: `PHYS.Located` with confidence `0.87`.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Favorite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison table — memorize for 14-mark questions:**

| Feature | Feature-Based | Kernel-Based | Neural (Deep Learning) | Distant Supervision |
|---------|---------------|--------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| **Feature Engineering** | Heavy (manual) | Moderate (tree kernels) | Minimal (auto-learned) | Moderate (align KB) |
| **Performance (F1)** | 60–70% (ACE) | 65–75% | **85–92%** (BERT-based) | 70–80% (with denoising) |
| **Training Data** | Labeled sentences | Labeled + parse trees | Labeled sentences | KB + raw corpus |
| **Scalability** | Low | Low–Medium | High (GPU) | **Very High** |
| **Interpretability** | High | Medium | **Low** (black box) | Medium |
| **Noise Tolerance** | Low | Low | Medium (with dropout) | **High** (multi-instance) |
| **Domain Adaptation** | Hard | Hard | Medium (fine-tuning) | Easy (new KB) |
| **Key Limitation** | Sparsity | Compute cost | Data hungry | Wrong-label problem |
| **Representative Paper** | Kambhatla 2004 | Zelenko 2003, Bunescu 2005 | Zeng 2014, Zhang 2015, Shi 2016 | Mintz 2009, Riedel 2010, Hoffmann 2011 |
| **Best For** | Small, clean datasets | Structured domains | **Modern state-of-the-art** | Web-scale extraction |

## 3.5 The Bootstrapping Algorithm in Pseudocode (Conceptual, for KTU CS branch)

> [!NOTE]
> **Since this is a CS course (PCCMT601), pseudocode IS allowed. The KTU system prompt forbids Python only in non-CS subjects.**

```
ALGORITHM: Bootstrapping-RDC
INPUT: Seed tuples S = {(e1, r, e2)}, Corpus C, Iteration limit I
OUTPUT: Extracted tuple set T

1. T ← S
2. Patterns ← ∅
3. for i = 1 to I do
4.     for each tuple (e1, r, e2) in T do
5.         Sentences ← FindSentences(C, e1, e2)
6.         for each sentence s in Sentences do
7.             Context ← ExtractContext(s, e1, e2)
8.             Patterns ← Patterns ∪ {Context → r}
9.     NewTuples ← ApplyPatterns(C, Patterns)
10.    T ← T ∪ NewTuples
11.    if T converged then break
12. return T
```

flowchart TD
    A[Raw Text Input] --> B[Tokenization & POS Tagging]

    C --> D[Entity Pair Enumeration]
    D --> E{Feature Extraction}

    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H{Classifier Choice}

    H --> I2[Tree Kernel SVM]
    H --> I3[CNN / BiLSTM / Transformer]

    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    J --> K{Evaluation}

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    KB[(Knowledge Base e.g., Freebase)] --> A[Known Triples e1, r, e2]
    CORPUS[(Large Text Corpus e.g., Wikipedia)] --> A
    A --> B[Sentence-Level Alignment]
    B --> C[Noisy Labeled Training Set]
    C --> D[Feature Extractor or Neural Encoder]
    D --> E[Multi-Class Classifier]
    E --> F[New Relation Predictions]
    F --> G{At-Least-One Model or Multi-Instance Learning}
    G --> H[Final Aggregated Relations]
```

## 4.3 Bootstrapping Loop (Lightly Supervised)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Small Seed Tuples e1,r,e2] --> P1[Find Sentences Containing Seeds]
    P1 --> P2[Learn Contextual Patterns]
    P2 --> P3[Apply Patterns to Corpus]
    P3 --> P4[Extract New Tuples]
    P4 --> P5{Score / Filter by Confidence}
    P5 -->|Above Threshold| P6[Add to Seed Set]
    P6 --> P1
    P5 -->|Below Threshold| P7[Discard or Manual Review]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Set A]**
**Q: Define Relation Detection and Classification (RDC). How is it different from Named Entity Recognition (NER)?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks distribution):**
> - **(1 mark)** RDC is the NLP task of identifying and classifying semantic relationships between pairs of entity mentions in text, outputting a relation label from a predefined set $R$ (or `NONE` if no relation holds).
> - **(1 mark)** NER identifies *what* entities exist (their types — PERSON, ORG, GPE, etc.). RDC goes one step further and identifies *how* two entities are semantically connected (e.g., `Employ-Exec`, `Located-In`).
> - **(1 mark)** RDC is therefore a downstream task that typically **consumes NER output as input** and adds a relational layer of meaning.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q: List and briefly explain any THREE evaluation metrics used for Relation Analysis Systems.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (1 mark per metric):**
> 1. **Precision (P):** Fraction of predicted relations that are correct — $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$. Answers *"Of the relations we predicted, how many are right?"*
> 2. **Recall (R):** Fraction of gold relations that the system recovered — $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$. Answers *"Of all true relations, how many did we find?"*
> 3. **F1-Score:** Harmonic mean of P and R — $F_1 = \frac{2PR}{P+R}$. Preferred over arithmetic mean because it penalizes extreme imbalances between P and R.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper Module 3]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the **Supervised Feature-Based Approach** to Relation Classification in detail. List the major categories of features used with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Distant Supervision** approach for relation extraction. Clearly state the **at-least-one assumption** and the **wrong-label problem**, with a numerical example. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Framework (2 marks):**
> The supervised feature-based approach treats RDC as a multi-class classification problem. Given a sentence $S$ with two marked entity mentions $e_1$ and $e_2$, the system extracts a feature vector $\vec{f}(S, e_1, e_2)$ and feeds it to a trained classifier (SVM, MaxEnt, or Random Forest). The classifier outputs $\hat{r} = \arg\max_r P(r \mid \vec{f})$.

> **Step 2 — Categories of Features (4 marks, with 1 mark per category):**
>
> | Feature Category | Examples | Intuition |
> |------------------|----------|-----------|
> | **Lexical / Entity** | $e_1$ type, $e_2$ type, mention level (NAME/NOM/PRON), entity head nouns | Type pair `PERSON-ORG` strongly biases toward `EMP-ORG` |
> | **Word/Token** | Bag-of-words between $e_1$ and $e_2$, left and right context windows | "CEO", "spokesperson", "located in" are high-value cue words |
> | **Syntactic** | Base NP chunks, constituency path, dependency path, POS tags | Shortest dependency path between arguments |
> | **Semantic** | Gazetteer features, WordNet hypernyms, entity class, country lists | Matches entity to predefined semantic categories |
> | **Distance & Position** | Number of tokens between $e_1$ and $e_2$, entity order (e1-before-e2 vs reverse) | Shorter distance = higher chance of direct relation |
> | **Overlap** | Number of common tokens, whether entities share NPs | Detects appositive and copular relations |

> **Step 3 — Limitations (1 mark):**
> - Requires extensive manual feature engineering.
> - Sparse features (high-dimensional, low-density vectors).
> - Does not generalize well to new domains or relation types.

### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Motivation (1 mark):**
> Hand-labeling training data for supervised systems is expensive. Distant supervision (Mintz et al., 2009) leverages existing Knowledge Bases (KBs) like Freebase to automatically create large, noisy training sets.

> **Step 2 — Algorithm (2 marks):**
> 1. Take a KB with known triples $(e_1, r, e_2)$.
> 2. For each triple, retrieve all sentences in a large corpus that mention both $e_1$ and $e_2$.
> 3. Use these sentences as positive training examples for relation $r$.
> 4. Train a standard multi-class classifier (SVM or neural) on the noisy data.

> **Step 3 — The Wrong-Label Problem (2 marks):**
> Not every sentence containing $e_1$ and $e_2$ actually expresses the KB relation. Example: KB says `("Obama", born-in, "Hawaii")`, but the sentence *"Obama vacationed in Hawaii last summer"* does NOT express `born-in`. The label is **noisy/incorrect**, and naive training propagates this error.

> **Step 4 — Numerical Example of At-Least-One Assumption (2 marks):**
> Per-sentence expression probability $p_r = 0.25$, number of sentences $N = 4$.
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> P(\text{at least one correct}) &= 1 - (1 - p_r)^N \\
> &= 1 - (1 - 0.25)^4 \\
> &= 1 - (0.75)^4 \\
> &= 1 - 0.3164 \\
> &\approx 0.6836 \;\; (\text{i.e., } 68.36\%)
> \end{aligned}
> $$

---

## 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Module 3, Set B]**

**Q: (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Bootstrapping approach** for lightly supervised relation extraction. Mention its strengths and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Supervised, Distantly Supervised, and Bootstrapping** approaches to relation extraction across at least 6 dimensions. Why is the F1-score preferred over simple accuracy for evaluating these systems? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Concept (2 marks):**
> Bootstrapping is a lightly supervised technique that begins with a small set of seed tuples $\{(e_1, r, e_2)\}$ and iteratively learns contextual patterns to extract new tuples of the same relation type. Famous systems: **DIPRE** (Brin, 1998), **Snowball** (Agichtein & Gravano, 2000), **KnowItAll** (Etzioni et al., 2004).

> **Step 2 — Iterative Loop (3 marks, with the diagram as 1 mark):**
>
> ```
> SEEDS → Find Sentences → Learn Patterns 
>    ↑                              ↓
>    └── Add New Tuples ← Apply Patterns to Corpus
> ```
>
> 1. **Seed phase:** Start with high-confidence example tuples.
> 2. **Pattern learning:** From sentences containing seed tuples, extract context patterns (e.g., `<PER>`, the `CEO` of `<ORG>`).
> 3. **Pattern application:** Apply learned patterns to the entire corpus.
> 4. **Tuple extraction & filtering:** Keep only tuples that match multiple high-confidence patterns.
> 5. **Iteration:** Add new tuples to the seed set and repeat.

> **Step 3 — Strengths (1 mark):**
> - Requires minimal manual annotation (only seeds).
> - Easily adapts to new domains by changing seeds.
> - Scales to very large corpora.

> **Step 4 — Limitations (1 mark):**
> - **Semantic drift:** Errors compound over iterations (e.g., starting with "CEO of" drifts to "director of" → "actor in").
> - Confidence scoring and pattern validation are non-trivial.
> - Patterns may overfit to seed entities.

### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Comparison Table (4 marks):**
>
> | Dimension | Supervised | Distant Supervision | Bootstrapping |
> |-----------|------------|---------------------|---------------|
> | **Labeled Data Needed** | Fully hand-labeled sentences | KB + raw corpus | Small seed set |
> | **Annotation Cost** | High | **Low** (KB auto) | Very low |
> | **Coverage** | Limited to annotated relations | **Wide** (KB scale) | Medium |
> | **Noise Level** | Clean | **High** (wrong-label) | Medium (drift) |
> | **F1 Performance** | 85–92% (with deep learning) | 70–80% | 50–70% |
> | **Scalability** | Low | **Very high** | High |
> | **Domain Adaptation** | Hard | Easy (swap KB) | Easy (swap seeds) |
> | **Best Use Case** | High-accuracy benchmarks | Web-scale KG construction | Quick prototyping |

> **Step 2 — Why F1 over Accuracy (3 marks):**
> - RDC datasets are **highly imbalanced** — the `NONE` class (no relation) usually dominates 80–95% of instances.
> - A trivial system that predicts `NONE` for everything achieves **95% accuracy** but **0% F1**.
> - F1 is invariant to class imbalance, focuses on the positive (relation) class.
> - F1 combines both types of errors (false positives and false negatives) into a single balanced metric.
> - It is the **official metric** of ACE, TAC-KBP, and SemEval shared tasks.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks in RDC questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Detection with Classification:** Detection is binary ("Is there a relation?"), Classification is multi-class ("What type?"). Examiners explicitly check this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting the `NONE` class:** Always include the `NONE` (no-relation) class in your discussion of relation taxonomies — it is often the majority class.
> 3. **Wrong formula for F1:** Writing $F_1 = \frac{P + R}{2}$ instead of the harmonic mean $\frac{2PR}{P+R}$ costs full marks on the formula question.
> 4. **Skipping the wrong-label problem:** Any 7-mark question on Distant Supervision that fails to mention the wrong-label/noisy-label issue is considered incomplete.
> 5. **Missing the at-least-one assumption:** Riedel et al.'s at-least-one formulation is the most cited remedy for the wrong-label problem — it MUST appear in your answer.
> 6. **Not naming the at-least-one formula explicitly:** $P(\text{at least one}) = 1 - (1 - p_r)^N$ is a 2-mark formula — know it cold.
> 7. **Confusing Bootstrapping with Distant Supervision:** Bootstrapping uses *seeds*; Distant Supervision uses a *Knowledge Base*. They are different paradigms.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔖 High-Density Revision Bullets — Read This the Night Before the Exam:**

- **RDC** = Relation Detection (binary) + Relation Classification (multi-class). Together they answer *"What relation, if any, holds between $e_1$ and $e_2$ in sentence $S$?"*
- **Three supervised families:** (i) **Feature-based** (manual features → SVM/MaxEnt), (ii) **Kernel-based** (tree kernels over parse trees), (iii) **Neural** (CNN, BiLSTM, Transformer, BERT-based).
- **Lightly supervised families:** **Bootstrapping** (seeds → patterns → new tuples), **Distant Supervision** (KB alignment → noisy auto-labeled data), **Unsupervised/OpenIE** (no schema).
- **Distant Supervision Assumption:** If $(e_1, r, e_2)$ is in a KB, then any sentence containing both $e_1$ and $e_2$ likely expresses $r$. **The wrong-label problem** is the central challenge.
- **At-Least-One Formula (Riedel 2010):** $P(r \mid e_1, e_2, S) = 1 - (1 - p_r)^N$. Master this.
- **Bootstrapping Risk:** **Semantic drift** — iterative errors compound and the system may drift to wrong relation types.
- **Evaluation Metrics — know cold:** $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$, $R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$, $F_1 = \frac{2PR}{P+R}$.
- **F1 over Accuracy:** RDC datasets are imbalanced (the `NONE` class dominates); F1 is the standard.
- **Macro-F1 vs Micro-F1:** Macro = average per class (equal weight). Micro = pool all instances (weight by class size). Use **Macro-F1** for balanced evaluation, **Micro-F1** for skewed data.
- **ACE 7 major relation types:** `PHYS`, `PER-SOC`, `EMP-ORG`, `ART`, `GPE-AFF`, `DISC`, `NONE`. ACE 2005 is the **default benchmark** for KTU exam questions.
- **TAC-KBP** uses slot-filling style relations (e.g., `per:spouse`, `org:city_of_headquarters`).
- **Feature categories to list in any 7-mark feature-based question:** Lexical (entity type, mention level), Word-based (BOW between entities), Syntactic (dependency/constituency path), Semantic (gazetteers, WordNet), Distance/position, Overlap features.
- **Subsequence Kernel (Bunescu & Mooney, 2005):** operates on the **shortest dependency path** between two entities — name this technique when discussing kernel methods.
- **State-of-the-art:** BERT/Transformer-based relation classifiers reach **F1 ≈ 90%+** on ACE 2005; always mention deep learning as the modern SOTA in conclusion paragraphs.
- **Real-world applications to mention in any "applications" question:** Knowledge graph construction (Google KG), biomedical relation extraction, financial intelligence, legal tech, question answering, and defense/OSINT systems.
- **Year of paper + author = 1 mark** in examiner's eyes: Kambhatla (2004), Zelenko (2003), Bunescu & Mooney (2005), Mintz (2009), Riedel (2010), Zeng (2014). Cite at least 2-3 in any long-answer question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Information Retrieval: Term Weighting, Document Scoring, Inverted Index, Neural IR, Transformer-Based IR Models, Evaluation of IR Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT601 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Information Retrieval: Term Weighting, Document Scoring, Inverted Index, Neural IR, Transformer-Based IR Models, Evaluation of IR Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Information Retrieval (IR)

**Information Retrieval (IR)** is the science and engineering discipline concerned with representing, storing, organizing, and accessing textual information items (documents) to satisfy a user's information need expressed as a query. Formally, given a corpus $\mathcal{D} = \{d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_N\}$ of $N$ documents and a query $q$, the goal of an IR system is to return a ranked list $\mathcal{R}(q) \subseteq \mathcal{D}$ ordered by a **relevance score** $S(q, d)$ such that documents most likely to satisfy the user's information need appear at the top of the ranking.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic spans both classical (lexical) IR and modern (neural/Transformer-based) IR. Students must be conversant with both paradigms, since questions in Module 3 frequently contrast them.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Library Analogy:** Imagine a massive library with millions of books but **no card catalog, no librarian, and no computer search**. Information Retrieval is the technology that builds the *invisible librarian*. You walk up and say *"I want books about deep learning for medical imaging"* (your **query**), and the IR system sifts through millions of documents and hands you the **top 10 most relevant** books, ranked from best to worst match. The "librarian's brain" is built from a stack of techniques: an **Inverted Index** acts as the card catalog, **Term Weighting** decides which words matter most, **Document Scoring** calculates how well a book matches your request, and **Evaluation Metrics** measure whether the librarian is actually doing a good job.

### 1.3 The Four Pillars of Modern IR (KTU High-Yield Framework)

| Pillar | Classical (Sparse) IR | Modern (Dense/Neural) IR |
|---|---|---|
| **Representation** | Bag-of-Words, TF-IDF vectors | Dense embeddings (BERT, DPR) |
| **Indexing** | Inverted Index (term → postings) | FAISS, HNSW, ScaNN (approximate NN) |
| **Scoring** | Cosine similarity, BM25, BM25F | Dot product, dual-encoder, cross-encoder |
| **Strength** | Exact lexical match, fast, interpretable | Semantic match, robust to paraphrasing |

### 1.4 Formal Components of an IR System

A modern IR system can be decomposed into five logical components:

1. **Crawler / Document Acquisition** — fetches raw text from web/corpus.
2. **Indexer** — tokenizes, normalizes, and builds an **Inverted Index**.
3. **Query Processor** — transforms the user's natural-language query into the same representation space as documents.
4. **Scoring/Ranking Module** — computes $S(q, d)$ and produces a ranked list.
5. **Evaluation Module** — measures system quality using **Precision, Recall, F-Measure, MAP, NDCG, MRR**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Students Must Memorize:** The five primary evaluation metrics in KTU Module 3 are **Precision (P), Recall (R), F-Measure (F), Mean Average Precision (MAP), and Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG)**. Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) is also frequently tested.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Term Weighting: The Core Intuition

**Term Weighting** assigns a numerical importance to each term within a document and across the corpus. The rationale is straightforward: not all words carry equal information. The word *"the"* appears almost everywhere and tells us nothing; the word *"quantum"* appearing once in a paper is highly discriminative.

#### 2.1.1 Term Frequency (TF)

The simplest weighting is raw term frequency:

$$tf(t, d) = f_{t,d}$$

where $f_{t,d}$ is the number of times term $t$ appears in document $d$.

A more robust variant is the **logarithmically normalized TF** (used by most modern systems):

$$tf(t, d) = \begin{cases} 1 + \log_{10}(f_{t,d}) & \text{if } f_{t,d} > 0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why log normalization?** A term appearing 100 times is not 100× more important than a term appearing once. Log dampens the effect of term burstiness.

#### 2.1.2 Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)

The IDF measures how *rare* a term is across the entire corpus:

$$idf(t) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{N}{df_t}\right)$$

where $N$ is the total number of documents and $df_t$ is the number of documents containing term $t$.

The **smoothed IDF** (commonly used in practice to avoid division-by-zero and log-of-zero):

$$idf(t) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{N + 1}{df_t + 1}\right) + 1$$

#### 2.1.3 TF-IDF (The Classical Champion)

The product combines local importance (TF) with global discriminativeness (IDF):

$$w_{t,d} = tf(t, d) \cdot idf(t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** Students must clearly distinguish *raw TF*, *log-normalized TF*, *smoothed IDF*, and *TF-IDF*. Examiners commonly give a small corpus and ask for the full TF-IDF vector of a document.

#### 2.1.4 BM25 (Best Match 25) — The Industry Standard

BM25 is a probabilistic relevance framework and the de facto ranking function in classical search engines (Elasticsearch, Lucene, Solr). Its scoring formula is:

$$S(q, d) = \sum_{t \in q} idf(t) \cdot \frac{f_{t,d} \cdot (k_1 + 1)}{f_{t,d} + k_1 \cdot \left(1 - b + b \cdot \frac{\vert d \vert}{avgdl}\right)}$$

where:
- $k_1 \in [1.2, 2.0]$ — term frequency saturation parameter
- $b \in [0, 1]$ — document length normalization (typically $b = 0.75$)
- $\vert d \vert$ — document length (in words)
- $avgdl$ — average document length in the corpus

### 2.2 Document Scoring Models

#### 2.2.1 Vector Space Model (VSM)

Documents and queries are represented as vectors in a high-dimensional term space $\mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert}$, where $\vert V \vert$ is the vocabulary size. Scoring is the **cosine similarity**:

$$S(q, d) = \cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d}}{\vert\vec{q}\vert \cdot \vert\vec{d}\vert} = \frac{\sum_{t \in V} w_{t,q} \cdot w_{t,d}}{\sqrt{\sum_t w_{t,q}^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_t w_{t,d}^2}}$$

#### 2.2.2 Probabilistic Model (BM25 above) and Language Model

The **Query Likelihood Model** scores a document as the probability that the query was *generated* by a document language model, typically with Dirichlet smoothing:

$$S(q, d) = \sum_{t \in q} \log\left((1-\lambda) \cdot \frac{f_{t,d}}{\vert d \vert} + \lambda \cdot \frac{cf_t}{CS}\right)$$

where $cf_t$ is the corpus frequency of $t$, $CS$ is the total collection size, and $\lambda$ is the smoothing parameter.

### 2.3 The Inverted Index — The Backbone of Lexical IR

An **Inverted Index** is the data structure that maps each term in the vocabulary to a *postings list* — the list of documents containing that term along with positional and frequency information.

**Logical structure:**

$$\text{term} \rightarrow \langle df_t, \text{Postings List: } [(d_1, f_{t,d_1}, [p_1, p_2, \ldots]), (d_2, f_{t,d_2}, [\ldots]), \ldots] \rangle$$

**Components:**
- **Lexicon (Dictionary):** sorted list of all unique terms.
- **Postings File:** for each term, the list of $(docID, TF, [positions])$ tuples.
- **Skip pointers** and **variable-byte / Elias-Gamma / Roaring Bitmap compression** for efficiency.

### 2.4 Neural IR

**Neural Information Retrieval** replaces the hand-engineered lexical match with **learned dense representations**. Both query and document are encoded into a $d$-dimensional vector using a neural network, and relevance is computed as a dot product or cosine similarity in this dense space.

**Two main families:**

1. **Dual-Encoder (Bi-Encoder) Architecture:** Query and document are encoded *independently*. Scoring = similarity($E_Q(q), E_D(d)$). Highly efficient, supports precomputation of document vectors (e.g., **DPR — Dense Passage Retrieval**).
2. **Cross-Encoder Architecture:** Query and document are concatenated and fed *jointly* through a Transformer; a single relevance score is output. More accurate but computationally expensive (no precomputation).

### 2.5 Transformer-Based IR Models

The introduction of **BERT** (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) in 2018 catalyzed modern IR. Key milestones include:

- **BERT Maximal Marginal Relevance (monoBERT, monoT5):** cross-encoder rerankers.
- **DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020):** dual-encoder dense retriever trained with in-batch negatives.
- **ColBERT (Khattab & Zaharia, 2020):** late-interaction model using token-level embeddings and MaxSim.
- **ANCE (Xiong et al., 2020):** uses asynchronously refreshed hard negatives.
- **SPLADE:** sparse expansion model that mimics lexical matching with BERT-derived weights.

### 2.6 Evaluation of IR Systems

#### 2.6.1 Precision and Recall

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{\text{Relevant Retrieved}}{\text{Total Retrieved}}$$

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{\text{Relevant Retrieved}}{\text{Total Relevant}}$$

#### 2.6.2 F-Measure (Harmonic Mean)

$$F_\beta = \frac{(1 + \beta^2) \cdot P \cdot R}{\beta^2 \cdot P + R}$$

The most common form is $F_1$ (with $\beta = 1$):

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$$

#### 2.6.3 Mean Average Precision (MAP)

For a single query:

$$AP = \frac{1}{\vert R \vert} \sum_{k=1}^{n} P(k) \cdot rel(k)$$

where $P(k)$ is the precision at rank $k$ and $rel(k) \in \{0, 1\}$ is the relevance label. MAP is the mean of $AP$ across all queries.

#### 2.6.4 Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG)

$$DCG@k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{2^{rel_i} - 1}{\log_2(i + 1)}$$

$$NDCG@k = \frac{DCG@k}{IDCG@k}$$

where $IDCG@k$ is the DCG of the *ideal* ranking (relevant documents sorted by relevance).

#### 2.6.5 Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR)

$$MRR = \frac{1}{\vert Q \vert} \sum_{i=1}^{\vert Q \vert} \frac{1}{rank_i}$$

where $rank_i$ is the position of the first relevant document for query $i$.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Symbol | Description | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $tf(t,d) = 1 + \log_{10}(f_{t,d})$ | $f_{t,d}$ | Log-normalized term frequency | $[0, +\infty)$ |
| $idf(t) = \log_{10}(N/df_t)$ | $N, df_t$ | Inverse document frequency | $[0, \log_{10} N]$ |
| $w_{t,d} = tf(t,d) \cdot idf(t)$ | — | TF-IDF weight | $[0, +\infty)$ |
| $\cos(\vec{q}, \vec{d})$ | — | Cosine similarity (VSM) | $[-1, 1]$, typically $[0, 1]$ |
| BM25 score | $k_1, b$ | Probabilistic relevance | $k_1 \approx 1.2$, $b \approx 0.75$ |
| $P = TP/(TP+FP)$ | — | Precision | $[0, 1]$ |
| $R = TP/(TP+FN)$ | — | Recall | $[0, 1]$ |
| $F_1 = 2PR/(P+R)$ | — | Harmonic mean | $[0, 1]$ |
| $AP = \frac{1}{\vert R \vert}\sum P(k) \cdot rel(k)$ | — | Average Precision per query | $[0, 1]$ |
| $NDCG@k = DCG@k / IDCG@k$ | $k$ | Normalized DCG at cutoff $k$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| $MRR = \frac{1}{\vert Q \vert} \sum 1/rank_i$ | — | Mean Reciprocal Rank | $[0, 1]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Markdown Note on the BM25 Equation:** In the BM25 formula above, the notation $\vert d \vert$ and $avgdl$ uses vertical bars as **absolute value / length** symbols. In any markdown table, **always escape vertical bars as $\vert$ or $\mid$** to prevent the table from breaking. This is a common mistake students make in their own notes.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Web Search Engines (Google, Bing):** BM25 is still the first-stage retriever before neural reranking.
- **E-Commerce (Amazon, Flipkart):** product search uses hybrid lexical + dense retrieval.
- **Enterprise Search (Elasticsearch):** built on Lucene's BM25 implementation.
- **Legal and Patent Search:** high-recall requirements, often pure BM25.
- **Conversational AI / RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation):** modern LLMs use dense retrievers (DPR, ColBERT, E5) to fetch context before generation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Constructing a TF-IDF Vector

**Given Corpus (3 documents):**
- $d_1$ = *"the cat sat on the mat"*
- $d_2$ = *"the dog sat on the rug"*
- $d_3$ = *"the cat and the dog played"*

**Query:** $q$ = *"cat dog"*

#### Step 1 — Tokenization and Vocabulary

After lowercasing and stop-word filtering, the relevant vocabulary is:

$$V = \{\text{cat, dog, sat, mat, rug, played}\}$$

(Removing "the", "on", "and" as stop-words.)

#### Step 2 — Term Frequencies

| Term | $f_{t,d_1}$ | $f_{t,d_2}$ | $f_{t,d_3}$ | $df_t$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| cat | 1 | 0 | 1 | 2 |
| dog | 0 | 1 | 1 | 2 |
| sat | 1 | 1 | 0 | 2 |
| mat | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| rug | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| played | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 |

Total documents $N = 3$.

#### Step 3 — Compute IDF (raw)

$$idf(t) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{N}{df_t}\right)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
idf(\text{cat}) &= \log_{10}(3/2) = 0.1761 \\
idf(\text{dog}) &= \log_{10}(3/2) = 0.1761 \\
idf(\text{sat}) &= \log_{10}(3/2) = 0.1761 \\
idf(\text{mat}) &= \log_{10}(3/1) = 0.4771 \\
idf(\text{rug}) &= \log_{10}(3/1) = 0.4771 \\
idf(\text{played}) &= \log_{10}(3/1) = 0.4771
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Compute TF-IDF Weights

Using $w_{t,d} = tf(t,d) \cdot idf(t)$ with raw TF (since values are small):

For $d_1$ = {cat, sat, mat}:
$$w_{\text{cat},d_1} = 1 \cdot 0.1761 = 0.1761$$
$$w_{\text{sat},d_1} = 1 \cdot 0.1761 = 0.1761$$
$$w_{\text{mat},d_1} = 1 \cdot 0.4771 = 0.4771$$

For $d_2$ = {dog, sat, rug}:
$$w_{\text{dog},d_2} = 1 \cdot 0.1761 = 0.1761$$
$$w_{\text{sat},d_2} = 1 \cdot 0.1761 = 0.1761$$
$$w_{\text{rug},d_2} = 1 \cdot 0.4771 = 0.4771$$

For $d_3$ = {cat, dog, played}:
$$w_{\text{cat},d_3} = 1 \cdot 0.1761 = 0.1761$$
$$w_{\text{dog},d_3} = 1 \cdot 0.1761 = 0.1761$$
$$w_{\text{played},d_3} = 1 \cdot 0.4771 = 0.4771$$

#### Step 5 — Compute Cosine Similarity Between $q$ and Each Document

Query $q$ = {cat, dog}, so $w_{\text{cat},q} = 0.1761$, $w_{\text{dog},q} = 0.1761$.

**Magnitude of $\vec{q}$:**

$$|\vec{q}| = \sqrt{0.1761^2 + 0.1761^2} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 0.0310} = \sqrt{0.0620} = 0.2491$$

**Magnitude of $\vec{d_1}$:**

$$|\vec{d_1}| = \sqrt{0.1761^2 + 0.1761^2 + 0.4771^2} = \sqrt{0.0310 + 0.0310 + 0.2276} = \sqrt{0.2896} = 0.5382$$

**Dot product $q \cdot d_1$:** only the term *"cat"* matches.

$$q \cdot d_1 = 0.1761 \cdot 0.1761 = 0.0310$$

$$\cos(q, d_1) = \frac{0.0310}{0.2491 \cdot 0.5382} = \frac{0.0310}{0.1341} = 0.2314$$

**Dot product $q \cdot d_2$:** only the term *"dog"* matches.

$$q \cdot d_2 = 0.1761 \cdot 0.1761 = 0.0310$$

$$\cos(q, d_2) = \frac{0.0310}{0.2491 \cdot 0.5382} = 0.2314$$

**Dot product $q \cdot d_3$:** both *"cat"* and *"dog"* match.

$$q \cdot d_3 = 0.1761 \cdot 0.1761 + 0.1761 \cdot 0.1761 = 2 \cdot 0.0310 = 0.0620$$

$$|\vec{d_3}| = \sqrt{0.1761^2 + 0.1761^2 + 0.4771^2} = 0.5382$$

$$\cos(q, d_3) = \frac{0.0620}{0.2491 \cdot 0.5382} = \frac{0.0620}{0.1341} = 0.4628$$

#### Step 6 — Final Ranked List

| Rank | Document | Cosine Similarity |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d_3$ | **0.4628** |
| 2 (tie) | $d_1$ | 0.2314 |
| 2 (tie) | $d_2$ | 0.2314 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $d_3$ ranks highest because it contains *both* query terms, while $d_1$ and $d_2$ each contain only one. The classical VSM correctly captures the intuition that more query-term overlap = higher relevance.

### 3.2 Worked Example: BM25 Scoring

**Same corpus, query $q$ = "cat dog".** Assume $k_1 = 1.2$, $b = 0.75$, $|d_1| = |d_2| = |d_3| = 3$, $avgdl = 3$.

**Step 1 — Length normalization factor** (since all lengths equal $avgdl$):

$$1 - b + b \cdot \frac{|d|}{avgdl} = 1 - 0.75 + 0.75 \cdot 1 = 1$$

**Step 2 — Score for $d_1$ (contains "cat", $f_{\text{cat},d_1}=1$; "dog" not present):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(q, d_1) &= idf(\text{cat}) \cdot \frac{1 \cdot (1.2 + 1)}{1 + 1.2 \cdot 1} \\
&= 0.1761 \cdot \frac{2.2}{2.2} \\
&= 0.1761 \cdot 1.0 = 0.1761
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Score for $d_3$ (contains both "cat" and "dog" with $f=1$ each):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(q, d_3) &= 0.1761 \cdot 1.0 \;(\text{cat}) + 0.1761 \cdot 1.0 \;(\text{dog}) \\
&= 0.1761 + 0.1761 = 0.3522
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $d_3$ scores twice as high as $d_1$, consistent with the cosine result.

### 3.3 Worked Example: NDCG@k Calculation

**System's Top-4 Ranking for a query:**
Rank 1: Relevant grade 3
Rank 2: Relevant grade 2
Rank 3: Not relevant (0)
Rank 4: Relevant grade 1

**Step 1 — Compute DCG@4** (using $2^{rel_i} - 1$ gain, $\log_2(i+1)$ discount):

$$
\begin{aligned}
DCG@4 &= \frac{2^3 - 1}{\log_2 2} + \frac{2^2 - 1}{\log_2 3} + \frac{2^0 - 1}{\log_2 4} + \frac{2^1 - 1}{\log_2 5} \\
&= \frac{7}{1} + \frac{3}{1.585} + \frac{0}{2} + \frac{1}{2.322} \\
&= 7.000 + 1.893 + 0.000 + 0.431 \\
&= 9.324
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute IDCG@4** (ideal ranking: 3, 2, 1, 0):

$$
\begin{aligned}
IDCG@4 &= \frac{7}{1} + \frac{3}{1.585} + \frac{1}{2.322} + \frac{0}{2} \\
&= 7.000 + 1.893 + 0.431 + 0.000 \\
&= 9.324
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — NDCG@4:**

$$NDCG@4 = \frac{9.324}{9.324} = 1.000$$

A perfect ranking — the system is doing optimally at this cutoff.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Classical vs Neural IR

| Dimension | Classical (BM25, TF-IDF) | Neural (DPR, ColBERT, BERT) |
|---|---|---|
| **Representation** | Sparse, high-dimensional | Dense, low-dimensional (e.g., 768-d) |
| **Vocabulary Mismatch** | Fails (no shared words) | Handles via semantics |
| **Speed** | Very fast (inverted index lookup) | Slower (Transformer encoding) |
| **Memory** | Compact | Large (must store dense vectors) |
| **Training Data** | None (unsupervised) | Requires large query-doc pairs |
| **Interpretability** | High (term-level inspection) | Low (black box) |
| **Out-of-domain Robustness** | Strong | Weaker (fine-tuning helps) |
| **Indexing Cost** | One-time, fast | Expensive (BERT forward pass) |
| **Best Use** | First-stage retrieval | Reranking, second-stage |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Architecture of a Modern (Hybrid) IR System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Raw Corpus of Documents] --> B[Document Preprocessing]
    B --> B1[Tokenization]
    B1 --> B2[Stopword Removal]
    B2 --> B3[Stemming and Lemmatization]
    B3 --> C1[Sparse Representation]
    B3 --> C2[Dense Representation via BERT Encoder]
    C1 --> D1[Inverted Index Construction]
    C2 --> D2[FAISS Vector Index]
    D1 --> E[First Stage Retrieval BM25]
    D2 --> E
    Q[User Query] --> Q1[Query Preprocessing]
    Q1 --> E
    E --> F[Top K Candidate Documents]
    F --> G[Neural Reranker Cross Encoder]
    G --> H[Final Ranked Result List]
    H --> U[User Interface]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A modern hybrid IR system typically uses a *two-stage* pipeline. Stage 1 uses fast lexical retrieval (BM25 over an inverted index) and dense retrieval (BERT-encoder into FAISS) in parallel. Stage 2 reranks the top-K candidates using a cross-encoder for maximum accuracy.

### 4.2 Inverted Index Construction — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Document Collection] --> B[Tokenizer]
    B --> C[Normalizer lowercase, stemming]
    C --> D[Tokenizer Output Stream of terms]
    D --> E[Indexer Module]
    E --> F[Sort by term and then by docID]
    F --> G[Build Lexicon and Postings]
    G --> H[Compressed Inverted Index File]
    H --> I[Serve Query lookups via postings intersection]
```

### 4.3 Neural Dual-Encoder vs Cross-Encoder — Functional Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DE[Dual Encoder Pipeline Efficient]
        Q1[Query q] --> QE[BERT Query Encoder]
        DE1[Document d] --> DE[DOC BERT Doc Encoder]
        QE --> VS1[Vector Space Similarity Dot Product]
        DE --> VS1
        VS1 --> RS1[Relevance Score]
    end
    subgraph CE[Cross Encoder Pipeline Accurate]
        Q2[Query q] --> CON[Concatenate with q and d]
        DE2[Document d] --> CON
        CON --> BERT2[Single BERT Joint Encoding]
        BERT2 --> CLS[CLS Head Output]
        CLS --> RS2[Relevance Score]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operational difference:** In the dual-encoder, document embeddings are precomputed and stored, so query time is a single fast vector lookup (millions of docs in milliseconds). In the cross-encoder, query and document must be re-encoded together for *every* candidate, which is $100\times$ to $1000\times$ slower — but more accurate.

### 4.4 BM25 Scoring Components — Functional Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[Query terms t1 t2 t3] --> IDF[IDF Lookup per term]
    IDF --> M1[Multiply by TF saturation factor]
    D1[f_td and document length d] --> M1
    M1 --> SUM[Sum over all query terms]
    SUM --> SCORE[BM25 Score per document]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style, Module 3]**

Define the **Inverted Index** in Information Retrieval. With a suitable example, explain how it enables fast query processing.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Inverted Index** is a data structure that maps each unique term in a document collection to the list of documents containing that term, along with frequency and positional information (postings list). It is called "inverted" because it inverts the natural document-to-term relationship into a term-to-document mapping.

**Example:** For a corpus with documents $d_1$ = "cat sat", $d_2$ = "dog sat", $d_3$ = "cat dog", the inverted index is:

- cat → $\{d_1: 1, d_3: 1\}$
- sat → $\{d_1: 1, d_2: 1\}$
- dog → $\{d_2: 1, d_3: 1\}$

To process query *"cat dog"*, the system retrieves the postings lists for *cat* and *dog*, intersects them to find common documents, and returns the result — without scanning every document. This enables **sub-linear-time** query processing. **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for example, 1 for speed/explanation)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style, Module 3]**

Differentiate between **Precision** and **Recall** in IR evaluation. Why is the **F1-Measure** often preferred over either metric alone?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Precision (P)** is the fraction of retrieved documents that are relevant:
$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$.

**Recall (R)** is the fraction of relevant documents that are retrieved:
$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$.

**Why F1 is preferred:** Precision and Recall are often in **tension** — increasing one decreases the other. F1 is the **harmonic mean** of P and R:
$F_1 = \frac{2PR}{P+R}$.
Unlike the arithmetic mean, the harmonic mean is **low when either P or R is low**, ensuring a balanced measure. For example, a system with $P=1.0$ and $R=0.01$ has an arithmetic mean of 0.505 (misleadingly high) but an F1 of only 0.02 (correctly indicating poor performance). **(3 marks: 1 for each definition, 1 for F1 explanation)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — TF-IDF & Inverted Index Numerical**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3 Pattern]**

(a) Consider the following mini-corpus of 4 documents:
- $d_1$ = *"machine learning is powerful"*
- $d_2$ = *"deep learning is a branch of machine learning"*
- $d_3$ = *"neural networks learn from data"*
- $d_4$ = *"data science uses statistics and learning"*

Construct the **complete inverted index** for this corpus (with term frequencies). **(7 marks)**

(b) For the query $q$ = *"machine learning"*, compute the **TF-IDF vector** for each document and the **cosine similarity** between $q$ and each document. Rank the documents. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Inverted Index Construction — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Tokenize and normalize (lowercase, remove stop-words is NOT required unless specified).** Vocabulary (excluding stop-words "is", "a", "of", "from", "and"):

$$V = \{\text{machine, learning, powerful, deep, branch, neural, networks, learn, data, science, uses, statistics}\}$$

**Step 2: Build postings list (term → docs with frequency):**

| Term | Postings (docID, TF) |
|---|---|
| machine | (1,1), (2,1) |
| learning | (1,1), (2,2), (4,1) |
| powerful | (1,1) |
| deep | (2,1) |
| branch | (2,1) |
| neural | (3,1) |
| networks | (3,1) |
| learn | (3,1) |
| data | (3,1), (4,1) |
| science | (4,1) |
| uses | (4,1) |
| statistics | (4,1) |

Document frequencies: $df_{\text{machine}} = 2$, $df_{\text{learning}} = 3$, $df_{\text{deep}} = 1$, $df_{\text{data}} = 2$, etc. $N = 4$.

**(b) TF-IDF Vectors & Cosine — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Compute IDF** for query terms:
$$idf(\text{machine}) = \log_{10}(4/2) = \log_{10}(2) = 0.3010$$
$$idf(\text{learning}) = \log_{10}(4/3) = 0.1249$$

**Step 2 — TF-IDF weights:**

| Document | $w_{\text{machine},d}$ | $w_{\text{learning},d}$ |
|---|---|---|
| $d_1$ | $1 \cdot 0.3010 = 0.3010$ | $1 \cdot 0.1249 = 0.1249$ |
| $d_2$ | $1 \cdot 0.3010 = 0.3010$ | $2 \cdot 0.1249 = 0.2498$ |
| $d_3$ | 0 | 0 |
| $d_4$ | 0 | $1 \cdot 0.1249 = 0.1249$ |

**Step 3 — Query vector $q$:** $w_{\text{machine},q} = 0.3010$, $w_{\text{learning},q} = 0.1249$.

$$|\vec{q}| = \sqrt{0.3010^2 + 0.1249^2} = \sqrt{0.0906 + 0.0156} = \sqrt{0.1062} = 0.3259$$

**Step 4 — Document magnitudes and dot products:**

$|\vec{d_1}| = \sqrt{0.3010^2 + 0.1249^2} = 0.3259$; $q \cdot d_1 = 0.3010^2 + 0.1249^2 = 0.1062$
$$\cos(q, d_1) = \frac{0.1062}{0.3259 \cdot 0.3259} = \frac{0.1062}{0.1062} = 1.000$$

$|\vec{d_2}| = \sqrt{0.3010^2 + 0.2498^2} = \sqrt{0.0906 + 0.0624} = \sqrt{0.1530} = 0.3911$
$q \cdot d_2 = 0.3010 \cdot 0.3010 + 0.1249 \cdot 0.2498 = 0.0906 + 0.0312 = 0.1218$
$$\cos(q, d_2) = \frac{0.1218}{0.3259 \cdot 0.3911} = \frac{0.1218}{0.1275} = 0.9556$$

$|\vec{d_3}| = 0$ (no overlap, cosine undefined / = 0)

$|\vec{d_4}| = 0.1249$; $q \cdot d_4 = 0 \cdot 0.3010 + 0.1249 \cdot 0.1249 = 0.0156$
$$\cos(q, d_4) = \frac{0.0156}{0.3259 \cdot 0.1249} = \frac{0.0156}{0.0407} = 0.3834$$

**Step 5 — Final Ranking:**

| Rank | Document | Cosine |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d_1$ | **1.000** |
| 2 | $d_2$ | 0.9556 |
| 3 | $d_4$ | 0.3834 |
| 4 | $d_3$ | 0 |

**Interpretation:** $d_1$ scores highest because it contains both query terms *with single occurrence*. $d_2$ is close — it also has both terms, but *learning* appears twice (no log dampening used in raw TF) which actually *reduces* cosine slightly by length-normalization asymmetry. $d_4$ matches only *learning*. $d_3$ has no query terms. **(Full 14 marks distributed across (a) and (b))**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — BM25 + NDCG Evaluation**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Pattern, Module 3]**

(a) Explain the **BM25 ranking function** in detail. List the roles of parameters $k_1$ and $b$ in the formula. Use BM25 to score the following two documents for the query $q$ = *"information retrieval"*, given $N = 1000$, $df_{\text{information}} = 50$, $df_{\text{retrieval}} = 30$, $f_{\text{information},d_1} = 2$, $f_{\text{retrieval},d_1} = 1$, $f_{\text{information},d_2} = 1$, $f_{\text{retrieval},d_2} = 2$, $|d_1| = |d_2| = avgdl = 100$, $k_1 = 1.2$, $b = 0.75$. **(7 marks)**

(b) For the following ranked list of 5 documents returned by an IR system, compute the **NDCG@5**, given the relevance grades (4 = highly relevant, 3 = relevant, 2 = partially relevant, 1 = marginally relevant, 0 = not relevant):

| Rank | Document | Relevance Grade |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D_1$ | 3 |
| 2 | $D_2$ | 0 |
| 3 | $D_3$ | 2 |
| 4 | $D_4$ | 3 |
| 5 | $D_5$ | 1 |

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) BM25 Detailed Explanation + Computation — 7 marks**

**Theory (3 marks):** BM25 (Best Match 25) is a probabilistic ranking function derived from the Binary Independence Model. It scores a document $d$ for query $q$ by summing per-term contributions, where each contribution combines **IDF** (term rarity) with a **saturating TF** function and **document-length normalization**.

**Role of parameters:**
- $k_1$: controls how quickly TF saturates. Small $k_1$ means TF has less effect; large $k_1$ allows TF to boost the score more. Typical: $k_1 \in [1.2, 2.0]$.
- $b$: controls length normalization. $b = 0$ means no length normalization; $b = 1$ means full normalization by ratio $|d|/avgdl$. Typical: $b = 0.75$.

**Computation (4 marks):**

Length-normalization factor for both docs (since $|d| = avgdl$):
$$1 - b + b \cdot \frac{|d|}{avgdl} = 1 - 0.75 + 0.75 \cdot 1 = 1.00$$

**IDF values** (using $\log_{10}(N/df_t)$):
$$idf(\text{information}) = \log_{10}(1000/50) = \log_{10}(20) = 1.3010$$
$$idf(\text{retrieval}) = \log_{10}(1000/30) = \log_{10}(33.33) = 1.5229$$

**Score for $d_1$:**

$$S(q, d_1) = 1.3010 \cdot \frac{2 \cdot 2.2}{2 + 1.2 \cdot 1} + 1.5229 \cdot \frac{1 \cdot 2.2}{1 + 1.2 \cdot 1}$$
$$= 1.3010 \cdot \frac{4.4}{3.2} + 1.5229 \cdot \frac{2.2}{2.2}$$
$$= 1.3010 \cdot 1.375 + 1.5229 \cdot 1.0$$
$$= 1.7889 + 1.5229 = 3.3118$$

**Score for $d_2$:**

$$S(q, d_2) = 1.3010 \cdot \frac{1 \cdot 2.2}{1 + 1.2 \cdot 1} + 1.5229 \cdot \frac{2 \cdot 2.2}{2 + 1.2 \cdot 1}$$
$$= 1.3010 \cdot 1.0 + 1.5229 \cdot \frac{4.4}{3.2}$$
$$= 1.3010 + 1.5229 \cdot 1.375$$
$$= 1.3010 + 2.0940 = 3.3950$$

**Result:** $d_2$ (3.3950) ranks slightly higher than $d_1$ (3.3118) because BM25 rewards the rarer term *"retrieval"* (higher IDF) appearing twice, more than it rewards *"information"* appearing twice.

---

**(b) NDCG@5 Computation — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Compute DCG@5** using gain $2^{rel_i} - 1$ and discount $\log_2(i+1)$:

| Rank $i$ | $rel_i$ | $2^{rel_i} - 1$ | $\log_2(i+1)$ | Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3 | 7 | 1.000 | 7.000 |
| 2 | 0 | 0 | 1.585 | 0.000 |
| 3 | 2 | 3 | 2.000 | 1.500 |
| 4 | 3 | 7 | 2.322 | 3.015 |
| 5 | 1 | 1 | 2.585 | 0.387 |

$$DCG@5 = 7.000 + 0.000 + 1.500 + 3.015 + 0.387 = 11.902$$

**Step 2 — Compute IDCG@5** (ideal ranking = sort relevance descending: 3, 3, 2, 1, 0):

| Rank $i$ | $rel_i$ (ideal) | $2^{rel_i} - 1$ | $\log_2(i+1)$ | Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3 | 7 | 1.000 | 7.000 |
| 2 | 3 | 7 | 1.585 | 4.416 |
| 3 | 2 | 3 | 2.000 | 1.500 |
| 4 | 1 | 1 | 2.322 | 0.431 |
| 5 | 0 | 0 | 2.585 | 0.000 |

$$IDCG@5 = 7.000 + 4.416 + 1.500 + 0.431 + 0.000 = 13.347$$

**Step 3 — NDCG@5:**

$$NDCG@5 = \frac{DCG@5}{IDCG@5} = \frac{11.902}{13.347} = 0.8918$$

**Interpretation:** A score of **0.8918** indicates a near-ideal ranking. The system placed one of the two grade-3 documents at rank 1, demoted a non-relevant document to rank 2 (a flaw), but recovered with the other grade-3 at rank 4. **(Full 14 marks distributed across (a) and (b))**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to log-normalize TF:** If raw TF is used in VSM, longer documents with repeated terms are unfairly favored. Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **Wrong IDF base:** KTU expects $\log_{10}$ unless specifically asked for natural log. Mixing bases = 1 mark lost.
> 3. **Missing $\log_2(i+1)$ in NDCG discount:** Some students use $1/i$ or $\log_e$. This is wrong; the standard NDCG uses $\log_2$. Deduct 1 mark.
> 4. **Not computing IDCG:** NDCG *requires* IDCG normalization. Forgetting IDCG and reporting raw DCG = 2 marks lost.
> 5. **Confusing F1 with arithmetic mean:** F1 is the **harmonic** mean. Writing $(P+R)/2$ = 1.5 marks lost.
> 6. **Confusing dual-encoder and cross-encoder:** A common trick question. Dual = independent encoding (fast), Cross = joint encoding (accurate). Mixing them = 2 marks lost.
> 7. **Inverted Index example:** Students often forget to include **term frequencies** in the postings list, which loses 1–2 marks.
> 8. **No code expected:** Do NOT write Python/Java code in IR theory questions. Use mathematical notation. KTU deducts marks for pseudocode in non-programming questions.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 — Information Retrieval: The 12-Point Revision Checklist**

1. **Information Retrieval (IR)** = finding relevant documents for a query from a large collection. Output = *ranked list*, not just a set.
2. **Inverted Index** = term-to-postings-list mapping. The core data structure for fast lexical retrieval.
3. **Term Frequency (TF)** = $f_{t,d}$; **Log-normalized TF** = $1 + \log_{10}(f_{t,d})$. The latter dampens the burstiness effect.
4. **Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)** = $\log_{10}(N/df_t)$ — rewards rare terms, penalizes common ones.
5. **TF-IDF** = $tf(t,d) \cdot idf(t)$ — the classical sparse weighting scheme.
6. **Vector Space Model (VSM)** = documents and queries as vectors; relevance = cosine similarity in $[-1, 1]$.
7. **BM25** = probabilistic ranking with TF saturation ($k_1$) and length normalization ($b$). Industry default. Parameters: $k_1 \approx 1.2$, $b \approx 0.75$.
8. **Neural IR** = dense vector representations via neural encoders (BERT, etc.). Two main architectures: **dual-encoder** (efficient) and **cross-encoder** (accurate).
9. **Transformer-Based IR** = DPR, ColBERT, monoBERT, monoT5, SPLADE, ANCE, E5. These are the state-of-the-art on benchmark IR datasets (MS MARCO, BEIR).
10. **Precision** = $TP/(TP+FP)$; **Recall** = $TP/(TP+FN)$; **F1** = $2PR/(P+R)$ — the harmonic balance metric.
11. **MAP** = mean of Average Precision across queries. Rewards returning relevant documents *early* in the ranking.
12. **NDCG@k** = $DCG@k / IDCG@k$ with gain $2^{rel_i}-1$ and discount $\log_2(i+1)$. Use it for *graded* relevance. **MRR** = reciprocal rank of first relevant doc, averaged.

**Syllabus-mandated keywords for Module 3 IR (must recognize in any KTU paper):**
> Term Weighting • TF-IDF • BM25 • Cosine Similarity • Vector Space Model • Inverted Index • Postings List • Skip Pointers • Lexicon • Neural IR • Dual Encoder • Cross Encoder • DPR • ColBERT • BERT Reranking • Precision • Recall • F-Measure • MAP • NDCG • MRR • MS MARCO • BEIR benchmark.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: QA Systems, Machine Translation, and Large Language Models: Question-Answering Systems - Transformer-Based QA Models, Open-Domain QA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT601 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** QA Systems, Machine Translation, and Large Language Models: Question-Answering Systems - Transformer-Based QA Models, Open-Domain QA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Question-Answering (QA) Systems — Formal Definition

A **Question-Answering (QA) System** is a specialized branch of Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Information Retrieval (IR) that aims to automatically answer questions posed by humans in natural language by either retrieving relevant evidence from a structured/unstructured corpus, generating answers from parametric memory, or performing a hybrid combination of both.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCMT601 — Module 4)** syllabus, QA Systems are positioned alongside **Machine Translation (MT)** and **Large Language Models (LLMs)** as the three converging pillars of modern generative and discriminative language understanding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Examiner Definition (Use this verbatim in 14-mark answers):**
> *"A Question-Answering system is an information retrieval and language understanding framework that, given a natural language question `q` and (optionally) a context `C`, produces a precise answer `a` in the form of an extractive span, an abstractive text, a structured tuple, or a generated response, with an associated confidence score."*

### 1.2 Taxonomy of QA Systems

QA systems can be classified along multiple orthogonal axes. The KTU syllabus specifically emphasizes **Transformer-based** and **Open-Domain** variants, but a complete understanding requires knowing the full landscape:

| Axis | Categories | KTU Relevance |
|------|-----------|---------------|
| **Domain Scope** | Closed-domain vs **Open-domain** | Core Module-4 focus |
| **Answer Type** | Extractive vs Abstractive vs Generative | High — covers all three |
| **Knowledge Source** | Textual, Knowledge-base, Hybrid, Parametric (LLM) | Core |
| **Architecture** | Retriever-only, Reader-only, Retriever-Reader (RAG) | Core |
| **Model Backbone** | LSTM-based, BERT-based, **Transformer-based**, LLM-based | Core |

## 1.3 Transformer-Based QA Models — The Intuitive Overview

A **Transformer-based QA model** is a QA system whose encoder (and optionally decoder) is built using the **Transformer architecture** introduced in *Vaswani et al., 2017 (NeurIPS) — "Attention Is All You Need"*. Models like **BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers)**, **RoBERTa**, **ALBERT**, **DistilBERT**, and **T5** have become the de facto backbones for almost all modern QA pipelines.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The "Librarian with Perfect Memory"

> [!NOTE]
> **The Librarian Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine you walk into a massive library containing **millions of books** and ask the librarian: *"Who was the first person to walk on the Moon?"*
>
> 1. **Traditional IR systems (pre-Transformer):** The librarian uses **keywords** like "first" + "Moon" + "walk". They find books, but must *manually read* and summarize. Slow and error-prone.
>
> 2. **Transformer-based QA (e.g., BERT):** The librarian has **read every book in the library** (pre-training), understands **context bidirectionally** (sees the words before AND after), and can directly *point to the exact sentence* containing the answer — like highlighting the relevant line: *"**Neil Armstrong** was the first person to walk on the Moon."*
>
> 3. **Open-Domain QA (e.g., DPR + BERT, or RAG):** The librarian is allowed to **search the entire open internet** in real-time before answering — they don't rely only on memory, they fetch fresh evidence.
>
> The **Transformer** is the *"perfect memory + bidirectional understanding"* part. **Open-domain** is the *"search the whole world"* part. Together, they power modern systems like Google Search's BERT-based ranking and ChatGPT's retrieval-augmented generation.

## 1.4 Open-Domain QA — Formal Definition

**Open-Domain QA (ODQA)** is the task of answering questions on *any topic* (open-domain) without restricting the subject area, typically by leveraging a large-scale external knowledge corpus such as Wikipedia, the open web, or a vector database of documents.

The two dominant paradigms in KTU Module 4 are:

1. **Retriever–Reader Pipeline (DrQA, ORQA, DPR, RAG):** Retrieve relevant documents, then read & extract.
2. **End-to-End Generative QA (REALM, FiD, Atlas, LLMs):** Jointly retrieve and generate answers with a Transformer/LLM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently asked in KTU exams):**
> - **Closed-Domain QA:** Restricted to a specific vertical (e.g., medical QA over PubMed only). Often easier, higher precision.
> - **Open-Domain QA:** Must handle *any* question. Requires massive retrieval corpus, robust to vocabulary mismatch, and is significantly harder.

## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in the KTU 2024 Scheme

- It is the **culmination of Module 4**, integrating retrieval, attention, and generation.
- Direct questions on **Transformer-based QA (BERT, RoBERTa)** and **Open-Domain QA pipelines (DrQA, DPR, RAG)** carry high weightage.
- The **shift from RNN/LSTM to Transformer** in QA is a recurring 7–14 mark question.
- Understanding **Extractive vs Abstractive vs Generative QA** is foundational for LLMs covered later in the module.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Transformer Architecture — Foundation for QA Models

Every Transformer-based QA model is built on three core mechanisms. KTU examiners expect students to articulate each clearly.

### 2.1.1 Self-Attention Mechanism

Given input token embeddings `X ∈ ℝ^{n × d}`, self-attention computes three learned projections:

$$Q = X W^Q, \quad K = X W^K, \quad V = X W^V$$

where `W^Q, W^K ∈ ℝ^{d × d_k}` and `W^V ∈ ℝ^{d × d_v}` are trainable parameter matrices.

The **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** is then:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the `√d_k` scaling?**
> As `d_k` grows, the dot products `QKᵀ` grow in magnitude, pushing the softmax into regions with extremely small gradients. Dividing by `√d_k` keeps the variance of the dot products at 1, stabilizing training.

### 2.1.2 Multi-Head Attention (MHA)

Instead of one attention function, the Transformer runs `h` attention heads in parallel and concatenates:

$$\text{MHA}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h) W^O$$

$$\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(Q W_i^Q, K W_i^K, V W_i^V)$$

This allows the model to attend to information from **different representation subspaces** simultaneously — critical for QA where the question and context may require multiple semantic alignments.

### 2.1.3 Positional Encoding

Since self-attention is **permutation-equivariant**, positional information is injected via sinusoidal or learned encodings:

$$PE_{(pos, 2i)} = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d}}\right)$$

$$PE_{(pos, 2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d}}\right)$$

## 2.2 BERT for Extractive QA — The Workhorse

**BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers)** by Devlin et al. (2018, NAACL) is the most cited model in Transformer-based QA. KTU examiners love it because it cleanly illustrates the extractive QA paradigm.

### Architecture for QA:

Given a question `q` and a context passage `c`, BERT receives a single packed input:

$$\text{Input} = [\text{CLS}]\, q\, [\text{SEP}]\, c\, [\text{EOS}]$$

BERT outputs contextualized embeddings `H ∈ ℝ^{n × d}`. For extractive QA, two lightweight linear heads predict the **start** and **end** token positions:

$$P_{\text{start}}(i) = \frac{\exp\!\left(s_i\right)}{\sum_j \exp\!\left(s_j\right)}, \quad s_i = W_{\text{start}}^{\top} H_i$$

$$P_{\text{end}}(i) = \frac{\exp\!\left(e_i\right)}{\sum_j \exp\!\left(e_j\right)}, \quad e_i = W_{\text{end}}^{\top} H_i$$

The answer span is the contiguous sub-text from `argmax_i P_start(i)` to `argmax_j P_end(j)`, with the constraint `i ≤ j`.

### Fine-Tuning Objective:

The model is fine-tuned on QA datasets like **SQuAD (Stanford Question Answering Dataset)** using cross-entropy loss on the start and end positions:

$$\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}\left[\log P_{\text{start}}(y_s^{(k)}) + \log P_{\text{end}}(y_e^{(k)})\right]$$

## 2.3 RoBERTa, ALBERT, DistilBERT — Optimized Variants

| Model | Key Innovation | QA Impact |
|-------|---------------|-----------|
| **RoBERTa** (Liu et al., 2019) | Removed NSP, more data, dynamic masking, longer training | +1–2 F1 on SQuAD |
| **ALBERT** (Lan et al., 2019) | Factorized embedding + cross-layer parameter sharing | Smaller memory footprint |
| **DistilBERT** (Sanh et al., 2019) | Knowledge distillation from BERT | 40% smaller, 60% faster, retains 97% performance |
| **SpanBERT** (Joshi et al., 2020) | Span-boundary objective during pre-training | Better at span extraction |
| **ELECTRA** (Clark et al., 2020) | Replaced token detection (RTD) pre-training | Sample-efficient |

## 2.4 Generative QA with Seq2Seq Transformers

**T5 (Text-to-Text Transfer Transformer)** and **BART** reformulate QA as sequence-to-sequence generation. The input is `"question: <q> context: <c>"` and the output is the generated answer string.

$$\hat{a} = \arg\max_{a} P_{\theta}(a \mid q, c) = \arg\max_{a}\prod_{t=1}^{T} P_{\theta}(a_t \mid a_{<t}, q, c)$$

This **abstractive** formulation can produce answers that are not present verbatim in the context — essential for synthesizing multi-hop answers.

## 2.5 Open-Domain QA — The Retriever-Reader Paradigm

### 2.5.1 DrQA (Chen et al., 2017) — The Classic Pipeline

DrQA pioneered the **Document Retriever + Neural Reader** architecture:

- **Retriever:** TF-IDF over Wikipedia. Returns top-`k` documents.
- **Reader:** BiLSTM with question-aware attention that predicts answer spans.

$$\text{Score}(q, d) = \sum_{w \in q \cap d} \text{TF}(w, d) \cdot \text{IDF}(w) \cdot \text{BM25}(q, d)$$

### 2.5.2 Dense Passage Retrieval (DPR) (Karpukhin et al., 2020)

DPR replaces sparse TF-IDF with **dense embeddings** from two BERT encoders:

$$E_Q = f_Q(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{d}, \quad E_D = f_D(d) \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$$

$$\text{sim}(q, d) = E_Q^{\top} E_D$$

The encoders are trained to maximize similarity for positive `(q, d⁺)` pairs and minimize for negatives `(q, d⁻)`:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{DPR}} = -\log \frac{\exp\!\left(\text{sim}(q, d^+)\right)}{\exp\!\left(\text{sim}(q, d^+)\right) + \sum_{j=1}^{m}\exp\!\left(\text{sim}(q, d_j^-)\right)}$$

This is trained with **in-batch negatives** — treating other questions' positives as negatives — yielding massive efficiency gains.

### 2.5.3 RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) (Lewis et al., 2020)

RAG fuses DPR retrieval with a **BART generator**:

$$P_{\text{RAG}}(a \mid q) = \sum_{d \in \text{TopK}(q)} P_{\eta}(d \mid q) \cdot P_{\theta}(a \mid q, d)$$

- `P_η(d|q)`: Retriever probability (from DPR).
- `P_θ(a|q,d)`: Generator probability (from BART).
- Marginalization over the top-`K` retrieved documents produces robust answers.

## 2.6 End-to-End ODQA — REALM, FiD, Atlas

**REALM (Guu et al., 2020)** jointly trains the retriever and reader by marginalizing over millions of documents. **Fusion-in-Decoder (FiD)** (Izacard & Grave, 2021) encodes each retrieved passage independently and fuses them in the decoder cross-attention. **Atlas** (Izacard et al., 2022) scales this to LLM-level performance with only 770M parameters by aggressive pre-training.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Symbol | Meaning | KTU Use-Case |
|------------------|--------|---------|--------------|
| `Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QKᵀ/√d_k)V` | Scaled dot-product attention | Core Transformer equation | Derivation, architecture |
| `MHA = Concat(head_1,...,head_h)W^O` | Multi-head attention | Parallel attention heads | Architecture diagram |
| `PE(pos,2i) = sin(pos/10000^(2i/d))` | Sinusoidal positional encoding | Inject position info | Transformer basics |
| `P_start(i) = softmax(s_i)`, `s_i = W_startᵀH_i` | BERT start-logit | Extractive QA head | BERT-QA derivation |
| `P_end(i) = softmax(e_i)`, `e_i = W_endᵀH_i` | BERT end-logit | Extractive QA head | BERT-QA derivation |
| `L_BERT = -log P_start(y_s) - log P_end(y_e)` | SQuAD fine-tuning loss | Cross-entropy on span | Fine-tuning objective |
| `sim(q,d) = E_Qᵀ E_D` | DPR similarity | Dense retrieval | DPR derivation |
| `L_DPR = -log[exp(sim(q,d⁺)) / Z]` | DPR contrastive loss | In-batch negatives | DPR derivation |
| `P_RAG(a|q) = Σ_d P_η(d|q) P_θ(a|q,d)` | RAG marginalization | Retrieve-then-generate | RAG architecture |
| `â = argmax_a P_θ(a|q,c)` | Generative QA decoding | T5/BART formulation | Generative QA |
| **Exact Match (EM)** | `EM = (1/N)Σ I[a_pred = a_gold]` | Strict string match | SQuAD metric |
| **F1 Score** | `F1 = 2·P·R/(P+R)` | Token-level harmonic mean | SQuAD metric |
| **Recall@k** | `R@k = |Relevant ∩ Retrieved@k| / |Relevant|` | Retrieval quality | ODQA evaluation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** Transformer-based QA systems power **Google Search's featured snippets**, **Microsoft Bing's AI answers**, **enterprise search engines**, **legal-tech document review**, **medical literature QA (PubMedQA)**, and the **retrieval backbone of every modern LLM chat system (RAG)**. The same DPR + BART pipeline underlies enterprise copilots, customer support bots, and code-search tools like GitHub Copilot Workspace.
