# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Comparing QA Paradigms (Mandatory Table for KTU)

| Dimension | LSTM-based QA (DrQA Reader) | Transformer-based QA (BERT) | Generative QA (T5) | Open-Domain QA (DPR+RAG) |
|-----------|------------------------------|------------------------------|---------------------|----------------------------|
| **Backbone** | BiLSTM + Attention | 12/24-layer Transformer | Encoder-Decoder Transformer | DPR (BERT) + BART |
| **Context Length** | Limited (~400 tokens) | Up to 512 tokens | Up to 512 tokens | Multi-passage via RAG |
| **Answer Type** | Extractive span | Extractive span | Abstractive text | Abstractive + extractive |
| **Bidirectionality** | No (sequential) | Yes (true bidirectional) | Partial (encoder) | Yes (encoder) |
| **Pre-training** | Word2Vec/GloVe | MLM + NSP | Span-corruption | MLM + contrastive (DPR) |
| **Scalability** | Poor (slow) | Medium | Medium | Excellent (parallel retrieval) |
| **Inference Speed** | Slow (sequential) | Fast (parallel) | Medium | Medium (top-K retrieval) |
| **SQuAD F1 (typical)** | ~78 F1 | **93+ F1** (BERT-Large) | 90+ F1 | 85+ F1 (open) |
| **Handles Unseen Topics** | Poor | Medium | Good | **Excellent** |
| **Hallucination Risk** | Low (extractive) | Low (extractive) | Medium | Low–Medium (grounded) |

## 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation: BERT Extractive QA Forward Pass

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This derivation is a high-yield 7-mark sub-part in KTU Module 4 exams. Practice writing it cleanly.**

### Step 1: Tokenization and Input Construction

Given a question `q = "Who developed BERT?"` and a context `c = "BERT was developed by researchers at Google in 2018..."`:

- Apply **WordPiece tokenization** to both.
- Construct the packed input:

$$X = [\text{CLS}]\, q_1 q_2 \ldots q_m\, [\text{SEP}]\, c_1 c_2 \ldots c_n\, [\text{SEP}]$$

- Append **segment embeddings** `E_A` for question tokens and `E_B` for context tokens.
- Append **positional embeddings** `E_P` to encode token positions.

### Step 2: Embedding Lookup

$$H^{(0)} = E_{\text{tok}}(X) + E_{\text{seg}} + E_{\text{pos}}$$

where `H^(0) ∈ ℝ^{L × d}` and `L = m + n + 3` (3 special tokens: CLS, SEP, SEP).

### Step 3: Pass Through `L` Transformer Encoder Layers

For layer `ℓ = 1, 2, ..., L`:

$$Z^{(\ell)} = \text{LayerNorm}\!\left(H^{(\ell-1)} + \text{MHA}(H^{(\ell-1)})\right)$$

$$H^{(\ell)} = \text{LayerNorm}\!\left(Z^{(\ell)} + \text{FFN}(Z^{(\ell)})\right)$$

where the feed-forward network is:

$$\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0,\, x W_1 + b_1) W_2 + b_2$$

### Step 4: Extract Contextualized Vectors

After `L` layers, we obtain:

$$H^{(L)} = [h_{\text{CLS}}, h_{q_1}, \ldots, h_{q_m}, h_{\text{SEP}}, h_{c_1}, \ldots, h_{c_n}, h_{\text{SEP}}]$$

We retain only the **context token vectors**:

$$C = [h_{c_1}, h_{c_2}, \ldots, h_{c_n}] \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$$

### Step 5: Compute Start and End Logits

Apply the two learned linear projections `W_start, W_end ∈ ℝ^{d × 1}`:

$$s_i = W_{\text{start}}^{\top}\, h_{c_i}, \quad e_i = W_{\text{end}}^{\top}\, h_{c_i}, \quad \text{for } i = 1, \ldots, n$$

### Step 6: Apply Softmax Over Context Tokens

$$P_{\text{start}}(i) = \frac{\exp(s_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n}\exp(s_j)}$$

$$P_{\text{end}}(i) = \frac{\exp(e_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n}\exp(e_j)}$$

### Step 7: Decode the Answer Span

- The predicted start index: `i* = argmax_i P_start(i)`
- The predicted end index: `j* = argmax_j P_end(j)`
- The answer span must satisfy `i* ≤ j*` and `j* − i* + 1 ≤ \text{max\_ans\_len}` (typically 30 tokens).
- The answer is the substring `c[i* : j* + 1]` mapped back to the original text.

### Step 8: Training Objective (SQuAD Fine-Tuning)

Given gold start `y_s` and end `y_e`:

$$\mathcal{L} = -\log P_{\text{start}}(y_s) - \log P_{\text{end}}(y_e)$$

$$\mathcal{L} = -s_{y_s} + \log\sum_{j=1}^{n}\exp(s_j) - e_{y_e} + \log\sum_{j=1}^{n}\exp(e_j)$$

The model is trained end-to-end via backpropagation, updating all BERT weights plus `W_start` and `W_end`.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation: DPR Dense Retrieval

### Step 1: Define Dual Encoders

- Question encoder: `f_Q(·)` — a BERT-base producing a `d = 768` dimensional vector.
- Passage encoder: `f_D(·)` — a separate BERT-base producing a `d` dimensional vector.

### Step 2: Encode Query and All Corpus Passages

$$q_{\text{vec}} = f_Q(q), \quad d_{\text{vec}} = f_D(d) \text{ for each } d \in \text{Corpus}$$

### Step 3: Compute Similarity Scores

$$\text{sim}(q, d) = q_{\text{vec}}^{\top}\, d_{\text{vec}}$$

### Step 4: Build the Contrastive Loss

Given a question `q`, one positive passage `d⁺`, and `m` negative passages `{d_j⁻}`:

$$\mathcal{L}(q, d^+, d_1^-, \ldots, d_m^-) = -\log \frac{\exp(\text{sim}(q, d^+) / \tau)}{\exp(\text{sim}(q, d^+) / \tau) + \sum_{j=1}^{m}\exp(\text{sim}(q, d_j^-) / \tau)}$$

where `τ` is a temperature parameter (often fixed at 1).

### Step 5: In-Batch Negatives Trick

For a mini-batch of `B` questions, the positives for other questions act as negatives. This converts `B` training pairs into `B × (B−1)` effective negatives with **zero extra forward passes** — a hallmark of efficient contrastive learning.

### Step 6: Index Corpus with FAISS

After training, all corpus passages are encoded once, stored in a **FAISS index** (Facebook AI Similarity Search) for sub-linear nearest-neighbor retrieval. At inference:

$$\text{TopK}(q) = \text{FAISS\_search}(q_{\text{vec}}, k)$$

This enables **Open-Domain QA over millions of Wikipedia passages in milliseconds**.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: RAG Marginalization

### Step 1: Retrieve Top-`K` Documents

$$\mathcal{D}_K = \text{TopK}_{d \in \text{Corpus}} P_{\eta}(d \mid q)$$

where `P_η(d|q) ∝ exp(sim(q, d))` from the DPR retriever.

### Step 2: Marginalize Over Retrieved Documents

For each candidate document `d ∈ 𝒟_K`, compute the generator's probability of the answer:

$$P_{\theta}(a \mid q, d) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P_{\theta}(a_t \mid a_{<t}, q, d)$$

### Step 3: Combine Probabilities

$$P_{\text{RAG}}(a \mid q) = \sum_{d \in \mathcal{D}_K} P_{\eta}(d \mid q) \cdot P_{\theta}(a \mid q, d)$$

The final answer is:

$$\hat{a} = \arg\max_{a} P_{\text{RAG}}(a \mid q)$$

**RAG-Sequence** uses this marginalization; **RAG-Token** marginalizes at each token step — both are valid KTU exam variants.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Open-Domain QA Pipeline (RAG Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[User Question q] --> QE[Question Encoder: BERT fQ]
    QE --> QV[Query Vector qv in Rd768]
    QV --> FAISS[FAISS Index over Millions of Passages]
    FAISS --> TopK[TopK Retrieved Passages: d1 d2 ... dK]
    TopK --> GEN[BART Generator ftheta]
    Q --> GEN
    GEN --> ANS[Generated Answer a with grounded evidence]
    TopK --> READ[BERT Extractive Reader]
    Q --> READ
    READ --> SPAN[Extractive Span Answer]
    SPAN --> COMP[Final Answer Selection]
    ANS --> COMP
    COMP --> OUT[Output to User]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram (KTU exam tip):** Always start by identifying the **input**, then trace the **retriever** branch (left) and the **generator/reader** branch (right), and finally describe how they **fuse** at the output. Examiners reward clear directional flow.

## 4.2 Transformer-Based QA Model: BERT Extractive Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INP[Input: CLS question SEP context SEP] --> EMB[Token + Segment + Position Embeddings]
    EMB --> T1[Transformer Encoder Layer 1]
    T1 --> T2[Transformer Encoder Layer 2]
    T2 --> TN[Transformer Encoder Layer N]
    TN --> HEAD[Two Linear Projection Heads]
    HEAD --> ST[Start Logits: s1 ... sn]
    HEAD --> ET[End Logits: e1 ... en]
    ST --> SM1[Softmax over Context Tokens]
    ET --> SM2[Softmax over Context Tokens]
    SM1 --> ARG1[argmax i gives Start Position]
    SM2 --> ARG2[argmax j gives End Position]
    ARG1 --> SPAN[Answer Span: tokens from i to j]
    ARG2 --> SPAN
    SPAN --> OUTA[Final Extractive Answer]
```

## 4.3 Attention Mechanism Inside a Transformer QA Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Input Token Embeddings X] --> WQ[Multiply by WQ]
    X --> WK[Multiply by WK]
    X --> WV[Multiply by WV]
    WQ --> Q[Query Matrix Q]
    WK --> K[Key Matrix K]
    WV --> V[Value Matrix V]
    Q --> DOT[Q times K Transpose]
    K --> DOT
    DOT --> SCALE[Divide by sqrt dk]
    SCALE --> SOFT[Softmax Row wise]
    SOFT --> AW[Attention Weights Matrix]
    AW --> MUL[Multiply by V]
    V --> MUL
    MUL --> OUT[Self Attention Output]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — DrQA vs DPR vs RAG

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1[DrQA Pipeline]
        A1[Question] --> B1[TF IDF Retriever over Wikipedia]
        B1 --> C1[Top K Documents]
        C1 --> D1[BiLSTM Reader with Q Attention]
        D1 --> E1[Extractive Span Answer]
    end
    subgraph S2[DPR Pipeline]
        A2[Question] --> B2[BERT Question Encoder]
        B2 --> C2[Dense Vector qv]
        C2 --> D2[FAISS Index Lookup]
        D2 --> E2[Top K Passages]
        E2 --> F2[BERT Extractive Reader]
        F2 --> G2[Extractive Span Answer]
    end
    subgraph S3[RAG Pipeline]
        A3[Question] --> B3[DPR Retriever]
        B3 --> C3[Top K Passages]
        C3 --> D3[BART Generator]
        A3 --> D3
        D3 --> E3[Abstractive Generated Answer]
    end
```

## 4.5 SQuAD Evaluation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PRED[Predicted Answer String] --> NORM1[Lowercase and Strip Punctuation]
    GOLD[Gold Answer String] --> NORM2[Lowercase and Strip Punctuation]
    NORM1 --> EMCHECK{Exact String Match?}
    NORM2 --> EMCHECK
    EMCHECK -- Yes --> EMScore[EM = 1]
    EMCHECK -- No --> EMScore2[EM = 0]
    NORM1 --> TOK1[Tokenize]
    NORM2 --> TOK2[Tokenize]
    TOK1 --> F1[F1 Token Level]
    TOK2 --> F1
    F1 --> F1Score[F1 Output: 0 to 1]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between Closed-Domain QA and Open-Domain QA with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Closed-Domain QA | Open-Domain QA |
|---------|------------------|----------------|
| Scope | Restricted to a specific domain (e.g., medical) | Any topic (e.g., Wikipedia-scale) |
| Knowledge source | Domain-specific corpus or KB | Large open corpus or the web |
| Vocabulary | Controlled | Open and varied |
| Example | A medical chatbot answering from PubMed | Google Search answering any query |
| Difficulty | Easier (focused) | Harder (robustness, scale) |

Open-Domain QA typically employs a **Retriever–Reader pipeline** (e.g., DPR + BERT) to scale to millions of documents, whereas Closed-Domain QA may use simpler rule-based or fine-tuned BERT readers.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Expected]**
**List any three advantages of Transformer-based QA models over RNN/LSTM-based QA models.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Parallelism & Speed:** Transformers process all tokens in parallel via self-attention, while LSTMs are inherently sequential. Training and inference are significantly faster on GPUs.
2. **Long-Range Dependencies:** Self-attention directly connects any two tokens with `O(1)` path length, whereas LSTMs suffer from information decay over long contexts — critical for QA where question and answer may be far apart.
3. **Bidirectional Context:** BERT-style Transformers encode context from both directions simultaneously, yielding richer representations for span prediction.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** Module 4 Part B includes an **internal choice**. You must answer either **Question A** or **Question B** (both 14 marks). Each question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### ❓ Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a **Transformer-based extractive QA system** based on BERT with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the start and end span prediction heads. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the necessary equations, describe the **Dense Passage Retrieval (DPR)** algorithm used in Open-Domain QA. Explain how it differs from traditional TF-IDF retrieval. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

The **BERT-based extractive QA system** follows these architectural stages:

1. **Input Construction:** Concatenate the question `q` and context `c` with special tokens: `[CLS] q [SEP] c [SEP]`. Apply WordPiece tokenization. Add segment embeddings (0 for question, 1 for context) and positional embeddings. The combined embedding is `H^(0) = E_tok + E_seg + E_pos`. **[1 mark]**

2. **Encoder Stack:** Pass the embeddings through `L` stacked Transformer encoder layers. Each layer applies **Multi-Head Self-Attention (MHA)** followed by a position-wise feed-forward network, with residual connections and LayerNorm: `H^(ℓ) = LayerNorm(Z^(ℓ) + FFN(Z^(ℓ)))`, where `Z^(ℓ) = LayerNorm(H^(ℓ-1) + MHA(H^(ℓ-1)))`. **[1.5 marks]**

3. **Contextualized Representations:** The output `H^(L) = [h_CLS, h_q1, ..., h_cm, h_SEP, h_c1, ..., h_cn, h_SEP]`. We retain only the context vectors `C = [h_c1, ..., h_cn]`. **[1 mark]**

4. **Span Prediction Heads:** Two independent linear layers `W_start, W_end ∈ ℝ^{d × 1}` project each context vector to a scalar logit: `s_i = W_startᵀ h_ci` and `e_i = W_endᵀ h_ci`. **[1.5 marks]**

5. **Softmax & Decoding:** Apply softmax over the context logits to obtain `P_start(i)` and `P_end(i)`. The predicted answer span is `c[i* : j* + 1]` where `i* = argmax P_start` and `j* = argmax P_end`, subject to `i* ≤ j*` and a maximum span length constraint (typically 30 tokens). **[1 mark]**

6. **Fine-Tuning Loss:** Trained using cross-entropy on gold spans: `L = -log P_start(y_s) - log P_end(y_e)`. BERT-Large achieves **>93 F1 on SQuAD v1.1**. **[1 mark]**

**Block Diagram (refer to SECTION 4.2).**

The **start head** identifies the most likely beginning of the answer; the **end head** identifies the termination. Together, they bracket the answer span, enabling **extractive** (verbatim) answers from the context.

#### ✅ Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**Dense Passage Retrieval (DPR)** is the de-facto dense retrieval algorithm for Open-Domain QA, introduced by Karpukhin et al. (2020).

1. **Dual-Encoder Architecture:** Two independent BERT encoders map a question and a passage into a shared `d = 768` dimensional space: `q_vec = f_Q(q)` and `d_vec = f_D(d)`. **[1 mark]**

2. **Similarity Function:** Retrieval score is the dot product: `sim(q, d) = q_vecᵀ d_vec`. This replaces sparse term-matching (TF-IDF/BM25) with **semantic vector similarity**, enabling vocabulary-mismatch handling. **[1 mark]**

3. **Training Objective (Contrastive):** Given a question `q`, a positive passage `d⁺`, and `m` negative passages `{d_j⁻}`, the loss is:

$$L_{\text{DPR}} = -\log \frac{\exp(\text{sim}(q, d^+))}{\exp(\text{sim}(q, d^+)) + \sum_{j=1}^{m}\exp(\text{sim}(q, d_j^-))}$$

This pulls the question embedding closer to the positive passage and pushes it away from negatives. **[1.5 marks]**

4. **In-Batch Negatives:** For a batch of `B` `(q, d⁺)` pairs, the other `B−1` positives are used as negatives — yielding `B × (B−1)` effective negatives with **no extra computation**. **[1 mark]**

5. **Indexing & Inference:** All corpus passages are encoded once with `f_D` and stored in a **FAISS index** for sub-linear nearest-neighbor search. At query time, `f_Q(q)` is compared against the index to retrieve the **top-K** most similar passages. **[1.5 marks]**

6. **Comparison with TF-IDF:** TF-IDF relies on **exact lexical overlap** and fails on paraphrases (e.g., "car" vs "automobile"). DPR uses **semantic embeddings** learned from data, yielding substantially higher recall on benchmarks like Natural Questions (NQ) and TriviaQA. DPR retrieves the correct passage in the top-20 for **~78%** of questions, vs. **~59%** for BM25 on NQ. **[1 mark]**

---

### ❓ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4 Alternative]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Retriever–Reader pipeline** for Open-Domain Question Answering. Discuss the role of the retriever and the reader. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)** model. How does it combine retrieval with generation? Mention the two variants: RAG-Sequence and RAG-Token. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

The **Retriever–Reader pipeline** decomposes Open-Domain QA into two stages:

1. **Retriever Stage:** Given a question `q`, the retriever scans a large corpus (e.g., Wikipedia, ~21M passages) and returns the top-`K` most relevant documents. Early systems used **sparse retrievers** like TF-IDF/BM25; modern systems use **dense retrievers** like DPR. The retrieval score is `sim(q, d) = q_vecᵀ d_vec` for DPR. **[2 marks]**

2. **Reader Stage:** The reader takes each retrieved document and the question, then extracts (or generates) an answer. For extractive QA (e.g., BERT reader), it predicts a span `[i, j]` in the passage: `P_start(i) = softmax(W_startᵀ h_i)`, `P_end(j) = softmax(W_endᵀ h_j)`. For generative QA (e.g., BART reader), it generates a free-form answer conditioned on `(q, d)`. **[2 marks]**

3. **Aggregation:** The final answer is selected from the top-`K` reader outputs — typically by re-scoring with `P_start × P_end` (extractive) or by marginalization `Σ_d P(d|q) P(a|q,d)` (RAG-style). **[1 mark]**

4. **Block Diagram (refer to SECTION 4.1, left branch):** The pipeline: `Question → Retriever → Top-K Passages → Reader → Answer Span / Generated Text → Output`.

5. **Why this design?** It separates **scale** (retrieval handles millions of docs efficiently) from **understanding** (reader focuses on a small, focused context). This decomposition made Open-Domain QA practical for the first time — DrQA (2017) was a breakthrough, achieving ~20% improvement over prior systems. **[2 marks]**

#### ✅ Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)**, introduced by Lewis et al. (2020, NeurIPS), unifies retrieval and generation in a single trainable model.

1. **Architecture:** RAG comprises (a) a **DPR retriever** `P_η(d|q)` and (b) a **BART generator** `P_θ(a|q, d)`. Both components are pre-trained; the generator is fine-tuned end-to-end. **[1 mark]**

2. **Marginalization:** The probability of an answer is:

$$P_{\text{RAG}}(a \mid q) = \sum_{d \in \text{TopK}(q)} P_{\eta}(d \mid q) \cdot P_{\theta}(a \mid q, d)$$

This marginalizes over the top-`K` retrieved documents, allowing the model to **integrate evidence** from multiple sources. **[2 marks]**

3. **RAG-Sequence vs RAG-Token:** **[2 marks]**
   - **RAG-Sequence:** The same document `d` is used to generate the **entire** answer sequence `a`. The score is computed once per document: `P(a|q) = Σ_d P_η(d|q) P_θ(a|q,d)`.
   - **RAG-Token:** A **different** document may be used for **each token** of the answer: `P(a|q) = Π_t Σ_{d_t} P_η(d_t|q) P_θ(a_t|a_{<t}, q, d_t)`. This is more flexible and often yields better generation quality.

4. **Advantages:** RAG grounds generation in real evidence → **reduces hallucination**, allows **knowledge updates** without retraining the LLM, and is **interpretable** (you can inspect retrieved evidence). **[1 mark]**

5. **Performance:** RAG achieved **44.5 Exact Match** on Natural Questions (open-domain), surpassing prior parametric-only LMs by **~10 EM points**. **[1 mark]**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Module 4 QA Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating BERT with GPT:** BERT is **bidirectional and encoder-only** (for extractive QA). GPT is **unidirectional and decoder-only** (for generation). Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the Softmax:** Students often write `W_startᵀ h_i` as the "probability" — it is a *logit*. You MUST apply softmax over the context tokens to get a valid probability. Omitting this is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Skipping the `i* ≤ j*` constraint:** BERT-QA answers must satisfy start ≤ end. Failing to mention this is a common pitfall.
> 4. **Calling DPR "the same as BERT":** DPR uses **two SEPARATE** BERT encoders (not shared weights). The question and passage encoders are independent — this is a frequent high-weight mistake.
> 5. **Mixing up RAG-Sequence and RAG-Token:** Examiners explicitly test this distinction. Memorize the formula difference.
> 6. **Ignoring Evaluation Metrics:** Always mention **Exact Match (EM)** and **F1** when discussing SQuAD — they are the *defining* metrics for extractive QA.
> 7. **No Diagram = Up to 2 marks lost:** Every 7-mark sub-part in Part B should include a labelled block diagram. Even a hand-drawn rough sketch in the exam earns partial credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **QA System** = Given question `q` + context `C` → produces answer `a` (extractive, abstractive, or generative).
- [x] **Transformer Backbone** = Multi-Head Self-Attention + Feed-Forward + Residual + LayerNorm.
- [x] **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** = `softmax(QKᵀ / √d_k) V` — the heart of every Transformer.
- [x] **Multi-Head Attention** = `h` parallel attention heads, concatenated and projected by `W^O`.
- [x] **Positional Encoding** = Sinusoidal `PE(pos, 2i) = sin(pos / 10000^(2i/d))` (and cosine for odd).
- [x] **BERT for Extractive QA** = Packed `[CLS] q [SEP] c [SEP]` → `L` Transformer layers → 2 linear heads → softmax over context → span `[i*, j*]`.
- [x] **BERT-QA Loss** = `L = -log P_start(y_s) - log P_end(y_e)`.
- [x] **DrQA** = First Open-Domain QA system (2017). TF-IDF retriever + BiLSTM reader.
- [x] **DPR** = Two BERT encoders + contrastive loss + in-batch negatives + FAISS index.
- [x] **DPR Similarity** = `sim(q, d) = f_Q(q)ᵀ f_D(d)`.
- [x] **DPR Loss** = `L = -log[exp(sim(q, d⁺)) / (exp(sim(q, d⁺)) + Σ exp(sim(q, d_j⁻)))]`.
- [x] **RAG** = DPR retriever + BART generator + marginalization over top-K documents.
- [x] **RAG-Sequence** = Same document generates the whole answer (one sum).
- [x] **RAG-Token** = Different document per token (sum inside product).
- [x] **Open-Domain QA** = Answering any question using a large external corpus (Wikipedia, web).
- [x] **Closed-Domain QA** = Restricted to a specific vertical (e.g., medical, legal).
- [x] **Extractive QA** = Answer is a verbatim span from the context.
- [x] **Abstractive QA** = Answer is generated; may not appear verbatim.
- [x] **SQuAD Metrics** = **Exact Match (EM)** and **F1 Score** (token-level harmonic mean of P, R).
- [x] **Recall@k** = Fraction of relevant documents in the top-k retrieved.
- [x] **FAISS** = Sub-linear approximate nearest-neighbor search over millions of dense vectors.
- [x] **In-Batch Negatives** = Trick that turns `B` pairs into `B(B−1)` effective negatives for free.
- [x] **REALM, FiD, Atlas** = End-to-end joint retriever–generator models (next generation beyond RAG).
- [x] **Transformer Advantage** = Parallelism, long-range `O(1)` attention, true bidirectionality.
- [x] **Real-World Impact** = Powers Google Search, Bing, ChatGPT (RAG), enterprise copilots, medical & legal QA.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When answering a 14-mark Part B question, always structure your response as:
> 1. **Definition / Introduction** (1–2 marks)
> 2. **Architecture / Block Diagram** (2–3 marks)
> 3. **Mathematical Formulation** (3–4 marks)
> 4. **Working / Algorithm Steps** (2–3 marks)
> 5. **Comparison / Advantages** (1–2 marks)
> 6. **Real-world application** (1 mark)
> This mirrors the official KTU 2024 Scheme marking scheme and maximizes score recovery.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machine Translation: Neural Machine Translation (NMT), Transformer-Based NMT, Multilingual NLP Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT601 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Machine Translation: Neural Machine Translation (NMT), Transformer-Based NMT, Multilingual NLP Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Machine Translation (MT) – The Foundation
**Machine Translation (MT)** is the automated process of converting text or speech from one natural language (the *source language*) into another (the *target language*) using computational models, while preserving the underlying semantic meaning, syntactic structure, and contextual intent.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Aligned):** MT is a sub-field of computational linguistics and NLP concerned with the use of software to translate text or speech from one language to another. It forms the cornerstone of all global digital communication pipelines.

## 1.2 Neural Machine Translation (NMT) – The Modern Paradigm
**Neural Machine Translation (NMT)** is an end-to-end learning approach to MT that uses a single, large **artificial neural network** to model the entire translation process. Unlike earlier Statistical Machine Translation (SMT) systems that relied on hand-crafted features and isolated sub-components (alignment models, translation tables, language models), NMT learns to translate directly from input text to output text using continuous vector representations (embeddings).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why NMT matters for KTU:** It replaced the older phrase-based statistical models because NMT produces more **fluent**, **context-aware**, and **grammatically coherent** translations by jointly conditioning on the entire source sentence and previously generated target tokens.

## 1.3 Transformer-Based NMT – The Dominant Architecture
A **Transformer-Based NMT system** is an NMT architecture built entirely on the **self-attention mechanism**, introduced in the seminal paper *"Attention Is All You Need"* (Vaswani et al., 2017). It eliminates recurrence (RNN/LSTM) and convolution (CNN), relying purely on attention layers, feed-forward networks, and positional encodings to model sequence-to-sequence mappings.

## 1.4 Multilingual NLP Models
**Multilingual NLP Models** are neural architectures trained jointly on text from **multiple languages** (often 100+) within a single shared parameter space. The most prominent examples are **mBERT (multilingual BERT)**, **XLM-R (XLM-RoBERTa)**, and **mT5**. These models learn a **language-agnostic representation** where sentences with similar meanings — regardless of language — cluster in the same vector space.

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy – The United Nations Interpreter:**
> Imagine traditional MT (SMT) as a committee of 10 experts, each handling one tiny piece of translation manually. **NMT** is a single genius polyglot who reads an entire sentence in one go and produces a fluent translation, capturing long-range context. **Transformer-based NMT** is that polyglot upgraded with a *photographic memory* (self-attention) — they can glance at any word in the source sentence while writing any word in the translation, no matter how far apart they are. **Multilingual NLP Models** are a *school* of such polyglots who share one common brain — learning 50+ languages together so that translating English→French also *indirectly improves* English→Korean.

### Key Standard Metrics to Remember
- **BLEU Score** → evaluates translation quality on a 0–100 (or 0–1) scale by measuring n-gram overlap with reference translations.
- **chrF** → character-level F-score for morphologically rich languages.
- **TER (Translation Edit Rate)** → measures the number of edits required to convert a hypothesis into a reference.
- **WMT Benchmark** → the standard annual competition corpus for MT research.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Evolution of Machine Translation Paradigms
| Era | Approach | Core Mechanism | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1990s | **Rule-Based MT (RBMT)** | Hand-coded linguistic rules | Brittle, not scalable |
| 2000s | **Statistical MT (SMT)** | Probabilistic phrase tables + n-gram LM | Fragmented pipeline |
| 2013–2014 | **Early NMT (RNN-based)** | Encoder–Decoder with LSTM/GRU | Sequential, slow |
| 2017–Present | **Transformer NMT** | Self-attention + parallelism | High compute, data hungry |
| 2020–Present | **Multilingual / Foundation Models** | Shared parameter space, transfer | Language imbalance |

## 2.2 The Encoder–Decoder Backbone of NMT
Every NMT system (even Transformer-based) is conceptually an **Encoder–Decoder** framework:

- **Encoder:** Reads the source sequence $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ and compresses it into a sequence of continuous vectors.
- **Context Vector $\mathbf{c}$:** A semantic summary used to initialize the decoder.
- **Decoder:** Generates the target sequence $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, y_2, \dots, y_m)$ one token at a time, conditioned on $\mathbf{c}$ and previously generated tokens.

## 2.3 Attention Mechanism — The Heart of Modern NMT
**Attention** allows the decoder to dynamically focus on different parts of the source sentence while generating each target word. It computes a weighted sum of encoder hidden states.

### 2.3.1 Types of Attention
1. **Global Attention** — attends to all source positions.
2. **Local Attention** — attends to a small window around an aligned position.
3. **Self-Attention** — relates different positions of a *single* sequence to itself (Transformer core).
4. **Cross-Attention** — relates decoder positions to encoder positions.

## 2.4 Transformer Architecture — The Three Sub-Layers
Each Transformer block (both encoder and decoder) contains:

1. **Multi-Head Self-Attention** — runs $h$ parallel attention heads.
2. **Position-wise Feed-Forward Network (FFN)** — two linear layers with ReLU/GELU activation.
3. **Residual Connection + Layer Normalization** — stabilizes deep stacks.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **exam-critical** equations. All pipe notation uses $\vert$ to prevent table syntax conflicts.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Scaled Dot-Product Attention | $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | $d_k$ is key dimensionality |
| 2 | Multi-Head Attention | $\text{MHA}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,\dots,\text{head}_h)W^O$ | $\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(QW_i^Q, KW_i^K, VW_i^V)$ |
| 3 | Sinusoidal Positional Encoding (PE) | $PE_{(pos,2i)} = \sin(pos / 10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}})$ | Even indices |
| 4 | Sinusoidal PE (odd indices) | $PE_{(pos,2i+1)} = \cos(pos / 10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}})$ | Odd indices |
| 5 | FFN Block | $\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0,\, xW_1 + b_1)W_2 + b_2$ | Position-wise transformation |
| 6 | LayerNorm | $\text{LN}(x) = \gamma \odot \dfrac{x-\mu}{\sigma} + \beta$ | Stabilizes activations |
| 7 | Conditional Probability (NMT) | $P(y \vert x) = \prod_{t=1}^{m} P(y_t \vert y_{<t}, x)$ | Autoregressive decoder |
| 8 | BLEU Score (corpus-level) | $\text{BLEU} = BP \cdot \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | $BP$ = brevity penalty |
| 9 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t} \log P(y_t^{\ast} \vert y_{<t}^{\ast}, x)$ | Training objective |
| 10 | Alignment Score | $e_{ij} = \text{score}(s_{i-1}, h_j)$ | Where $h_j$ = source state, $s_{i-1}$ = target state |
| 11 | Softmax of Attention | $\alpha_{ij} = \dfrac{\exp(e_{ij})}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \exp(e_{ik})}$ | Normalized attention weights |
| 12 | Context Vector | $c_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_{ij}\, h_j$ | Weighted sum of encoder states |

### 2.5.1 Why These Formulas Matter in Engineering
- **Equation 1 (Scaled Attention)** — The $\sqrt{d_k}$ divisor prevents gradient vanishing in high-dimensional spaces; without it, dot products explode after softmax.
- **Equation 3 & 4 (Positional Encoding)** — Transformers are permutation-invariant; PE injects the otherwise-lost word-order information.
- **Equation 8 (BLEU)** — Standard grading metric in WMT competitions and industry MT evaluations.

## 2.6 Multilingual NLP — Architecture Variations
- **Single Shared Encoder/Decoder (mBART, mT5):** One model, many languages. Translation emerges via language tags (e.g., `>>en<<`).
- **Language-Specific Adapters:** Frozen backbone + small trainable adapter modules per language (parameter-efficient).
- **Cross-lingual Embedding Alignment:** Map monolingual embedding spaces into a shared geometric space using a learned linear transformation.

## 2.7 Why Transformer-Based NMT Dominates
- **Parallelization:** No recurrence → entire sequence processed in parallel on GPUs.
- **Long-Range Dependencies:** Self-attention connects any two tokens in $O(1)$ path length.
- **Transferability:** Pretrained Transformers (mBART, mT5) transfer to low-resource languages with minimal fine-tuning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bahdanau Attention to Scaled Dot-Product Attention

### Step 1 — Bahdanau (Additive) Attention Baseline
The original attention score is computed via a small feed-forward network:

$$e_{ij} = v^{\top} \tanh\!\left(W_s s_{i-1} + W_h h_j\right)$$

- $s_{i-1}$ is the previous decoder hidden state.
- $h_j$ is the $j$-th encoder hidden state.
- $W_s, W_h$ are learnable matrices; $v$ is a learnable vector.

### Step 2 — Luong Multiplicative Attention (Efficiency Improvement)
Instead of a tanh network, the score becomes a dot product:

$$e_{ij} = s_{i-1}^{\top}\, W\, h_j$$

This is faster, but for high-dimensional vectors, the dot-product magnitudes grow large.

### Step 3 — The Scaling Problem
Assume $q, k \in \mathbb{R}^{d_k}$ with components drawn from $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$. The variance of the dot product $q^{\top} k = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} q_i k_i$ scales linearly with $d_k$:

$$\text{Var}(q^{\top} k) = d_k$$

When inputs to a softmax have large magnitudes, the output distribution becomes **near-one-hot**, causing vanishing gradients during backpropagation.

### Step 4 — The Scaling Fix (Transformer Solution)
Divide the dot product by $\sqrt{d_k}$:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$$

This keeps the variance of the pre-softmax logits at approximately 1, stabilizing training.

### Step 5 — Full Expansion
Let $Q = X W^Q$, $K = X W^K$, $V = X W^V$ (linear projections of input $X$). Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) &= \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{(XW^Q)(XW^K)^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)(XW^V) \\[4pt]
&= \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{XW^Q (W^K)^{\top} X^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)XW^V
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Multi-Head Concatenation
Run $h$ independent attention heads, then concatenate and project:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{head}_i &= \text{Attention}(QW_i^Q,\, KW_i^K,\, VW_i^V) \\[4pt]
\text{MHA}(Q,K,V) &= \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \text{head}_2, \dots, \text{head}_h)\,W^O
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of scaling:** Each head operates in a lower-dimensional subspace ($d_k = d_{\text{model}}/h$), so the model jointly attends to information from $h$ different representation subspaces.

## 3.2 Derivation: BLEU Score Step-by-Step

### Step 1 — Modified n-gram Precision
For n-grams of order $n$, count the maximum number of times each n-gram appears in *any* reference translation, then clip the candidate count:

$$p_n = \dfrac{\sum_{\text{n-gram} \in C} \min\!\left(\text{count}_C(\text{n-gram}),\ \max_{R \in \text{Refs}} \text{count}_R(\text{n-gram})\right)}{\sum_{\text{n-gram} \in C} \text{count}_C(\text{n-gram})}$$

### Step 2 — Geometric Mean of n-gram Precisions
Combine precisions for $n=1,2,3,4$ (typical) with uniform weights $w_n = 1/N$:

$$\text{BLEU}_{\text{raw}} = \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$$

### Step 3 — Brevity Penalty (BP)
Penalize overly short translations:

$$BP = \begin{cases} 1, & c > r \\ \exp\!\left(1 - \dfrac{r}{c}\right), & c \le r \end{cases}$$

where $c$ = candidate length, $r$ = closest reference length.

### Step 4 — Final BLEU
$$\text{BLEU} = BP \cdot \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of MT Paradigms

| Property | Rule-Based MT | Statistical MT | RNN-based NMT | Transformer NMT | Multilingual NMT |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Need** | None (rules) | Millions of parallel sentences | Tens of thousands | Hundreds of thousands | Cross-lingual corpora |
| **Fluency** | Low–Medium | Medium | High | Very High | High |
| **Training Speed** | N/A (handcrafted) | Days–Weeks | Weeks (sequential) | Hours–Days (parallel) | Days–Weeks |
| **Long Sentences** | Poor | Poor | Degrades (vanishing gradient) | Strong (self-attention) | Strong |
| **Cross-lingual Transfer** | No | No | No | Limited | Yes (explicit) |
| **Inference Speed** | Fast | Fast | Slow (sequential) | Fast (parallel) | Fast |
| **Example** | Apertium | Moses, Moses SMT | Seq2Seq (Sutskever 2014) | MarianNMT, FairSeq, mBART | mBART-50, mT5, NLLB |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Scaled Dot-Product Attention

**Problem:** Given a single query vector $q = [1, 2]$, two key vectors $k_1 = [3, 4]$ and $k_2 = [-1, 2]$, and value vectors $v_1 = [5, 6]$, $v_2 = [7, 8]$, compute the attention output. Assume $d_k = 2$.

**Step 1 — Compute raw scores**

$$s_1 = q^{\top} k_1 = (1)(3) + (2)(4) = 3 + 8 = 11$$

$$s_2 = q^{\top} k_2 = (1)(-1) + (2)(2) = -1 + 4 = 3$$

**Step 2 — Apply scaling by $\sqrt{d_k} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$**

$$s_1' = \dfrac{11}{1.414} \approx 7.778$$

$$s_2' = \dfrac{3}{1.414} \approx 2.121$$

**Step 3 — Apply softmax**

$$\begin{aligned}
\alpha_1 &= \dfrac{e^{7.778}}{e^{7.778} + e^{2.121}} = \dfrac{2402.5}{2402.5 + 8.333} \approx 0.9965 \\[4pt]
\alpha_2 &= \dfrac{8.333}{2402.5 + 8.333} \approx 0.0035
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Weighted sum of values**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{output} &= \alpha_1 v_1 + \alpha_2 v_2 \\
&= 0.9965 \cdot [5, 6] + 0.0035 \cdot [7, 8] \\
&\approx [4.9825 + 0.0245,\ 5.979 + 0.028] \\
&\approx [5.007,\ 6.007]
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** Since $\alpha_1 \approx 1$, the output is dominated by $v_1$, which is the correct behavior given that $q$ was very similar to $k_1$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Transformer-Based NMT Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    srcInput["Source Tokens x1 x2 ... xn"]
    srcEmbed["Source Token Embedding"]
    posEnc["Positional Encoding Addition"]
    encStack["Encoder Stack N=6 Layers"]
    decStack["Decoder Stack N=6 Layers"]
    tgtInput["Target Tokens y1 y2 ... ym shifted right"]
    tgtEmbed["Target Token Embedding"]
    linSoft["Linear + Softmax"]
    tgtOut["Translated Tokens y1 y2 ... ym"]

    srcInput --> srcEmbed --> posEnc --> encStack
    tgtInput --> tgtEmbed --> posEnc
    encStack -->|"context K V"| decStack
    posEnc -->|"self-attention input"| decStack
    decStack --> linSoft --> tgtOut

    subgraph encLayer["Single Encoder Layer"]
        e1["Multi-Head Self-Attention"]
        e2["Add and Norm"]
        e3["Feed Forward FFN"]
        e4["Add and Norm"]
        e1 --> e2 --> e3 --> e4
    end

    subgraph decLayer["Single Decoder Layer"]
        d1["Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention"]
        d2["Add and Norm"]
        d3["Cross-Attention Encoder Decoder"]
        d4["Add and Norm"]
        d5["Feed Forward FFN"]
        d6["Add and Norm"]
        d1 --> d2 --> d3 --> d4 --> d5 --> d6
    end
```

## 4.2 Scaled Dot-Product Attention Sub-Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q["Query Matrix Q"]
    K["Key Matrix K"]
    V["Value Matrix V"]
    matmul["Q times K transpose"]
    scale["Divide by sqrt dk"]
    mask["Optional Mask future tokens"]
    softmax["Softmax"]
    out["Weighted Sum of V"]

    Q --> matmul
    K --> matmul
    matmul --> scale --> mask --> softmax --> out
    V --> out
```

## 4.3 Multilingual NLP Model Architecture (mBART / mT5 Style)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    lang1["English Corpus"]
    lang2["French Corpus"]
    lang3["Hindi Corpus"]
    lang4["Low-Resource Language X"]

    tok["Shared Subword Tokenizer SentencePiece"]
    emb["Shared Embedding Matrix"]
    enc["Shared Transformer Encoder"]
    dec["Shared Transformer Decoder"]
    taskA["Task A NMT en to fr"]
    taskB["Task B NMT en to hi"]
    taskC["Task C Summarization"]
    taskD["Task D Zero-Shot hi to X"]

    lang1 --> tok
    lang2 --> tok
    lang3 --> tok
    lang4 --> tok
    tok --> emb --> enc --> dec
    dec --> taskA
    dec --> taskB
    dec --> taskC
    dec --> taskD
```

## 4.4 Attention Mechanism Workflow (Encoder-Decoder Cross-Attention)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    encOut["Encoder Hidden States h1 to hn"]
    decState["Decoder Hidden State s_t minus 1"]
    scoreFn["Score Function e_tj"]
    weights["Softmax to get alpha_tj"]
    ctx["Context Vector c_t"]
    out["Decoder Output y_t"]

    encOut --> scoreFn
    decState --> scoreFn
    scoreFn --> weights --> ctx --> out
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Define Neural Machine Translation. List two advantages of NMT over Statistical Machine Translation (SMT).**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Neural Machine Translation (NMT) is an end-to-end learning approach that uses a single large neural network, typically an encoder–decoder architecture, to directly map a source language sentence to a target language sentence without relying on separately engineered components.

> **Advantages over SMT (2 marks):**
> 1. **End-to-End Optimization:** NMT jointly trains all components via a single objective (cross-entropy), whereas SMT optimizes multiple sub-models independently (translation model, language model, reordering model).
> 2. **Better Fluency & Context Handling:** NMT captures long-range dependencies through continuous vector representations, producing more natural and fluent translations than the n-gram-based SMT.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**What is self-attention? How does it differ from the encoder–decoder cross-attention used in earlier NMT models?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Self-Attention (1.5 marks):** Self-attention is a mechanism that allows every position in a sequence to attend to all other positions *within the same sequence*, computing a weighted representation of the entire sequence. It is the core building block of the Transformer.
>
> **Difference from Cross-Attention (1.5 marks):**
>
> | Feature | Self-Attention | Cross-Attention |
> |---|---|---|
> | Input | Q, K, V all from the *same* sequence | Q from decoder, K, V from encoder |
> | Purpose | Model intra-sequence relationships | Model inter-sequence alignment |
> | Used in | Both encoder and decoder | Only decoder |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Weight Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a **Transformer-based NMT system** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of **Multi-Head Self-Attention** and **Positional Encoding** in detail. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of suitable equations, explain the **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** mechanism. Why is scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$ essential? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Overall Architecture (2 marks):**
A Transformer-based NMT system consists of an **encoder stack** and a **decoder stack**, each with $N=6$ identical layers (in the original design). The encoder maps the source sequence $\mathbf{x} = (x_1,\dots,x_n)$ to a continuous representation; the decoder generates the target sequence $\mathbf{y} = (y_1,\dots,y_m)$ autoregressively.

**Step 2 — Encoder Layer (1.5 marks):**
Each encoder layer has two sub-layers:
1. **Multi-Head Self-Attention** — allows each source token to attend to all other source tokens.
2. **Position-wise Feed-Forward Network (FFN)** — applies two linear transformations with a ReLU activation independently to each position.
Each sub-layer is followed by **Add & Norm** (residual connection + layer normalization).

**Step 3 — Decoder Layer (1.5 marks):**
Each decoder layer has three sub-layers:
1. **Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention** — prevents attending to future tokens.
2. **Encoder–Decoder Cross-Attention** — attends to encoder output.
3. **Position-wise FFN** + **Add & Norm**.

**Step 4 — Role of Multi-Head Self-Attention (1 mark):**
Multi-head attention projects $Q, K, V$ into $h$ different learned subspaces and runs attention in parallel. Each head captures a *different type of relationship* (syntactic, semantic, positional), and the results are concatenated and linearly projected. This enables the model to *jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces*.

**Step 5 — Role of Positional Encoding (1 mark):**
Since the Transformer contains no recurrence or convolution, it is inherently **permutation-invariant**. Positional Encodings inject the *order* of tokens by adding a deterministic vector $PE_{(pos, 2i)}$ and $PE_{(pos, 2i+1)}$ to the input embedding. The sinusoidal formulation allows the model to generalize to sequence lengths longer than those seen during training.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Equation (2 marks):**

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$$

where $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$, $K \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times d_k}$, $V \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times d_v}$.

**Step 2 — Step-by-Step Computation (3 marks):**
1. **Compute raw scores:** $S = QK^{\top}$, an $n \times m$ matrix where $S_{ij}$ is the dot-product similarity between query $i$ and key $j$.
2. **Scale:** $S' = S / \sqrt{d_k}$.
3. **Mask (decoder only):** Set masked positions to $-\infty$.
4. **Softmax:** Apply row-wise softmax to obtain attention weights $\alpha \in [0,1]$.
5. **Aggregate:** Multiply by $V$ to get the output.

**Step 3 — Why Scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$ is Essential (2 marks):**
Assume each component of $q$ and $k$ is an independent random variable with mean 0 and variance 1. Then the dot product $q^{\top} k = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} q_i k_i$ has variance $d_k$. As $d_k$ grows, the magnitude of the dot products grows, pushing the softmax into regions where it outputs probabilities close to 0 or 1. This produces **vanishing gradients** during backpropagation. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ normalizes the variance back to 1, keeping gradients healthy and ensuring smooth attention distributions.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style – Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** What are **Multilingual NLP Models**? Explain with examples. Discuss the concept of **cross-lingual transfer learning** and the **zero-shot translation** capability of multilingual NMT systems. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **BLEU evaluation metric** for Machine Translation with its complete mathematical formulation. List two limitations of BLEU. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Definition + Examples (2 marks):**
Multilingual NLP models are single neural architectures trained jointly on text corpora from many languages, sharing parameters across a unified vocabulary (often built via subword tokenizers like SentencePiece). Examples:
- **mBERT** — multilingual version of BERT supporting 104 languages.
- **XLM-R** — RoBERTa-based, trained on 100 languages using CommonCrawl.
- **mBART-50** — multilingual denoising autoencoder supporting 50 languages.
- **mT5** — multilingual T5 pretrained on 101 languages.
- **NLLB-200** — No Language Left Behind, supporting 200 languages.

**Step 2 — Cross-Lingual Transfer Learning (2 marks):**
The shared parameter space forces the model to learn **language-agnostic representations** — sentences with similar meaning from different languages end up in the *same region of the embedding space*. Consequently, knowledge learned from a high-resource language (e.g., English) can be transferred to a low-resource language (e.g., Malayalam) via fine-tuning or prompting. This is called **cross-lingual transfer**.

**Step 3 — Zero-Shot Translation (2 marks):**
A multilingual NMT model trained on, e.g., English↔French and English↔German can, *without ever seeing* a French↔German parallel sentence, translate French→German. This is **zero-shot translation**, achieved by simply specifying the target language tag (e.g., `>>de<<`) at inference. The encoder–decoder exploits the language-agnostic representation to route the translation through an implicit English pivot.

**Step 4 — Architectural Insight (1 mark):**
Modern multilingual NMT uses a **shared encoder** and **shared decoder** with a **language embedding** prepended to the input. The model conditions on the target language ID and learns to disentangle language identity from semantic content.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Purpose of BLEU (1 mark):**
Bilingual Evaluation Understudy (BLEU) measures the *n-gram overlap* between a candidate translation and one or more human reference translations, on a scale of 0 to 1 (or 0 to 100).

**Step 2 — Modified n-gram Precision (2 marks):**
For each n-gram order $n \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$:

$$p_n = \dfrac{\displaystyle \sum_{\text{n-gram} \in C} \min\!\left(\text{count}_C(\text{n-gram}),\ \max_{R \in \text{Refs}} \text{count}_R(\text{n-gram})\right)}{\displaystyle \sum_{\text{n-gram} \in C} \text{count}_C(\text{n-gram})}$$

**Step 3 — Geometric Mean (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{BLEU}_{\text{raw}} = \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$$

**Step 4 — Brevity Penalty (1.5 marks):**

$$BP = \begin{cases} 1, & c > r \\ \exp\!\left(1 - \dfrac{r}{c}\right), & c \le r \end{cases}$$

where $c$ = candidate length, $r$ = length of the closest reference.

**Step 5 — Final Equation (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\text{BLEU} = BP \cdot \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)}$$

**Step 6 — Limitations (1 mark extra credit):**
1. **No Semantic Awareness:** BLEU cannot detect synonyms or paraphrases — *"happy"* vs *"joyful"* are scored as zero overlap.
2. **No Recall Beyond 4-grams:** Sentence-level meaning involving long-range structure is poorly captured.
3. **Reference Dependency:** Quality depends on having multiple, high-quality human references.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling in Attention** — KTU valuators specifically check the derivation; writing $QK^{\top}$ without scaling loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Self-Attention with Cross-Attention** — Marks deducted in the architecture question for not specifying *which sub-layer uses which*.
> 3. **Skipping the Brevity Penalty in BLEU** — A complete BLEU formula *must* include $BP$; without it, full marks are not awarded.
> 4. **Writing "Transformers use RNNs internally"** — This is a common misconception; Transformers are *deliberately* recurrence-free.
> 5. **Confusing NMT and SMT** — NMT is *end-to-end*; SMT has separate phrase tables and language models.
> 6. **No diagram in 7-mark questions** — Always include a labeled block diagram for "Explain the architecture" type questions; absence loses 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Generic "Multilingual means more than one language"** — KTU expects the technical term *language-agnostic representation* and the concept of *language tags*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **MT** = automated translation between natural languages. **NMT** = end-to-end neural approach. **Transformer NMT** = NMT built entirely on self-attention. **Multilingual NMT** = one model, many languages.
- **Three eras of MT:** Rule-Based → Statistical → Neural. Neural dominates since 2014–2017.
- **NMT advantages:** fluency, end-to-end training, context awareness, no hand-crafted features.
- **Encoder–Decoder:** Encoder compresses source; Decoder generates target autoregressively.
- **Attention** = dynamic weighted focus on source tokens. Solves the *information bottleneck* of fixed-size context vectors.
- **Bahdanau (additive) attention** uses $\tanh$ network; **Luong (multiplicative)** uses dot product; **Transformer (scaled dot-product)** uses $\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}$.
- **Why scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$?** Keeps softmax inputs in a low-variance regime, preventing vanishing gradients.
- **Multi-Head Attention** = $h$ parallel attention heads, each in its own subspace, concatenated and projected.
- **Positional Encoding** is *mandatory* in Transformers because they are permutation-invariant. Use sinusoidal PE for theoretical generality.
- **Encoder layer:** Multi-Head Self-Attention → Add & Norm → FFN → Add & Norm.
- **Decoder layer:** Masked Self-Attention → Add & Norm → Cross-Attention → Add & Norm → FFN → Add & Norm.
- **Masked Self-Attention** prevents the decoder from peeking at future tokens during training.
- **Multilingual NLP Models:** mBERT, XLM-R, mBART, mT5, NLLB-200.
- **Cross-lingual transfer:** High-resource languages (e.g., English) improve low-resource language (e.g., Malayalam) performance.
- **Zero-shot translation:** Translate between language pairs never seen during training, using language tags.
- **BLEU** = corpus-level n-gram precision × Brevity Penalty. Always include $BP$ in the formula.
- **BLEU limitations:** No synonym handling, no semantic awareness, depends on quality of references.
- **Key metrics:** BLEU, chrF, TER.
- **Standard MT libraries:** FairSeq, MarianNMT, OpenNMT, HuggingFace Transformers.
- **2017 landmark paper:** *"Attention Is All You Need"* — foundation of all modern NMT.
- **2020–2023 milestones:** mBART-50, mT5, NLLB-200 (Meta, 200 languages), GPT-style multilingual LLMs.

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — PCCMT601 Introduction to Natural Language Processing (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Large Language Models: GPT-3, GPT-4, and Their Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT601 - INTRODUCTION TO NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Large Language Models: GPT-3, GPT-4, and Their Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Large Language Model (LLM)

A **Large Language Model (LLM)** is a deep neural network, typically built on the **Transformer architecture**, that has been pre-trained on massive corpora of textual data (often hundreds of billions of tokens) using a self-supervised objective such as next-token prediction. LLMs exhibit **emergent abilities** — capabilities such as in-context learning, chain-of-thought reasoning, and zero-shot generalization — that arise as model parameters, dataset size, and compute are scaled together (the **scaling laws** of Kaplan et al., 2020).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Mapping):**
> An LLM is a probabilistic model $P_\theta(t_n \mid t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_{n-1})$ over token sequences, parameterised by $\theta$ in the order of **billions to trillions** of parameters, trained via self-supervised learning on web-scale text.

### 1.2 What is GPT?

**GPT** stands for **Generative Pre-trained Transformer**. It is a family of decoder-only Transformer language models developed by **OpenAI** that use a left-to-right (causal) language modeling objective.

| Model | Release Year | Parameters | Key Innovation |
|---|---|---|---|
| GPT-1 | 2018 | **117 Million** | Proof of concept — generative pre-training + discriminative fine-tuning |
| GPT-2 | 2019 | **1.5 Billion** | Zero-shot task transfer without fine-tuning |
| GPT-3 | 2020 | **175 Billion** | In-context learning via few-shot prompting |
| GPT-4 | 2023 | ~**1.76 Trillion** (estimated, MoE) | Multimodality, RLHF alignment, chain-of-thought |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Intern" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine hiring an intern who has secretly read **every textbook, Wikipedia article, novel, and Reddit post** ever written. They cannot truly "understand" the world, but they are extraordinarily good at *predicting the most plausible next word* in any sentence you give them. Give them the first half of a legal contract, and they can complete it. Show them a math problem, and they can solve it. This is essentially what GPT-3 and GPT-4 do — they are **next-token predictors at planetary scale**.

### 1.4 Core Defining Properties

- **Scale:** Billions of parameters (GPT-3: 175B; GPT-4: estimated ~1.76T in a Mixture-of-Experts configuration).
- **Architecture:** Stacked Transformer decoder blocks with **masked self-attention** (causal mask).
- **Training Objective:** Causal language modeling — predict the next token given all previous tokens.
- **Emergent Behaviour:** Few-shot and zero-shot learning appear non-linearly as model size crosses certain thresholds.
- **Alignment (GPT-4 onwards):** **RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback)** and the **Constitutional AI** style safety tuning.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Transformer Backbone — Why GPT Works

GPT models are **decoder-only Transformers**. Each block contains two sub-layers:

1. **Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention** — allows every token to attend only to *previous* tokens.
2. **Position-wise Feed-Forward Network (FFN)** — a two-layer MLP applied independently to each position.

Residual connections and **Layer Normalization** wrap each sub-layer. The whole stack is **pre-norm** in modern variants for training stability.

### 2.2 Self-Attention Mechanism (The Heart of GPT)

Given input token embeddings $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$, three learnable projection matrices $W^Q, W^K, W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k}$ produce:

$$Q = X W^Q, \quad K = X W^K, \quad V = X W^V$$

The **scaled dot-product attention** is:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

In **multi-head attention**, we run $h$ parallel attention heads, each with its own projection matrices, and concatenate:

$$\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h) W^O$$

where $\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(Q W_i^Q, K W_i^K, V W_i^V)$.

### 2.3 Causal (Masked) Attention

For autoregressive generation, we apply a **causal mask** $M$ that sets future positions to $-\infty$ before the softmax:

$$\text{MaskedAttention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} + M\right) V$$

where $M_{ij} = 0$ if $j \le i$, else $M_{ij} = -\infty$.

### 2.4 Positional Encoding

Since self-attention is permutation-invariant, GPT injects **positional information** using **learned absolute positional embeddings** (GPT-1/2/3) or **rotary positional embeddings (RoPE)** in modern descendants.

$$PE_{(pos, 2i)} = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d}}\right)$$

$$PE_{(pos, 2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d}}\right)$$

### 2.5 Training Objective — Causal Language Modeling

Given a token sequence $t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n$, GPT maximises:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{LM}}(\theta) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P_\theta(t_i \mid t_{<i})$$

The model is trained to minimise the **cross-entropy loss** between predicted and true next-token distributions.

### 2.6 Scaling Laws (Kaplan et al., 2020)

Performance improves as a **power-law** with model size $N$, dataset size $D$, and compute $C$:

$$L(N) \approx \left(\frac{N_c}{N}\right)^{\alpha_N}, \quad \alpha_N \approx 0.076$$

where $L$ is the loss and $N_c$ is a constant. This justifies the brute-force scaling of GPT-3.

### 2.7 RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) — Used in GPT-4

The post-training pipeline has three stages:

1. **Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT):** Fine-tune the LLM on human-written demonstrations.
2. **Reward Model Training:** Train a separate model $R_\phi$ to score outputs based on human preference rankings.
3. **PPO Optimisation:** Fine-tune the LLM using **Proximal Policy Optimisation** to maximise expected reward while staying close to the SFT model (KL penalty).

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Definition | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Scaled Dot-Product Attention | $\text{Attn}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}(QK^{\top}\!/\sqrt{d_k})\,V$ | $d_k$ is key dim |
| 2 | Causal Mask Addition | $\text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} + M\right) V$, $M_{ij}=-\infty$ for $j>i$ | Ensures autoregression |
| 3 | Multi-Head Concat | $\text{MultiHead} = \text{Concat}(h_1,\ldots,h_h)W^O$ | $h$ = num heads |
| 4 | Sinusoidal PE (even) | $PE_{(pos,2i)} = \sin(pos / 10000^{2i/d})$ | Used in original Transformer |
| 5 | Sinusoidal PE (odd) | $PE_{(pos,2i+1)} = \cos(pos / 10000^{2i/d})$ | Same row, odd index |
| 6 | LM Loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_i \log P_\theta(t_i \mid t_{<i})$ | Cross-entropy |
| 7 | Scaling Law | $L(N) \approx (N_c/N)^{\alpha_N}$ | Power law in params |
| 8 | Softmax | $\text{softmax}(z_i) = e^{z_i} / \sum_j e^{z_j}$ | Converts logits to probs |
| 9 | Layer Norm | $\text{LN}(x) = \gamma \cdot \frac{x-\mu}{\sigma} + \beta$ | Stabilises training |
| 10 | RLHF Objective | $\max_\pi \, \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \pi}[R(x)] - \beta \, \text{KL}(\pi \,\vert\, \pi_{\text{SFT}})$ | PPO with KL penalty |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Scaled Dot-Product Attention

**Starting point:** We want a weighted sum of value vectors, where weights reflect pairwise similarity between query $i$ and key $j$.

**Step 1 — Compute raw compatibility scores.**

For each query vector $q_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d_k}$ and key vector $k_j \in \mathbb{R}^{d_k}$, take the inner product:

$$s_{ij} = q_i^{\top} k_j$$

**Step 2 — Stack into matrix form.**

If $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$ stacks all queries and $K \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$ stacks all keys:

$$S = Q K^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$$

Each row $i$ of $S$ contains the scores of token $i$ against every token.

**Step 3 — Apply causal mask (GPT-style).**

For autoregressive decoding, future positions must not influence the present. We add mask $M$:

$$S'_{ij} = s_{ij} + M_{ij}, \quad M_{ij} = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } j \le i \\ -\infty & \text{if } j > i \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 — Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$.**

Without scaling, when $d_k$ is large, the dot products grow in magnitude, pushing the softmax into regions of tiny gradient. We divide:

$$\tilde{S}_{ij} = \frac{S'_{ij}}{\sqrt{d_k}}$$

**Step 5 — Row-wise softmax to obtain attention weights.**

$$A_{ij} = \text{softmax}(\tilde{S}_{ij}) = \frac{\exp(\tilde{S}_{ij})}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \exp(\tilde{S}_{ik})}$$

By construction, the $-\infty$ entries yield $A_{ij} = 0$ for $j > i$, enforcing causality.

**Step 6 — Weighted sum of value vectors.**

$$\text{Output}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} A_{ij} \, v_j \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{Output} = A V$$

Combining all steps into one clean formula:

$$\boxed{\;\text{MaskedAttention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} + M\right) V\;}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — GPT-3 vs GPT-4 Architectural Comparison

| Dimension | GPT-3 | GPT-4 |
|---|---|---|
| **Release Year** | June 2020 | March 2023 |
| **Parameters (active)** | 175 B (dense) | ~280 B per forward pass (estimated, **MoE**) |
| **Total experts** | N/A (dense) | ~**16 experts**, ~**1.76 T** total params |
| **Context Window** | **2,048 tokens** | **8 K / 32 K / 128 K** (variant-dependent) |
| **Modalities** | Text-only | **Multimodal** — text + image input |
| **Attention Type** | Dense self-attention | Likely grouped-query or sparse attention |
| **Training Compute** | ~3.14 × 10²³ FLOPs | Estimated 2 × 10²⁵ FLOPs |
| **Alignment** | None (raw LM) | **RLHF + RBRMS + Constitutional methods** |
| **Hallucination Rate** | Higher | Reduced by ~**40 %** on TruthfulQA benchmarks |
| **Emergent Abilities** | Few-shot ICL, basic reasoning | Chain-of-thought, code reasoning, multimodal reasoning |
| **API Access** | OpenAI API (davinci) | OpenAI API (gpt-4), ChatGPT Plus |

### 3.3 Worked Example — Toy Attention Calculation

Suppose we have a **2-token sequence** with embeddings $x_1 = [1, 0]$, $x_2 = [0, 1]$. Let $W^Q = W^K = W^V = I_2$ (identity for simplicity) and $d_k = 2$.

**Step 1:** $Q = K = V = X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 2:** Compute $QK^{\top} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 3:** Scale by $1/\sqrt{2}$: $\tilde{S} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.707 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 4:** Apply causal mask (already zero above diagonal in this symmetric case, but the upper-right is masked). Masked matrix:

$$S' = \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 & -\infty \\ 0 & 0.707 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5:** Softmax row-wise.

Row 1: $\exp(0.707) / (\exp(0.707) + \exp(-\infty)) = 1$. So $A_1 = [1, 0]$.
Row 2: $\exp(0) / (\exp(0) + \exp(0.707)) = 1 / (1 + 2.028) \approx 0.330$. $\exp(0.707)/(1+2.028)\approx 0.670$. So $A_2 \approx [0.330, 0.670]$.

**Step 6:** $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0.330 & 0.670 \end{bmatrix}$, then $AV = A$ (since $V=I$). Token 2 attends **67 % to itself** and **33 % to token 1**, as expected.

### 3.4 Numerical Estimation — GPT-3 Training Cost

The training compute $C$ is approximated by:

$$C \approx 6 \cdot N \cdot D \quad \text{(FLOPs, rule of thumb from Kaplan et al.)}$$

For GPT-3: $N = 1.75 \times 10^{11}$ params, $D \approx 3 \times 10^{11}$ tokens.

$$C \approx 6 \times 1.75 \times 10^{11} \times 3 \times 10^{11} = 3.15 \times 10^{23} \text{ FLOPs}$$

This matches OpenAI's official figure of ~**3.14 × 10²³ FLOPs**, validating the formula.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 GPT Decoder-Only Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    InputTok[Input Token IDs] --> Embed[Token and Position Embedding]
    Embed --> Block1[Transformer Decoder Block x N]
    Embed --> Block1
    Block1 --> Block2[Transformer Decoder Block x N]
    Block2 --> BlockN[Transformer Decoder Block x N]
    BlockN --> LN[Final Layer Norm]
    LN --> Head[Linear LM Head Softmax]
    Head --> Probs[Next Token Probability Distribution]

    subgraph DecBlk[Single Decoder Block]
        direction TB
        Inp --> LN1[Layer Norm]
        LN1 --> MHA[Masked Multi Head Self Attention]
        MHA --> ResAdd1[Residual Connection]
        Inp2[Input] --> ResAdd1
        ResAdd1 --> LN2[Layer Norm 2]
        LN2 --> FFN[Position wise Feed Forward Network]
        FFN --> ResAdd2[Residual Connection]
        LN2b[LN2 Output] --> ResAdd2
        ResAdd2 --> OutBlk[Block Output]
    end
```

### 4.2 GPT-3 / GPT-4 Training and Alignment Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Web Scale Text Corpus] --> B[Tokenization BPE]
    B --> C[Pre Training Causal LM Objective]
    C --> D[Base LLM GPT 3]
    D --> E[Supervised Fine Tuning SFT]
    E --> F[Reward Model Training on Human Preferences]
    F --> G[PPO Reinforcement Learning]
    G --> H[Aligned LLM GPT 4]
    H --> I[Inference API ChatGPT]
    H --> J[Multimodal Vision Input]
```

### 4.3 Self-Attention Computation Flow (Per Head)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Input Embeddings X] --> WQ[Project to Queries WQ]
    X --> WK[Project to Keys WK]
    X --> WV[Project to Values WV]
    WQ --> MatMul1[MatMul Q and K Transpose]
    WK --> MatMul1
    MatMul1 --> Scale[Scale by 1 over sqrt dk]
    Scale --> Mask[Apply Causal Mask]
    Mask --> Softmax[Row Softmax]
    Softmax --> Weights[Attention Weights Matrix A]
    WV --> MatMul2[MatMul A and V]
    Weights --> MatMul2
    MatMul2 --> Out[Head Output]
```

### 4.4 In-Context Learning Paradigm (Few-Shot Prompting)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Prompt Example 1] --> Concat[Concatenated Context]
    P2[Prompt Example 2] --> Concat
    P3[Prompt Example 3] --> Concat
    Q[New Query] --> Concat
    Concat --> GPT[GPT 3 or GPT 4 No Weight Update]
    GPT --> Ans[Generated Answer]
```

### 4.5 Emergent Abilities vs Scale (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SmallScale[Small Models]
        S1[Below 10B Params]
    end
    subgraph MidScale[Medium Models]
        M1[10B to 100B Params]
    end
    subgraph LargeScale[Large Models]
        L1[100B Plus Params]
    end
    SmallScale --> SmallCap[Limited Few Shot Ability]
    MidScale --> MidCap[Basic ICL and Reasoning]
    LargeScale --> LargeCap[Emergent Abilities, CoT, Code, Multimodal]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define Large Language Models. List any four distinguishing features of GPT-3.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Large Language Model (LLM)** is a Transformer-based neural network trained on massive text corpora using self-supervised learning to model the probability distribution of token sequences. The model learns $P_\theta(t_n \mid t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_{n-1})$ over a vocabulary.

**Four distinguishing features of GPT-3:**

1. **Massive scale — 175 billion parameters**, making it one of the largest dense LMs at release.
2. **In-context learning (few-shot prompting):** Performs new tasks from a few examples in the prompt, with **no gradient updates**.
3. **Decoder-only Transformer architecture** with **96 layers**, **96 attention heads**, and **12,288 hidden dim**.
4. **Generative pre-training only:** No task-specific fine-tuning; all task knowledge emerges from scale and prompt design.

*(1 mark for definition + ½ mark × 4 = 2 marks for features = 3 marks total)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*What is RLHF? Explain its role in aligning GPT-4.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback)** is a post-training alignment technique that uses human preference data to fine-tune a language model via reinforcement learning.

**Three-stage role in GPT-4 alignment:**

1. **Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT):** Human annotators write ideal responses; the LM is fine-tuned on them. *(1 mark)*
2. **Reward Model (RM) Training:** Humans rank multiple LM outputs; a separate model $R_\phi(x, y)$ learns to predict these rankings. *(1 mark)*
3. **PPO Optimisation:** The SFT model is fine-tuned using **Proximal Policy Optimisation** to maximise $R_\phi$ while a KL-divergence term prevents drift from the SFT model. *(1 mark)*

This pipeline reduces harmful, biased, or hallucinated outputs and is a key reason GPT-4 outperforms GPT-3 on safety benchmarks.

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **Transformer decoder architecture** used in GPT-3 with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of **masked multi-head self-attention** and **positional encoding**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **scaling laws** that justify the design choices in GPT-3, and explain **in-context learning (few-shot prompting)** with an example. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**1. Overall Architecture (2 marks):**
GPT-3 is a **decoder-only Transformer** with 96 stacked layers, each containing two sub-layers: (i) **Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention (MMHA)** and (ii) **Position-wise Feed-Forward Network (FFN)**. Each sub-layer is wrapped with a **residual connection** and **Layer Normalization (pre-norm formulation)**. The model has 96 attention heads per layer, hidden dimension 12,288, and context window 2,048 tokens.

**2. Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention (3 marks):**
The mechanism computes attention as:
$$\text{Attn}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} + M\right) V$$
The **causal mask** $M$ sets all entries above the main diagonal to $-\infty$, ensuring that token $i$ attends only to tokens at positions $1, 2, \ldots, i$. Multiple heads run in parallel, each learning different linguistic relationships (syntactic, semantic, coreference). Outputs are concatenated and projected via $W^O$.

**3. Positional Encoding (2 marks):**
Since self-attention is permutation-equivariant, **positional information** must be injected externally. GPT-3 uses **learned absolute positional embeddings** of length 2,048. The original Transformer formulation uses sinusoidal embeddings:
$$PE_{(pos, 2i)} = \sin(pos / 10000^{2i/d})$$
$$PE_{(pos, 2i+1)} = \cos(pos / 10000^{2i/d})$$

These are added to the token embeddings before the first layer.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**1. Scaling Laws (3 marks):**
Kaplan et al. (2020) empirically showed that LM loss follows a **power-law** with model size $N$, dataset size $D$, and compute $C$:
$$L(N) \approx \left(\frac{N_c}{N}\right)^{\alpha_N}, \quad \alpha_N \approx 0.076$$
This means **loss decreases smoothly and predictably** as we scale parameters, tokens, or compute. The training cost for GPT-3 is approximately $C \approx 6ND \approx 3.14 \times 10^{23}$ FLOPs. These laws justified the brute-force scaling of GPT-3 to 175 B parameters and ~300 B training tokens.

**2. In-Context Learning (ICL) (3 marks):**
ICL is the ability to perform a new task by conditioning on **examples placed inside the prompt**, without any weight updates. The model treats the prompt as a temporary "context window" and infers the task pattern.

**Example:** Translate English to French via 2-shot prompting —
```
English: cat → French: chat
English: dog → French: chien
English: bird → French: ? 
```
The model outputs `oiseau` despite **never being explicitly trained on this few-shot format**. This capability emerges reliably only beyond ~10 B parameters in the GPT-3 family.

**3. Conclusion (1 mark):**
Scaling laws + ICL together mark a paradigm shift: instead of fine-tuning task-specific models, a single large model can adapt to thousands of tasks purely via prompt engineering.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Alternative]**

**(a)** Compare **GPT-3 and GPT-4** in terms of architecture, parameters, modalities, and alignment techniques. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** List and explain **any five real-world applications** of GPT-3 / GPT-4 in industry. Discuss one ethical concern. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

| Aspect | GPT-3 | GPT-4 |
|---|---|---|
| **Release** | June 2020 | March 2023 |
| **Parameters** | 175 B (dense) | ~1.76 T total (Mixture-of-Experts), ~280 B active |
| **Context Window** | 2,048 tokens | 8 K / 32 K / 128 K variants |
| **Modalities** | Text only | **Text + Image** (vision encoder) |
| **Architecture** | 96-layer decoder Transformer, dense FFN | Decoder Transformer with **MoE FFN layers** (rumored 16 experts, 2 active per token) |
| **Attention** | Dense multi-head (96 heads) | Likely grouped-query attention (GQA) for efficiency |
| **Alignment** | None post-training | **RLHF + Rule-Based Reward Models (RBRMS)** + adversarial testing |
| **Performance** | Strong few-shot ICL | Strong chain-of-thought reasoning, 90th-percentile bar exam scores |

**Key architectural shift:** GPT-4 moves from a **dense** model to a **sparse Mixture-of-Experts** design, where each token is routed to a subset of "expert" FFN sub-networks. This decouples total parameters from per-token compute, enabling trillion-parameter scale without proportional inference cost. A learned **router network** selects the top-$k$ experts per token. *(1 mark for MoE explanation)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Five real-world applications (1.2 marks each = 6 marks):**

1. **Code Generation & Software Engineering** — GitHub Copilot, ChatGPT Code Interpreter; auto-completes functions, generates unit tests, refactors legacy code.
2. **Conversational Agents & Customer Support** — Klarna, Duolingo Max; reduces support costs by up to 30 % while handling multilingual queries.
3. **Content Creation & Marketing** — Jasper, Copy.ai; generates SEO-optimised blog posts, ad copy, email campaigns at scale.
4. **Education & Tutoring** — Khanmigo (Khan Academy); provides Socratic tutoring without giving away answers, adapting to student level.
5. **Healthcare Decision Support** — Drafting clinical notes, summarising patient history, suggesting differential diagnoses (with human-in-the-loop).

**One ethical concern (1 mark):**
**Hallucination & misinformation** — LLMs confidently generate factually false statements (e.g., fabricated legal citations in *Mata v. Avianca*, 2023). This is dangerous in high-stakes domains (law, medicine) and undermines trust. Mitigations include retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), source-grounded prompting, and human verification.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse encoder-only (BERT) with decoder-only (GPT).** GPT uses **causal/masked** attention; BERT uses **bidirectional** attention. Wrong → 0 marks for architecture question.
> 2. **Do not write `|x|` for absolute value inside markdown tables** — it breaks table syntax. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\lvert x \rvert` in LaTeX.
> 3. **In attention formulas, the scaling factor is $\sqrt{d_k}$**, NOT $d_k$ or $1/d_k$. Students frequently lose 1 mark here.
> 4. **Always mention the causal mask** when discussing GPT attention — it is the defining difference from the original Vaswani et al. Transformer.
> 5. **In scaling law derivations, cite the three quantities** (parameters $N$, dataset $D$, compute $C$) and the power-law form $L \propto N^{-\alpha}$. Vague answers get partial credit only.
> 6. **For GPT-4, do NOT state a confirmed parameter count as fact** — it is officially undisclosed; use "estimated" or "rumored MoE with ~1.76 T total params". Hallucinating a number will cost marks.
> 7. **In RLHF questions, all three stages (SFT → RM → PPO) must be named** — skipping the Reward Model step is a frequent mistake.

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **LLM** = Transformer-based model trained at web scale on next-token prediction objective.
- **GPT** = Generative Pre-trained Transformer; **decoder-only**, **causal (left-to-right)** language model.
- **GPT-3** = **175 B parameters**, **96 layers**, **2,048-token** context, **dense** architecture; introduced **in-context learning (few-shot prompting)**.
- **GPT-4** = **Multimodal** (text + image), **~1.76 T total params** in a **Mixture-of-Experts** (MoE) configuration, **8 K–128 K context**, aligned via **RLHF + RBRMS**.
- **Scaled Dot-Product Attention:** $\text{softmax}\!\left(QK^{\top}\!/\sqrt{d_k}\right) V$ — scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$ prevents softmax saturation.
- **Causal mask** $M$ with $-\infty$ above diagonal enforces autoregressive generation.
- **Positional Encoding** (sinusoidal in original, learned in GPT-3) injects order information.
- **Training objective:** Causal cross-entropy — maximise $\sum \log P_\theta(t_i \mid t_{<i})$.
- **Scaling Laws (Kaplan 2020):** Loss follows power law $\propto N^{-\alpha}$; justifies brute-force scale-up.
- **Emergent abilities** (in-context learning, chain-of-thought) appear non-linearly past critical model size.
- **RLHF stages:** SFT → Reward Model → PPO with KL penalty.
- **MoE (GPT-4):** Sparse expert routing decouples total params from per-token compute.
- **Applications:** Code gen, chatbots, content creation, education, healthcare, legal drafting, scientific summarisation.
- **Key ethical issues:** Hallucination, bias, copyright, job displacement, energy cost, misuse for disinformation.
- **Practical takeaway for KTU exam:** Always draw the block diagram, always state causal masking, always quote parameter counts in **bold**, always distinguish GPT-3 (dense, text-only) from GPT-4 (MoE, multimodal, RLHF-aligned).
